name: States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)
category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Chemistry > States of Matter - Gases and Liquids (FA ONLY)

topics.name: Behaviour of Real Gases: Deviation From Ideal Gas Behaviour

questions.question: Which are three important states of matter?
questions.question_raw: Which are three important states of matter?
questions.explanation:
Three phases of matter are:

  1. Solid - firm and stable in shape; not liquid or fluid, strongest intermolecular forces.
  2. Liquid - A liquid is a nearly incompressible fluid that conforms to the shape of its container but retains a (nearly) constant volume independent of pressure. Intermediate molecular forces. 
  3. Gas -Takes the volume of the container, weakest intermolecular forces.

questions.question: Physical behavior of the states differs greatly even though chemical behavior of the three states is identical because chemical properties depends upon?
questions.question_raw: Physical behavior of the states differs greatly even though chemical behavior of the three states is identical because chemical properties depends upon?

options.text: covalent bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on energy supplied.
non bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular bonding forces.
intramolecular bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular Non-bonding forces.
intermolecular non bonding forces whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular bonding forces.

questions.explanation: The question is based on the understanding of how the forces operate in a molecule. It is known that chemical properties depend on intramolecular bonding forces (holding the atoms in a molecule together), whereas physical properties depend on intermolecular non-bonding force (such as different kinds of polar interactions holding the molecules together).

questions.question: The Dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have:
questions.question_raw: The Dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have:

options.text: an electron cloud that causes temporary dipoles.

questions.explanation: The dipole-dipole forces act between molecules that have a permanent electrical dipole moment, the partial charge of one molecule get attracted to the opposite charge of another molecule.

questions.question: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the:
questions.question_raw: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the:
questions.explanation: The intermolecular force primarily responsible for the condensed states of nonpolar substances is the dispersion force (or London force).

questions.question: The average kinetic energy and thermal energy are proportional to:
questions.question_raw: The average kinetic energy and thermal energy are proportional to:
questions.explanation:
The average KE and Thermal energy are proportional to absolute temperature.
The kinetic energy is the amount of motion in the atoms and molecules of a substance. The molecules of the gas gain energy as the temperature increases as there is more motion of the molecules. As the temperature decreases the motion lessens until at absolute zero all motion (kinetic energy) stops.

questions.question: Three states of matter are the result of?
questions.question_raw: Three states of matter are the result of?

options.text: The balance between intermolecular forces and the thermal energy of the molecules.
The balance between intermolecular forces and the intramolecular binding energy of the molecules.
The balance between Hydrogen bonding forces and the London forces.
The balance between Van Der Waals forces and the London forces.

questions.explanation: Three states of matter are the result of a balance between intermolecular forces and the thermal energy of the molecules. These two factors differentiate them from each other. For understanding, the strongest intermolecular forces are in solid than in liquid and weakest in gases.

questions.question: Gases have a much lower density than the solids and liquids because:
questions.question_raw: Gases have a much lower density than the solids and liquids because:

options.text: Gases contain lower specific enthalpy than solids.
Gases do not have intermolecular forces that attract each molecule to another.
Distance between the molecules is small compared to solids.
Thermal energy overcomes on the attractive intermolecular forces.

questions.explanation: In gases, molecules are far apart from each other, thermal energy which is a measure of freeness of motion is thus very high resulting in a very large volume for the same mass (compared to solid and liquid). Hence there density is the lowest.

questions.question: At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed amount (i.e., number of moles n) of gas is:
questions.question_raw: At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed amount (i.e., number of moles n) of gas is:

options.text: Varies as square of its volume.

questions.explanation:
From ideal gas equation PV = nRT,
keeping n and T fixed,
P {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{V}{/tex}. (Boyles Law)
Here, T = Temperature
P = Pressure
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = sign of Proportionality
V  = Volume
n = number of moles of gas
R = Universal gas constant

questions.question: Charles’ law states that pressure remaining constant, the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is?
questions.question_raw: Charles’ law states that pressure remaining constant, the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is?

options.text: Directly proportional to its absolute temperature.
Directly proportional to square of absolute temperature.
Inversely proportional to its absolute temperature.
Constant irrespective of its absolute temperature.

questions.explanation:
According to Charles law,  At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of a gas increases or decreases by {tex}\frac{1}{273}{/tex} of its volume at 0oC for each one-degree rise or fall in temperature. Therefore at constant pressure, volume of given mass of gas is directly proportional to absolute temperature.

Mathematically; 
{tex}V\,=kT{/tex} (at contant P)

questions.question: Gay Lussac’s law states that at constant volume, the pressure of a fixed amount of gas:
questions.question_raw: Gay Lussac’s law states that at constant volume, the pressure of a fixed amount of gas:

options.text: Directly proportional to the square of absolute temperature.
Constant irrespective of its absolute temperature.

questions.explanation:
The question is based on the statement of Gay Lussac’s law- “At constant temperature, the pressure of a given mass of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.”
Mathematically; 
{tex}P\alpha T{/tex} (at constant temperature)
or {tex}\frac{P}{T}{/tex} = constant

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct ideal gas law?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct ideal gas law?
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT is the correct Ideal Gas equation.
Here, P: Pressure
V: Volume
n: Number of  Moles
R: Universal Gas Constant
T: Temperature

questions.question: CO2 can be easily liquified and even solidified because
questions.question_raw: CO2 can be easily liquified and even solidified because

options.text: It has weak forces of attraction
It has comparatively more force of attraction than other gases

questions.explanation: It has comparatively more force of attraction than other gases

questions.question: Dalton’s Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure exerted by the mixture of non-reactive gases is equal to the:
questions.question_raw: Dalton’s Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure exerted by the mixture of non-reactive gases is equal to the:

options.text: the difference in the partial pressures of individual gases.
average of the partial pressures of individual gases.
maximum of the partial pressures of individual gases.
sum of the partial pressures of individual gases.

questions.explanation: The Dalton Law of Partial pressures for Gases states that total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressure of individual gases.

questions.question: In the kinetic molecular theory attraction between molecules of the gas is:
questions.question_raw: In the kinetic molecular theory attraction between molecules of the gas is:

options.text: depends on the speed of the molecule

questions.explanation: The Kinetic Theory of Gases assumes that no intermolecular forces exert between the gas molecules.

questions.question: Real gases show deviations from ideal gas law because
questions.question_raw: Real gases show deviations from ideal gas law because

options.text: size of molecules cannot be measured.

questions.explanation: Real gases have intermolecular forces of interaction due to which there is a difference in the actual pressure values when compared to the ideal gases, the pressure is found to be less than the pressure of ideal gases under similar conditions, similar deviation occurs when the size of molecules is taken into account due to which volume correction is required.

questions.question: Which is the correct form of van der Waals equation?
questions.question_raw: Which is the correct form of van der Waals equation?
questions.explanation: {tex}\left( {p + \frac{{a{n^2}}}{{{V^2}}}} \right)\left( {V - nb} \right) = nRT{/tex} is van der Waals gas equation of real gases for 'n' moles of gas.

questions.question: The deviation from ideal behavior can be measured in terms of compressibility factor Z, which is
questions.question_raw: The deviation from ideal behavior can be measured in terms of compressibility factor Z, which is
questions.explanation: Z = {tex} \frac{{pV}}{{nRT}}{/tex} is a correction factor which describes the deviation of real gas from ideal gas behaviour. It is defined as the ratio of the molar volume of a gas to the molar volume of an ideal gas at the same temperature and pressure.

questions.question: The behaviour of the gas becomes more ideal when:
questions.question_raw: The behaviour of the gas becomes more ideal when:

options.text: Number of moles is very low

questions.explanation: This question is based on understanding the concept of molecular interactions between a molecule of real gases which is least at low P and High T conditions. Thus under these conditions real gases behave as ideal.

questions.question: Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide is the highest temperature at which liquid carbon dioxide is observed. Above this temperature it is ________.
questions.question_raw: Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide is the highest temperature at which liquid carbon dioxide is observed. Above this temperature it is ________.
questions.explanation: Tc is the highest temperature where gas can exist in a liquid state. Above this, no liquid state exists. It is impossible to compress the gas into liquid above the critical temperature, no matter whatever amount of pressure is applied.

questions.question: Above the Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide, what happens?
questions.question_raw: Above the Critical temperature (TC) of carbon dioxide, what happens?

options.text: Liquefaction of the gas is possible.
Solidification of the gas is possible.
Liquefaction of the gas is not possible.

questions.explanation: Tc is the highest temperature where gas can exist in a liquid state. Above this, no liquid state exists hence it is not possible to liquefy the gas no matter what pressure is applied.

questions.question: Surface tension decreases as the temperature is raised because increase in temperature
questions.question_raw: Surface tension decreases as the temperature is raised because increase in temperature

options.text: decreases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction decreases.
increases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction decreases.
increases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction increases.
decreases the kinetic energy of the molecules and the effectiveness of intermolecular attraction is not changed.

questions.explanation: Surface tension is inversely proportional to the temperature. So surface tension decreases with rising temperature. With the rise in temperature liquids expands, the interparticle distance increases, and the kinetic energy of molecules and hence disorder decreases, so intermolecular forces decrease results in a decrease in surface tension.

questions.question: Viscous force is directly proportional to
questions.question_raw: Viscous force is directly proportional to

options.text: area and inversely proportional to velocity gradient.
velocity gradient and inversely proportional to area.
velocity gradient squared and inversely proportional to area.

questions.explanation:
Viscous force is directly proportional to area and velocity gradient.
We have,
{tex}{F_\upsilon } = \eta A(dV/dH){/tex}
where A = Area
We have, dV/dH = velocity gradient
{tex}\eta = Coefficient\;of\;viscocity{/tex}

questions.question: What will be the minimum pressure required to compress 500 dm3 of air at 1 bar to 200 dm3 at 30°C?
questions.question_raw: What will be the minimum pressure required to compress 500 dm3 of air at 1 bar to 200 dm3 at 30°C?
questions.explanation:
Using the ideal gas equation, PV = nRT,
Since T is constant, we have
P1V1 = P2V2 i.e.
500 dm3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 bar = 200 dm3 {tex}\times{/tex} P2
Which gives P2 = 2.5 bar

questions.question: A vessel of 120 mL capacity contains a certain amount of gas at 35°C and 1.2 bar pressure. The gas is transferred to another vessel of volume 180 mL at 35°C. What would be its pressure?
questions.question_raw: A vessel of 120 mL capacity contains a certain amount of gas at 35°C and 1.2 bar pressure. The gas is transferred to another vessel of volume 180 mL at 35°C. What would be its pressure?
questions.explanation:
Case I PV = nRT; n = {tex} \frac{{PV}}{{RT}}{/tex}
Here P = 1.2bar; V = 120ml and T = 308K.
Here n = {tex} \;\frac{{1.2 \times 120}}{{R \times (308)}} = \frac{{0.46}}{R}{/tex}
CaseII PV = nRT; P = {tex}\frac{{nRT}}{V}{/tex}
here V = 180ml T = 308K and P = {tex} \frac{{0.46\; \times \;R\; \times \;308}}{{R \times \;180\;}}{/tex} = 0.8bar

questions.question: At 0°C, the density of a certain oxide of a gas at 2 bar is the same as that of dinitrogen at 5 bar. What is the molecular mass of the oxide?
questions.question_raw: At 0°C, the density of a certain oxide of a gas at 2 bar is the same as that of dinitrogen at 5 bar. What is the molecular mass of the oxide?
questions.explanation:
Using relation; PV = ({tex}\frac{m}{M}{/tex})RT
we have
P1M1 = P2M2
P1 = {tex} \frac{{{P_2}{M_2}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{5 \times 28}}{2}{/tex} = 70 g/mol

questions.question: Pressure of 1 g of an ideal gas A at 27°C is found to be 2 bar. When 2 g of another ideal gas B is introduced in the same flask at same temperature the pressure becomes 3 bar. Relationship between their molecular masses is:
questions.question_raw: Pressure of 1 g of an ideal gas A at 27°C is found to be 2 bar. When 2 g of another ideal gas B is introduced in the same flask at same temperature the pressure becomes 3 bar. Relationship between their molecular masses is:
questions.explanation:
using realtion, PV = ({tex}\frac{m}{M}{/tex})RT
P of 1g an ideal gas at 27 °C = 2 bar
P of 2g another ideal gas in same flask = 3 bar
pressure due to B Ptotal - PA= 3 - 2 = 1bar. Now using relation,
{tex}P = \frac{{mRT}}{{MV}}{/tex}
2 = {tex} \frac{{1RT}}{{{M_A}V}}{/tex} .... (1)
1 = {tex} \frac{{2RT}}{{{M_B}V}}{/tex} .... (2)
now, (1)/(2), we get
{tex}\frac{2}{1} = \frac{{{M_B}}}{{2{M_A}}}{/tex}
MB = 4MA

questions.question: The drain cleaner, Drainex contains small bits of aluminum which react with caustic soda to produce dihydrogen. What volume of dihydrogen at 20°C and one bar will be released when 0.15g of aluminum reacts?
questions.question_raw: The drain cleaner, Drainex contains small bits of aluminum which react with caustic soda to produce dihydrogen. What volume of dihydrogen at 20°C and one bar will be released when 0.15g of aluminum reacts?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2Al + 2NaOH + 2{H_2}O}&{ \to 2NaAl{O_2} + 3{H_2}} \\ \begin{gathered} 2 \times 27 \hfill \\ 0.15g \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} 3mol \hfill \\ ? \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}{/tex}

Moles of {tex}Al = \frac{{0.15}}{{27}} = {/tex} 0.0055 moles
2 mole of Al produces 3 moles of H2
so 0.0055 moles of AL will produce {tex}{\frac{3}{2}\;}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} 0.0055 = 0.00825 moles of H2
Now PV = nRT where n = 0.00825, P = 1 bar and T = 293K.
{tex}V_{H_2}\;=\frac{0.00825\;\times0.0821\;\times\;293\;}{1\times0.987}={/tex} 0.2025dm3 = 202.5mL

questions.question: What will be the pressure exerted by a mixture of 3.2 g of methane and 4.4 g of carbon dioxide contained in a 9 dm3 flask at 27°C?
questions.question_raw: What will be the pressure exerted by a mixture of 3.2 g of methane and 4.4 g of carbon dioxide contained in a 9 dm3 flask at 27°C?
questions.explanation:
Acc. to Daltons Law
P = P1 + P2
and by applying PV = nRT
P1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} RT and P2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} RT  where T = 300K
now using P = P1 + P2
P = P1 + P2 = {tex}\frac{{(0.2 + 0.1)}}{9} \times RT = \frac{{(0.2 + 0.1)}}{9} \times \frac{{0.0821 \times 300}}{1} {/tex}= 0.82 atm 
P = 0.82 atm = 0.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 101325 = 83086.5 pa = 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 Pa

questions.question: What will be the pressure of the gaseous mixture when 0.5 L of ${{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$ at 0.8 bar and 2.0 L of dioxygen at 0.7 bar are introduced in a 1L vessel at ${\text{27}}^\circ {\text{C}}$ ?
questions.question_raw: What will be the pressure of the gaseous mixture when 0.5 L of {tex}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} at 0.8 bar and 2.0 L of dioxygen at 0.7 bar are introduced in a 1L vessel at {tex}{\text{27}}^\circ {\text{C}}{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
Here, {tex}{P_{{H_2}}} \times 1 = 0.8 \times 0.5{/tex} =0.40 bar
{tex}{P_{{O_2}}} \times 1 = 0.7 \times 2{/tex} =1.4 bar
And by applying {tex}P=\;P_{H_{2\;}}+P_{O_2}{/tex}= 0.40 +1.40 =1.80 bar

questions.question: The density of a gas is found to be 5.46 g/dm3 at 27°C at 2 bar pressure. What will be its density at STP?
questions.question_raw: The density of a gas is found to be 5.46 g/dm3 at 27°C at 2 bar pressure. What will be its density at STP?
questions.explanation:
For the same gas at different temperature and presuure; ratio of densities is given by relation {tex}\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}=\frac{P_1T_2}{P_2T_1}{/tex}
{tex}{\rho _1} = ?,{\rho _2} = 5.46,{/tex} We have, P1 = 1 bar, P2 = 2bar, T2 = 300K, T1 = 273K
Putting values,{tex}\rho_1=\frac{P_1\;T_2\;\rho_2}{P_2\;T_1}=\;\frac{1\times300\times5.46}{2\times273}={/tex} 3g/dm3
{tex}\rho_1{/tex} = 3g/dm3

questions.question: 34.05 mL of phosphorus vapour weighs 0.0625 g at 546°C and 0.1 bar pressure. What is the molar mass of phosphorus? (in one decimal value)
questions.question_raw: 34.05 mL of phosphorus vapour weighs 0.0625 g at 546°C and 0.1 bar pressure. What is the molar mass of phosphorus? (in one decimal value)
questions.explanation:
PV = {tex} \frac{{mRT}}{M}{/tex}
M = {tex} \frac{{mRT}}{{PV}}{/tex}
M = {tex}\frac{{0.0625g \times 0.083bar\;d{m^3}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 819K}}{{0.1bar \times 34.05 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}d{m^3}}}{/tex}
The molar mass of phosphorus  = 124.75g/mol

questions.question: A student forgot to add the reaction mixture to the round-bottomed flask at 27°C, but instead, he/she placed the flask on the flame. After a lapse of time, he realized his mistake and using a pyrometer he found the temperature of the flask was 477 °C. What fraction of air would have been expelled out?
questions.question_raw: A student forgot to add the reaction mixture to the round-bottomed flask at 27°C, but instead, he/she placed the flask on the flame. After a lapse of time, he realized his mistake and using a pyrometer he found the temperature of the flask was 477 °C. What fraction of air would have been expelled out?
questions.explanation:
Using ideal gas equation PV = nRT, at constant pressure and volume(here volume of the container remains constant as it is open to atmosphere)
n1T1 = n2T2
{tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}} = \frac{{300}}{{750}} = 0.4{/tex}
This is the fraction of air remaining in a flask, so a fraction of air expelled out = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6

questions.question: Calculate the temperature of 4.0 mol of a gas occupying 5 dm3 at 3.32 bar. (R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1).
questions.question_raw: Calculate the temperature of 4.0 mol of a gas occupying 5 dm3 at 3.32 bar. (R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1).
questions.explanation:
We have PV = nRT
or {tex}{T = \;\frac{{PV}}{{nR}} = \;\frac{{\;3.22bar\; \times 5d{m^3}}}{{4mol\; \times 0.083bar\;d{m^3}{K^{ - 1}}\;mo{l^{ - 1}}}}}{/tex} = 50K

questions.question: Calculate the total number of electrons present in 1.4 g of dinitrogen gas.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the total number of electrons present in 1.4 g of dinitrogen gas.
questions.explanation:
moles of {tex}{{N_2} = \frac{{1.4}}{{28}}\; = \;}{/tex} 0.05 mol
And 1 mole of N2 = 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 molecules of N2
And 1 molecule of N2 has 14 electrons
total number of electrons in 1.4 g of N2 = 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 {tex}\times{/tex} 14 = 4.2154 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023

questions.question: Bose-Einstein Condensate have
questions.question_raw: Bose-Einstein Condensate have
questions.explanation: Very low kinetic energy

questions.question: The colour of vapours formed on sublimation of iodine solid is
questions.question_raw: The colour of vapours formed on sublimation of iodine solid is
questions.explanation: Purple (violet)

questions.question: The payload is defined as the difference between the mass of displaced air and the mass of the balloon. Calculate the payload when a balloon of radius 10 m, mass 100 kg is filled with helium at 1.66 bar at 27°C. (Density of air = 1.2 kg m-3 and R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The payload is defined as the difference between the mass of displaced air and the mass of the balloon. Calculate the payload when a balloon of radius 10 m, mass 100 kg is filled with helium at 1.66 bar at 27°C. (Density of air = 1.2 kg m-3 and R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1)
questions.explanation:
Volume of balloon, V = {tex} \frac{4}{3} \times (3.14) \times {r^3} {/tex} = 4187 m3
Mass of displaced air, m = V {tex}\times {/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = 4187 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.2 = 5024.4kg
Temperature, T = 300 K
Mass of He filled in balloon, mHe = {tex} \frac{{PVM}}{{RT}}\; = \;\frac{{1.66 \times 4187\; \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.083 \times 300}} {/tex} = 1116.4 kg
Total Mass of filled balloon, m' = 100 + 1116.4 = 1216.4 kg
Payload = mass of displaced air - mass of balloon  = 5024.04 - 1216.44 = 3807.6 kg

questions.question: Calculate the volume occupied by 8.8 g of CO2 at 31.1°C and 1 bar pressure. R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the volume occupied by 8.8 g of CO2 at 31.1°C and 1 bar pressure. R = 0.083 bar dm3K-1mol-1.
questions.explanation:
{tex}{V\; = \frac{{wRT}}{{MP}}}{/tex}
{tex}{V = \;\frac{{8.8\; \times 0.083\; \times 304.1}}{{44 \times 1}} = 5.05L}{/tex}

questions.question: 2.9 g of a gas at 95°C occupied the same volume as 0.184 g of dihydrogen at 17°C, at the same pressure. What is the molar mass of the gas?
questions.question_raw: 2.9 g of a gas at 95°C occupied the same volume as 0.184 g of dihydrogen at 17°C, at the same pressure. What is the molar mass of the gas?
questions.explanation:
We know that, {tex}{PV = \frac{{mRT}}{M}}{/tex}
For unknown gas, P1V1 = {tex}{\frac{{{m_1}R{T_1}}}{{{M_1}}}}{/tex}
For H2 gas, P2V2{tex}{ = \frac{{{m_2}R{T_2}}}{{{M_2}}}}{/tex}
Here, P1V1 = P2V2
Hence, {tex}{\frac{{{m_1}R{T_1}}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{{m_2}R{T_2}}}{{{M_2}}}\;}{/tex}
{tex}{ \Rightarrow \frac{{2.9\;g \times R \times 368K}}{{{M_1}}} = \frac{{0.184g\; \times R \times 290K}}{{2\;g/mol}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Molar mass of unknown gas, M1 = 40 g/mol

questions.question: A mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen at one bar pressure contains 20% by weight of dihydrogen. Calculate the partial pressure of dihydrogen.
questions.question_raw: A mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen at one bar pressure contains 20% by weight of dihydrogen. Calculate the partial pressure of dihydrogen.
questions.explanation:
A mixture of H2 and O2 contains 20% by weight of H2 means H2 = 20g and O2 = 80g
moles of hydrogen, {tex}n_{H_2}\;=\frac{20}2{/tex} = 10 mol
moles of oxygen, {tex}n_{O_2} = \frac{80}{32}{/tex} = 2.5 mol
mole fraction of hydrogen,{tex}\begin{array}{l}{x_H}_{{}_2\;}=\frac{n_{H_2}\;}{n_{H_2}+n_{O_2}}=\frac{10}{10+2.5}=\;0.8\\\end{array}{/tex}
partial pressure of H2, {tex}P_{H_2}=P_{Total}\times x_{H_2}=1bar\times0.8=0.8\;bar{/tex}

questions.question: The vapor pressure of different substances at the same temperature increases if:
questions.question_raw: The vapor pressure of different substances at the same temperature increases if:

options.text: the molecules of the substance are ionic bonded.
the intermolecular forces of the substance are strong.
the molecules of the substance are covalent bonded.
the intermolecular forces of the substance are weak.

questions.explanation: Vapour pressure of a substance is inversely proportional to the intermolecular forces between molecules.

questions.question: A person living in Shimla observed that cooking food without using pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude:
questions.question_raw: A person living in Shimla observed that cooking food without using pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude:
questions.explanation: As we move to higher altitude, the atmospheric pressure decreases. Therefore, boiling point of water also decreases, hence , cooking of food takes longer time without using pressure cooker.

questions.question: Which of the following property of water can be used to explain the spherical shape of rain droplets?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following property of water can be used to explain the spherical shape of rain droplets?
questions.explanation: Due to surface Tension, the water droplet tends to acquire minimum surface area, hence water droplet attains spherical shape.

questions.question: The interaction energy of London force is inversely proportional to sixth power of the distance between two interacting particles but their magnitude depends upon
questions.question_raw: The interaction energy of London force is inversely proportional to sixth power of the distance between two interacting particles but their magnitude depends upon

options.text: strength of permanent dipoles in the particles

questions.explanation: London dispersion forces operate only over very short distance. The energy of interaction varies as 1/(distance between two interacting particles)6. Large or more complex are the molecules, greater is the magnitude of London forces. This is obviously due to the fact that the large electron clouds are easily distorted or polarised. Hence, greater the polarisability of the interacting particles, greater is the magnitude of the interaction energy.

questions.question: Dipole-dipole forces act between the molecules possessing permanent dipole. Ends of dipoles possess ‘partial charges’. The partial charge is
questions.question_raw: Dipole-dipole forces act between the molecules possessing permanent dipole. Ends of dipoles possess ‘partial charges’. The partial charge is
questions.explanation: Partial charge is a small charge developed by displacement of electrons. It is less than unit electronic charge and is represented as δ+ or δ–

questions.question: The pressure of a 1:4 mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen enclosed in a vessel is one atmosphere. What would be the partial pressure of dioxygen?
questions.question_raw: The pressure of a 1:4 mixture of dihydrogen and dioxygen enclosed in a vessel is one atmosphere. What would be the partial pressure of dioxygen?
questions.explanation:
moles of H2 and O2 are 1 and 4.
moles of {tex}{O_2} = \frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Partial pressure of O2 = mole fraction {tex} \times {/tex} total pressure {tex} = \frac{4}{5} \times 1 = 0.8atm{/tex}
{tex} = 0.8 \times {10^5}N{m^{ - 2}} = 8 \times {10^4}N{m^{ - 2}}{/tex}

questions.question: As the temperature increases, average kinetic energy of molecules increases. What would be the effect of increase of temperature on pressure provided the volume is constant?
questions.question_raw: As the temperature increases, average kinetic energy of molecules increases. What would be the effect of increase of temperature on pressure provided the volume is constant?
questions.explanation: P α T at constant volume.This is according to Gay Lussac’s law-“ At constant volume, the pressure of a given mass of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.”

questions.question: What is SI unit of viscosity coefficient$\left( \eta \right)$?
questions.question_raw: What is SI unit of viscosity coefficient{tex}\left( \eta \right){/tex}?
questions.explanation: Units of coefficient of viscosity, {tex}\eta=\frac{Fd}{AV}\;=\;\frac{Nm}{m^2ms^{-1}}=\;Nsm^{-2}{/tex}

questions.question: Increase in kinetic energy can overcome intermolecular forces of attraction. How will the viscosity of liquid be affected by the increase in temperature?
questions.question_raw: Increase in kinetic energy can overcome intermolecular forces of attraction. How will the viscosity of liquid be affected by the increase in temperature?

options.text: No regular pattern will be followed

questions.explanation: viscosity is inversely proportional to the temperature. As the temperature increase, viscosity decreases.

questions.question: How does the surface tension of a liquid vary with increase in temperature?
questions.question_raw: How does the surface tension of a liquid vary with increase in temperature?
questions.explanation: As temperature increases surface tension decreases beacuse the cohesive forces decreases with increase of molecular thermal activity.

questions.question: Which of the following figures does not represent 1 mole of dioxygen gas at STP?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following figures does not represent 1 mole of dioxygen gas at STP?
questions.explanation: 1 mole of O2 at STP has mass 32g and volume 22.7L. It contains 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of dioxygen.

questions.question: Under which of the following two conditions, a gas deviates most from the ideal behaviour?
questions.question_raw: Under which of the following two conditions, a gas deviates most from the ideal behaviour?
questions.explanation:
A gas which obeys the ideal gas equation, pV = nRT under all conditions of temperature and pressure is called an ‘ideal gas’.
However, there is no gas that obeys the ideal gas equation under all conditions of temperature and pressure. Hence, the concept of an ideal gas is only theoretical or hypothetical. The gases are found to obey the gas laws fairly well when the pressure is low or the temperature is high.
Such gases are, therefore, known as ‘real gases’. All gases are real gases. Hence, at high pressure and low temperature, a real gas deviates most from ideal behaviour.

questions.question: Which of the following changes decrease the vapour pressure of water kept in a sealed vessel?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following changes decrease the vapour pressure of water kept in a sealed vessel?

options.text: Decreasing the volume of the vessel to one-half.

questions.explanation: Adding a non-volatile solute like salt decreases the vapour pressure of water. This is because some of the solute(salt) particles occupy the position of solvent molecules (water) on the liquid surface. So escaping the tendency of molecules (water) is reduced and hence vapour pressure decreases.

questions.question: Heat of vaporization is always larger than the Heat of fusion because it takes:
questions.question_raw: Heat of vaporization is always larger than the Heat of fusion because it takes:

options.text: less energy to separate particles completely than just to free them from their fixed positions in the solid.
more energy to separate particles completely than just to free them from their random positions in the solid.
more energy to separate particles completely than just to free them from their fixed positions in the solid.
no energy to separate particles completely than just to free them from their oscillating positions in the solid.

questions.explanation: While melting (fusion) of a substance the molecules get separated to some extent but when the same substance is vaporised the molecules get separated to a greater extent which requires more energy, i.e. more heat.

questions.question: The fraction of molecules with enough energy to escape the liquid
questions.question_raw: The fraction of molecules with enough energy to escape the liquid

options.text: is lesser at the higher temperature
is greater at the lower temperature.
is greater at the higher temperature.

questions.explanation: The fraction of molecules ({tex}\frac{\triangle N}N{/tex})with enough energy to escape the liquid is greater at the higher temperature.

questions.question: The ease with which the electron cloud of a particle can be distorted is called _______.
questions.question_raw: The ease with which the electron cloud of a particle can be distorted is called _______.
questions.explanation: The polarizability in isotropic media is defined as the ratio of the induced dipole moment of an atom to the electric field that produces this dipole moment.

questions.question: Molecules in the interior of liquid experience intermolecular
questions.question_raw: Molecules in the interior of liquid experience intermolecular
questions.explanation: The net force on the molecules presents inside in bulk is zero. For example, the molecule 'p' will experience zero.

questions.question: Surface tension results because
questions.question_raw: Surface tension results because

options.text: a liquid tends to minimize the number of molecules at the surface.
a liquid tends to maximize the number of molecules at the surface.
a liquid tends to increase the number of molecules at the surface.

questions.explanation: Surface tension results because a liquid tends to minimize the number of molecules at the surface (i.e to minimize the surface area).

questions.question: Capillarity results from a competition between the intermolecular forces within the
questions.question_raw: Capillarity results from a competition between the intermolecular forces within the

options.text: liquid and intramolecular forces between walls and liquid.
liquid (cohesive forces) and those between the liquid and the tube walls (adhesive forces).
intermolecular forces within tube walls and liquid (adhesive forces) only.
liquid and intramolecular forces between liquid (cohesive forces).

questions.explanation: Capillarity action results because of intermolecular forces within the liquid (cohesive forces) and those between the liquid and the tube walls (adhesive forces). Generally, capillary action occurs when the adhesion to the walls is stronger than the cohesive forces between the liquid molecules.

questions.question: The van der Waals equation adjusts the measured pressure
questions.question_raw: The van der Waals equation adjusts the measured pressure

options.text: up by subtracting a factor that accounts for intermolecular attractions.
up by adding a factor that accounts for intramolecular attractions.
up by adding a factor that accounts for intermolecular attractions.
down by adding a factor that accounts for intermolecular attractions.

questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{an^2}{V^2}{/tex} is added. The value of constant 'a' gives the magnitude of attractive forces between molecules of the gas.

questions.question: In van der Waals, an equation of state of the gas law, the constant b is a measure of:
questions.question_raw: In van der Waals, an equation of state of the gas law, the constant b is a measure of:
questions.explanation:
In van der Waals equation of state of the gas law, the constant b is a measure of the volume occupied by the molecules.
It gives the effective size of the gas molecules. The greater value of b indicates a larger size of the molecules and smaller compressible volume.

questions.question: Acetylene (C2H2), an important fuel in welding, is produced in the laboratory when calcium carbide (CaC2) reacts with water:${\text{Ca}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}0\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ Ca}}{\left( {{\text{OH}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right)$ For a sample of acetylene collected over water, total gas pressure (adjusted to barometric pressure) is 738 torr and the volume is 543 mL. At the temperature of the gas (23°C), the vapour pressure of water is 21 torr. How many grams of acetylene are collected?
questions.question_raw: Acetylene (C2H2), an important fuel in welding, is produced in the laboratory when calcium carbide (CaC2) reacts with water:{tex}{\text{Ca}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}0\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ Ca}}{\left( {{\text{OH}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){/tex} For a sample of acetylene collected over water, total gas pressure (adjusted to barometric pressure) is 738 torr and the volume is 543 mL. At the temperature of the gas (23°C), the vapour pressure of water is 21 torr. How many grams of acetylene are collected?
questions.explanation:
The total pressure is the sum of the partial pressure due to C2H2(g) and H2O(g):
Ptotal = 748mm of Hg = pH2O + pC2H2 = (21 mm of Hg) + PC2H2;PC2H2 = (738 - 21) = 717mm of Hg
As 760 mm of Hg corresponds to 1 atm,
{tex}P{c_2}{H_2} = \frac{{717}}{{760}}atm {/tex} = 0.943atm.
Using the ideal gas law, pV = nRT,
The number of moles of C2H2 formed is: {tex}n = \frac{{pV}}{{RT}} = \frac{{(0.943atm) \times (0.543L)}}{{(0.08206\;Latm\;{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}})\left\{ {(23 + 273)K} \right\}}} {/tex} = 0.0207mol
The molar mass of C2H2 is {2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12(C)} + {2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1(H)} = 26g mol-1
This amount, therefore, corresponds to a mass of acetylene collected
mass = number of moles {tex} \times {/tex} molar mass  = 0.0207mol {tex}\times{/tex} 26g mol-1 = 0.540g

questions.question: A scale model of a blimp rises when it is filled with helium to a volume of 55.0 dm3. When 1.10 mol of He is added to the blimp, the volume is 26.2 dm3. How many more grams of He must be added to make it rise? Assume constant T and P.
questions.question_raw: A scale model of a blimp rises when it is filled with helium to a volume of 55.0 dm3. When 1.10 mol of He is added to the blimp, the volume is 26.2 dm3. How many more grams of He must be added to make it rise? Assume constant T and P.
questions.explanation:
By, Avogadro's Law; {tex}\frac{{{V_1}}}{{{V_2}}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{55}}{{26.2}} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{1.10}}{/tex}
So, {tex}{n_1} = \frac{{55 \times 1.10}}{{26.2}}{/tex} = 2.309mol
n1 = 2.309 mol.
So, {tex}\Delta n{/tex} = 2.309 - 1.10 = 1.209 mol of He
Therefore mass of He that should be added = (2.309 - 1.10)mol {tex}\times{/tex} 4.003 g/mol = 4.84g.

questions.question: Hot H2 can reduce copper(II) oxide, forming pure metal and H2O. What volume of H2 at 765°C torr and 225 is needed to reduce 35.5 g of copper(II) oxide?
questions.question_raw: Hot H2 can reduce copper(II) oxide, forming pure metal and H2O. What volume of H2 at 765°C torr and 225 is needed to reduce 35.5 g of copper(II) oxide?
questions.explanation:
CuO + H2  {tex}\to{/tex} Cu + H2O
1 mol of H2 is required to reduce 1 mol of CuO. Mole of CuO = {tex}\frac{35.5}{79.5}{/tex} = 0.45 mol. So, moles of H2 = 0.45mol.
PV = nRT
Here P = 765torr, n = 0.45mol, T = 498K
so V = 18.1L

questions.question: What mass of potassium chloride forms when 5.25 L of chlorine gas at 0.950 atm and 293 K reacts with 17.0 g of potassium?
questions.question_raw: What mass of potassium chloride forms when 5.25 L of chlorine gas at 0.950 atm and 293 K reacts with 17.0 g of potassium?
questions.explanation:
K + 0.5Cl2 {tex}\to{/tex} KCl 
1 mol of K reacts with 0.5 mol of Cl2  gas. Using relation; PV = nRT where P = 0.95 atm, V = 5.25 L and T = 293K
we have, n = 0.20733 mol
So, moles of Cl2 = 0.20733 mol
mole of K = 0.43 mol
Cl2 is limiting reagent. 0.5 mole of Cl2  give 1 mole of KCl.
So, 0.20733 mole will give 0.414 mole of KCl = 30.9 g

questions.question: The average kinetic energy of the gas molecule is:
questions.question_raw: The average kinetic energy of the gas molecule is:

options.text: equal to the square of its absolute temperature.
inversely proportional to its absolute temperature.
directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

questions.explanation: The question is based on understanding the postulates of the kinetic theory of gases, according to which, the average kinetic energy of a gas particle is directly proportional to the absolute temperature. An increase in temperature increases the speed at which the gas molecules move. All gases at a given temperature have the same average kinetic energy.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct mathematical relation for Charles law at constant pressure?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the correct mathematical relation for Charles law at constant pressure?
questions.explanation: Charles Law states that the volume of a fixed mass of a gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature when pressure is kept constant.

questions.question: At constant temperature, the pressure of the gas is reduced to one third, the volume?
questions.question_raw: At constant temperature, the pressure of the gas is reduced to one third, the volume?
questions.explanation:
This question is based on a simple application of Boyle's Law which states that the pressure of a given mass of an ideal gas is inversely proportional to its volume at a constant temperature.
P1V1 = P2 V2
according to the question, P1 = PV1 = V P2 = {tex}\frac{P}{3}{/tex}, V2 =?
{tex}{{V}_{2}}=\frac{{{P}_{1}}{{V}_{1}}}{{{P}_{2}}}=\frac{P\ V}{\frac{P}{3}}=3V{/tex}

questions.question: With the rise in temperature, the surface tension of a liquid:
questions.question_raw: With the rise in temperature, the surface tension of a liquid:
questions.explanation: In general, surface tension decreases when the temperature increases because cohesive forces decrease with an increase in molecular thermal activity. The influence of the surrounding environment is due to the adhesive action liquid molecules have at the interface.

questions.question: Viscosity of a liquid is a measure of
questions.question_raw: Viscosity of a liquid is a measure of

options.text: Repulsive forces between the liquid molecules

questions.explanation: The viscosity of a fluid is the property of a liquid which resists its own flow.

questions.question: The cleansing action of soaps and detergents is due to:
questions.question_raw: The cleansing action of soaps and detergents is due to:
questions.explanation: Adding detergent to water reduces its surface tension. Detergents and soaps have something called surface-active agent, or surfactants for short. These surfactants reduce the surface tension value of water and enhance its ability to stick to things. Thus after adding the detergents, the cleansing action of the water is enhanced as the solution thus formed sticks more easily to the dirt and helps to remove it.

questions.question: The compressibility factor, z for an ideal gas is:
questions.question_raw: The compressibility factor, z for an ideal gas is:
questions.explanation: Compressibility Factor: It is simply defined as the ratio of the molar volume of a gas to the molar volume of an ideal gas at the same temperature and pressure. hence for an ideal gas, the compressibility factor is equal to 1. The Compressibility Factor is a useful thermodynamic property for modifying the ideal gas law to account for real gas behaviour.

questions.question: Which of the following gases will have the lowest rate of diffusion?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following gases will have the lowest rate of diffusion?
questions.explanation: This question is based on the application of Graham's law for rate of diffusion of gases. According to Graham's law, "Diffusion rate of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass (molecular weight)".

vmp of N2 (300 K); vmp of O2(400 K); vmp of H2(300 K)
vmp of O2 (400 K); vmp of N2(300 K); vmp of H2(300 K)

questions.explanation:
{tex}v_{\mathrm{mp}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}}{/tex}, i.e. {tex}v_{\mathrm{mp}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}{/tex}

Gas M T(K) {tex}\sqrt{\boldsymbol{T} / \boldsymbol{M}}{/tex}
H2 2 300 {tex}\sqrt{300 / 2}=\sqrt{150}{/tex} ...III (Highest)
N2 28 300 {tex}\sqrt{300 / 28}=\sqrt{10.71}{/tex} ...I (Lowest)
O2 32 400 {tex}\sqrt{400 / 32}=\sqrt{12.5}{/tex} ...II

So,

questions.question_raw:
Consider the following table.

Gas a/(k Pa dm6 mol-1) b/(dm3 mol-1)
A 642.32 0.05196
B 155.21 0.04136
C 431.91 0.05196
D 155.21 0.4382

a and b are van der Waals’ constants. The correct statement about the gases is

options.text: gas C will occupy more volume than gas A; gas B will be lesser compressible than gas D.
gas C will occupy more volume than gas A; gas B will be more compressible than gas D.
gas C will occupy lesser volume than gas A; gas B will be more compressible than gas D.
gas C will occupy lesser volume than gas A ; gas B will be lesser compressible than gas D.

questions.explanation:
For 1 mole of a real gas, the van der Waals’ equation is
{tex}\left(p+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-b)=R T{/tex}
The constant ‘a’ measures the intermolecular force of attraction of gas molecules and the constant ‘6’ measures the volume correction by gas molecules after a perfectly inelastic binary collision of gas molecules.
For gas A and gas C given value of ‘b’ is 0.05196 dm3mol-1. Here,
a {tex}\propto{/tex} intermolecular force of attraction
{tex}\propto{/tex} compressibility {tex}\propto{/tex} real nature
{tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { volume occupied }}{/tex}
Value of a/(kPa dm6mol-1) for gas A (642.32) > gas C (431.91)
So, gas C will occupy more volume than gas A. Similarly, for a given value of a say 155.21 kPa dm6 mol-1 for gas B and gas D
{tex}\frac{1}{b} \propto{/tex} intermolecular force of attraction
{tex}\propto{/tex} compressibility oc real nature
{tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { volume occupied }}{/tex}
b/(dm3mol-1) for gas B (0.04136) < Gas D (0.4382)
So, gas B will be more compressible than gas D.

questions.question: At a given temperature T, gases Ne, Ar, Xe and Kr are found to deviate from ideal gas behaviour. Their equation of state is given as, p = $\frac{R T}{V-b}$ at T. Here, b is the van der Waals’ constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of Z (compression factor) vs p?
questions.question_raw: At a given temperature T, gases Ne, Ar, Xe and Kr are found to deviate from ideal gas behaviour. Their equation of state is given as, p = {tex}\frac{R T}{V-b}{/tex} at T. Here, b is the van der Waals’ constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of Z (compression factor) vs p?
questions.explanation:
Noble gases such as Ne, Ar, Xe and Kr found to deviate from ideal gas behaviour.
Xe gas will exhibit steepest increase in plot of Z vs p.
Equation of state is given as:
{tex}p=\frac{R T}{(V-b)} \Rightarrow p(V-b)=R T{/tex}
pV - p b - RT {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pV = RT + pb
{tex}\frac{p V}{R T}=1+\frac{p b}{R T}{/tex}
As, {tex}Z=\frac{p V}{R T}{/tex}
So, {tex}Z=1+\frac{p b}{R T} \Rightarrow y=c+m x{/tex}
The plot of z vs p is found to be

The gas with a high value of b will be steepest as the slope is directly proportional to b. b is the van der Waals’ constant and is equal to four times the actual volume of the gas molecules. Xe gas possesses the largest atomic volume among the given noble gases (Ne, Kr, Ar). Hence, it gives the steepest increase in the plot of Z (compression factor) vsp.

questions.question:
Consider the van der Waals’ constants, a and b, for the following gases.
 Gas
 Ar
 Ne
 Kr
 Xe
 a/(atm dm6 mol-2)
 1.3
 0.2
 5.1
 4.1
 b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1)
 3.2
 1.7
 1.0
 5.0
Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
questions.question_raw:
Consider the van der Waals’ constants, a and b, for the following gases.

Gas Ar Ne Kr Xe
a/(atm dm6 mol-2) 1.3 0.2 5.1 4.1
b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1) 3.2 1.7 1.0 5.0

Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
questions.explanation:
Critical temperature is the temperature of a gas above which it cannot be liquefied what ever high the pressure may be. The kinetic energy of gas molecules above this temperature is sufficient enough to overcome the attractive forces. It is represented as Tc.
{tex}T_{c}=\frac{8 a}{27 R b}{/tex}
For Ar, {tex}T_{c}=\frac{8 \times 1.3}{27 \times 8.314 \times 3.2}=0.0144{/tex}
For Ne, {tex}T_{c}=\frac{8 \times 0.2}{27 \times 8.314 \times 17}=0.0041{/tex}
For Kr, {tex}T_{c}=\frac{8 \times 5.1}{27 \times 8.314 \times 1.0}=0.18{/tex}
For Xe, {tex}T_{c}=\frac{8 \times 4.1}{27 \times 8.314 \times 5.0}=0.02{/tex}
The value of Tc is highest for Kr (Krypton).

questions.question: An open vessel at 27°C is heated until two-fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature at which the vessel has been heated is
questions.question_raw: An open vessel at 27°C is heated until two-fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature at which the vessel has been heated is
questions.explanation:
Given, temperature (T1) = 273 + 27 = 300 K
Volume of vessel = constant
Pressure in vessel = constant
the volume of air reduced by {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} so the remaining volume of air is {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}.
Let at T1 the volume of air inside the vessel is n so at T2 the
volume of air will be {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}n.
Now, as p and V are constant, so
{tex}n \cdot T_{1}=\frac{3}{5} n T_{2}{/tex}
Putting the value of T1 in equation (i) we get,
{tex}n \times 300=\frac{3}{5} n \times T_{2}{/tex}
or {tex}T_{2}=300 \times \frac{5}{3}=500 \mathrm{K}{/tex}

questions.question: The volume of gas A is twice that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at the same temperature. The pressures of the gases for an equal number of moles are
questions.question_raw: The volume of gas A is twice that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at the same temperature. The pressures of the gases for an equal number of moles are
questions.explanation:
Given, ZA = 3ZB
Compressibility factor (Z) {tex}=\frac{p V}{n R T}{/tex} [for real gases]
On substituting in equation (i), we get
{tex}\frac{p_{A} V_{A}}{n_{A} R T_{A}}=\frac{3 p_{B} V_{B}}{n_{B} R T_{B}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, it is given that
VA = 2VbB, nA = nB and TA = TB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Eq. (ii) becomes
{tex}\frac{p_{A} \times 2 V_{B}}{n_{B} R T_{B}}=\frac{3 p_{B} V_{B}}{n_{B} R T_{B}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2pA = 3pB

questions.question: A 10 mg effervescent tablet containing sodium bicarbonate and oxalic acid releases 0.25 mL of CO2 at T = 298.15 K and p = 1 bar. If the molar volume of CO2 is 25.0 L under such condition, what is the percentage of sodium bicarbonate in each tablet? [Molar mass of NaHCO3 = 84 g mol-1]
questions.question_raw: A 10 mg effervescent tablet containing sodium bicarbonate and oxalic acid releases 0.25 mL of CO2 at T = 298.15 K and p = 1 bar. If the molar volume of CO2 is 25.0 L under such condition, what is the percentage of sodium bicarbonate in each tablet? [Molar mass of NaHCO3 = 84 g mol-1]
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{2\;mol}{2NaHCO_3}+\;\underset{1\;mol}{H_2C_2O_4}\;\rightarrow\;\underset{2\;mol}{2CO_2\;}+\;Na_2C_4O_4+\;H_2O{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In the reaction, number of mole of CO2 produced
{tex}n=\frac{p V}{R T}=\frac{1 \text { bar } \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{L}}{0.082 \ \mathrm{L} \ \mathrm{atm} \ \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 298.15 \mathrm{K}}{/tex}
{tex}=1.02 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{mol}{/tex}
Number of mole of NaHCO3 = {tex}\frac {\text { Weight of } \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}}{Molecular \ mass \ of \ \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore w_{\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}}=1.02 \times 10^{-5} \times 84 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{mg}{/tex} = 0.856 mg
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \%=\frac{0.856}{10} \times 100=8.56 \%{/tex}

questions.question: 0.5 moles of gas A and x moles of gas B exert a pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 10m3 at 1000 K. Given R is the gas constant in JK-1 mol-1, x is
questions.question_raw: 0.5 moles of gas A and x moles of gas B exert a pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 10m3 at 1000 K. Given R is the gas constant in JK-1 mol-1, x is
questions.explanation:
From the ideal gas equation,
PV = {tex}\sum{/tex}nRT ...(i)
Given: p = 200Pa, V = 10m3, T = 1000K
nA =0.5 moles, nB = x moles
On substituting the given values in equation (i), we get
200 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = (nA + nB) {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} 1000
{tex}0.5+x=\frac{200 \times 10}{R \times 1000}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2}+x=\frac{2}{R}=\frac{2}{R}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{4-R}{2 R}{/tex}

questions.question: Two closed bulbs of equal volume (F) containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulbs is then raised to T2. The final pressure Pf is
questions.question_raw: Two closed bulbs of equal volume (F) containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulbs is then raised to T2. The final pressure Pf is
questions.explanation:
Initially,
Number of moles of gases in each container = {tex}\frac{p_{i} V}{R T_{1}}{/tex}
Total number of moles of gases in both containers = {tex}2 \frac{p_{l} V}{R T_{1}}{/tex}
After mixing, number of moles in left chamber = {tex}\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{1}}{/tex}
Number of moles in right chamber = {tex}\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{2}}{/tex}
Total number of moles = {tex}\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{1}}+\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{2}}=\frac{p_{f} V}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{1}}+\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right){/tex}
As total number of moles remains constant.
Hence, {tex}\frac{2 p_{i} V}{R T_{1}}=\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{1}}+\frac{p_{f} V}{R T_{2}} \Rightarrow p_{f}=2 p_{i}\left(\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}+T_{2}}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If Z is a compressibility factor, van der Waals' equation at low pressure can be written as
questions.question_raw: If Z is a compressibility factor, van der Waals' equation at low pressure can be written as
questions.explanation:
Compressibility factor (Z) = {tex}\frac {\text {PV}}{\text {RT}}{/tex}
van der Waals equation, (For one mole of real gas)
{tex}\left(p+\frac{a}{V^2}\right){/tex}(V - b) = RT
At low pressure, volume is very large and hence, correction term b can be neglected in comparison to very large volume of V, i.e., V - b {tex}\approx{/tex} V
{tex}\left(p+\frac{a}{V^2}\right) V{/tex} = RT; pV + {tex}\frac{a}{V}{/tex} = RT
pV = RT - {tex}\frac{a}{V}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{P V}{R T}{/tex} = {tex}1-\frac{a}{V R T}{/tex}
Hence, Z = {tex}1-\frac{a}{V R T}{/tex}

questions.question: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by C*, average speed by $\bar{C}$ and root square speed by C, then for a large number of molecules, the ratios of these speeds are
questions.question_raw: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by C*, average speed by {tex}\bar{C}{/tex} and root square speed by C, then for a large number of molecules, the ratios of these speeds are
questions.explanation:
C* = Most probable speed = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}}{/tex}
{tex}\overline C{/tex} = Average speed = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex}
C = Root square speed corrected as root means square speed, i.e.
{tex}\mathrm{rms}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex} and as we know {tex} \stackrel{*}{C}<\bar{C}<C{/tex}
{tex}\vec{C}: \bar{C}: C=1: \sqrt{\frac{4}{\mathrm{p}}}: \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} = =1: 1.128: 1.225

questions.question: For one mole of a van der Waals’ gas when b = 0 and T = 300 K, the pV vs 1/V plot is shown below. The value of the van der Waals’ constant a (atm L2 mol-2) is
questions.question_raw: For one mole of a van der Waals’ gas when b = 0 and T = 300 K, the pV vs 1/V plot is shown below. The value of the van der Waals’ constant a (atm L2 mol-2) is
questions.explanation:
The van der Waals’ equation of state is {tex}\left(p+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-n b)=n R T{/tex}
For one mole and when b = 0, the above equation condenses to {tex}\left(p+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right) V=R T{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p V=R T-\frac{a}{V}{/tex} ...(i)
Eq. (i) is a straight equation between pV and {tex}\frac{1}{v}{/tex} whose slope is ‘ - a' Equating with slope of the straight line given in the graph.
{tex}-a=\frac{20.1-21.6}{3-2}=-1.5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1.5

questions.question: The term that corrects for the attractive forces present in a real gas in the van der Waals’ equation is
questions.question_raw: The term that corrects for the attractive forces present in a real gas in the van der Waals’ equation is
questions.explanation:
In the van der Waals’ equation
{tex}\left(p+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-n b)=n R T{/tex}
The additional factor in pressure, i.e. {tex}\frac{n^2 a}{V^2}{/tex} corrects for intermolecular force while b corrects for molecular volume.

questions.question: The given graph represent the variations of Z (compressibility factor (Z) = $\frac{pV}{nRT}$) versus p, for three real gases A, B and C. Identify the only incorrect statement.
questions.question_raw: The given graph represent the variations of Z (compressibility factor (Z) = {tex}\frac{pV}{nRT}{/tex}) versus p, for three real gases A, B and C. Identify the only incorrect statement.

options.text: For the gas A, a = 0 and its dependence on p is linear at all pressure
At high pressure, the slope is positive for all real gases
For the gas B, b = 0 and its dependence on p is linear at all pressure
For the gas C, which is typical real gas for which neither a nor b = 0. By knowing the minima and the point of intersection, with Z = 1, a and b can be calculated

questions.explanation: This option is incorrect statement because at high pressure slope of the line will change from negative to positive.

questions.question: If helium and methane are allowed to diffuse out of the container under the similar conditions of temperature and pressure, then the ratio of rate of diffusion of helium to methane is
questions.question_raw: If helium and methane are allowed to diffuse out of the container under the similar conditions of temperature and pressure, then the ratio of rate of diffusion of helium to methane is
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{r_{(\mathrm{He})}}{r_{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right)}}=\sqrt{\frac{16}{4}}=2: 1{/tex}

questions.question: For a monatomic gas kinetic energy = E. The relation with rms velocity is
questions.question_raw: For a monatomic gas kinetic energy = E. The relation with rms velocity is
questions.explanation:
Kinetic energy {tex}(E)=\frac{3}{2} k T{/tex}
EMS speed {tex}(u)=\sqrt{\frac{3 k T}{m}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ u=\sqrt{\textstyle\frac{2E}m}{/tex}

questions.question: Positive deviation from ideal behaviour takes place because of
questions.question_raw: Positive deviation from ideal behaviour takes place because of
questions.explanation:
Positive deviation corresponds to Z > 1
{tex}\because \quad Z=\frac{p V}{n R T}{/tex}, for positive deviation, {tex}\frac{pV}{nRT}{/tex} > 1

questions.question: The root mean square velocity of an ideal gas at constant pressure varies with density (d) as
questions.question_raw: The root mean square velocity of an ideal gas at constant pressure varies with density (d) as
questions.explanation:
Root mean square velocity {tex}\left(u_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}p\cdot M{/tex} = dRT

Substituting for RT/M in urms expression gives,
{tex}u_{\max }=\sqrt{\frac{3 p}{d}} \Rightarrow u_{\operatorname{ms}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}{/tex}

questions.question: The compressibility of a gas is less than unity at STP. Therefore,
questions.question_raw: The compressibility of a gas is less than unity at STP. Therefore,
questions.explanation:
Compressibility factor {tex}(Z)=\frac{V}{V_{\mathrm{id}}}<1{/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V < 22.4 L
{tex}\because{/tex}  Vid (1 mol) = 22.4 L at STP

questions.question: The rms velocity of hydrogen is $\sqrt{7}$ times the rms velocity of nitrogen. If T is the temperature of the gas
questions.question_raw: The rms velocity of hydrogen is {tex}\sqrt{7}{/tex} times the rms velocity of nitrogen. If T is the temperature of the gas
questions.explanation:
Root mean square speed {tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{u_{\mathrm{rms}}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}{u_{\mathrm{rms}}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)}=\sqrt{7}=\sqrt{\frac{T\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}{2} \times \frac{28}{T\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 7=\frac{14 T\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}{T\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T(N2) = 2T(H2) i.e. T(H2) < T(N2)

questions.question: A gas will approach ideal behaviour at
questions.question_raw: A gas will approach ideal behaviour at
questions.explanation: At high temperature and low pressure, the gas volume is infinitely large and both intermolecular force as well as molecular volume can be ignored. Under this condition postulates of kinetic theory applies appropriately and gas approaches ideal behaviour.

questions.question: According to Graham’s law, at a given temperature the ratio of the rates of diffusion $\frac{r_A}{r_B}$ of gases A and B is given by (where, p and M are pressures and molecular weights of gases A and B respectively)
questions.question_raw: According to Graham’s law, at a given temperature the ratio of the rates of diffusion {tex}\frac{r_A}{r_B}{/tex} of gases A and B is given by (where, p and M are pressures and molecular weights of gases A and B respectively)
questions.explanation:
According to Graham’s law of diffusion for two gases undergoing diffusion at different pressures through same hole
{tex}\frac{r_A}{r_B}=\sqrt{\frac{M_B}{M_A}} \times \frac{P_A}{P_B}{/tex} {tex}\left(r \propto P \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}} \quad \text { At constant temperature }\right){/tex}

questions.question: The compressibility factor for an ideal gas is
questions.question_raw: The compressibility factor for an ideal gas is
questions.explanation:
Compressibility factor {tex}(Z)=\frac{V}{V_{\text {ideal }}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} For ideal gas V = Videal

questions.question: The ratio between the root mean square speed of H2 at 50 K and that of O2 at 800 K is
questions.question_raw: The ratio between the root mean square speed of H2 at 50 K and that of O2 at 800 K is
questions.explanation:
Expression of rms is, {tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{u_{\operatorname{ms}}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \text { at } 50 \mathrm{K}\right)}{u_{\operatorname{ms}}\left(\mathrm{O}_{2} \text { at } 800 \mathrm{K}\right)}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 50}{2}}}{\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 800}{32}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{50}{2} \times \frac{32}{800}}=1{/tex}

questions.question: Equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25° C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is
questions.question_raw: Equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25° C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is
questions.explanation:
Let x g of each gas is mixed.
Mole of ethane = {tex}\frac{x}{30}{/tex}
Mole of hydrogen = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Mole fraction of hydrogen = {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{2}}{\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{30}}=\frac{15}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\text { Partial pressure of } \mathrm{H}_{2}}{\text { Total pressure }}{/tex} = mole fraction of hydrogen
= 15 : 16

questions.question: At constant volume, for a fixed number of moles of a gas the pressure of the gas increases with rise of temperature due to
questions.question_raw: At constant volume, for a fixed number of moles of a gas the pressure of the gas increases with rise of temperature due to

options.text: increase rate of collisions amongst molecules

questions.explanation:
Average speed {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex} 
i.e. at constant volume, for a fixed mass, increasing temperature increases average speeds and molecules collide more frequently to the wall of container leading to increase in gas pressure.

questions.question: According to kinetic theory of gases, for a diatomic molecule
questions.question_raw: According to kinetic theory of gases, for a diatomic molecule

options.text: the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to the root mean velocity of the molecule
the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to mean velocity of the molecule
the root mean square velocity of the molecule is inversely proportional to the temperature
the mean translational kinetic energy of the molecule is proportional to the absolute temperature

questions.explanation:
Pressure exerted by the gas, P = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \frac{m n u^2}{V}{/tex} ...(i)
Here, u = root mean square velocity
m = mass of a molecule, n = No. of molecules of the gas Hence, (a) & (b) are clearly wrong.
Again u2 = {tex}\frac {3\text {RT}}{\text M}{/tex} [explained from (i)]
Here, M = Molecular wt. of the gas;
Hence, (c) is wrong
Further, Average K.E. = {tex}\frac 32{/tex} KT; Hence, (d) is true.

questions.question: The rate of diffusion of methane at a given temperature is twice that of a gas X. The molecular weight of X is
