questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}{r_{X}}=2=\sqrt{\frac{M_{X}}{16}} \Rightarrow M_{X}{/tex} = 64

questions.question: The density of neon will be highest at
questions.question_raw: The density of neon will be highest at
questions.explanation:
The ideal gas equation, {tex}p V=n R T=\frac{w}{M} R T{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p M=\left(\frac{w}{V}\right) R T=d R T{/tex} 9d =density)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad d=\frac{p M}{R T}{/tex}
i.e. density will be greater at low temperature and high pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following volume (V) temperature (T) plots represents the behaviour of one mole of an ideal gas at the atmospheric pressure?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following volume (V) temperature (T) plots represents the behaviour of one mole of an ideal gas at the atmospheric pressure?
questions.explanation:
Find the volume by either
V = {tex}\frac {RT}{P}{/tex} (PV = RT) or P1V1 = P2V2 and and match it with the values given in graph to find correct answer.
Volume of 1 mole of an ideal gas at 273 K and 1 atm is 22.4 L and that at 373 K and 1 atm pressure is calculated as;
V =  {tex}\frac {RT}{P}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {0.082 \times 373}{1}{/tex} = 30.58 L {tex}\cong{/tex} 30.6 L

questions.question: The value of van der Waals’ constant a for the gases O2, N2, NH3 and CH4 are 1.360,1.390,4.170 and 2.253L2 atm mol-2 respectively. The gas which can most easily be liquefied is
questions.question_raw: The value of van der Waals’ constant a for the gases O2, N2, NH3 and CH4 are 1.360,1.390,4.170 and 2.253L2 atm mol-2 respectively. The gas which can most easily be liquefied is
questions.explanation:
The ease of liquefication of a gas depends on their intermolecular force of attraction which in turn is measured in terms of van der Waals’ constant a. Hence, higher the value of a, greater the intermolecular force of attraction, easier the liquefication.
In the present case, NH3 has highest a, can most easily be liquefied.

questions.question: A bottle of dry ammonia and a bottle of dry hydrogen chloride connected through a long tube are opened simultaneously at both ends the white ammonium chloride ring first formed will be
questions.question_raw: A bottle of dry ammonia and a bottle of dry hydrogen chloride connected through a long tube are opened simultaneously at both ends the white ammonium chloride ring first formed will be

options.text: throughout the length of the tube
at the centre of the tube

questions.explanation:
Rate of diffusion {tex}\propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{\text { Molecular mass }}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Molecular mass of HCl > molecular mass of NH3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} HCl diffuses at slower rate and white ammonium chloride is first formed near HCl bottle.

questions.question: In van der Waals’ equation of state for a non-ideal gas, the term that accounts for intermolecular forces is
questions.question_raw: In van der Waals’ equation of state for a non-ideal gas, the term that accounts for intermolecular forces is
questions.explanation:
In van der Waals’ equation of state {tex}\left(p+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex} (V - b) = RT (For 1 mole)
The first factor (p + a/V2) correct for intermolecular force while the second term (V - b) correct for molecular volume.

questions.question: The average velocity of an ideal gas molecule at 27°C is 0.3 m/s. The average velocity at 927°C will be
questions.question_raw: The average velocity of an ideal gas molecule at 27°C is 0.3 m/s. The average velocity at 927°C will be
questions.explanation:
Expression for average velocity is {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex}
For the same gas but at different temperature
{tex}\frac{u_{\mathrm{avg}}\left(T_{1}\right)}{u_{\mathrm{avg}}\left(T_{2}\right)}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{1200}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} uav (927oC) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} uav (27oC) = 0.6 ms-1

questions.question: Rate of diffusion of a gas is
questions.question_raw: Rate of diffusion of a gas is

options.text: inversely proportional to the square root of its molecular weight
directly proportional to the square root of its molecular weight
directly proportional to its molecular weight

questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{r_1}{r_2}=\sqrt{\frac{d_2}{d_1}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_2}{M_1}}{/tex}

questions.question: Equal weights of methane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is
questions.question_raw: Equal weights of methane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is
questions.explanation:
Let x grams of each hydrogen and methane are mixed,
Moles of H2 = {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Moles of CH4 = {tex}\frac{x}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Mole fraction of H2 = {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{2}}{\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{16}}=\frac{8}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\text { Partial pressure of } \mathrm{H}_{2}}{\text { Total pressure }}{/tex} = Mole fraction of H2 = {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: When an ideal gas undergoes unrestrained expansion, no cooling occurs because the molecules
questions.question_raw: When an ideal gas undergoes unrestrained expansion, no cooling occurs because the molecules

options.text: exert no attractive forces on each other
do work equal to loss in kinetic energy

questions.explanation: According to postulates of kinetic theory, there is no intermolecular attractions or repulsions between the molecules of ideal gases.

questions.question: Helium atom is two times heavier than a hydrogen molecule. At 298 K, the average kinetic energy of a helium atom is
questions.question_raw: Helium atom is two times heavier than a hydrogen molecule. At 298 K, the average kinetic energy of a helium atom is

options.text: four times that of a hydrogen molecule
same as that of a hydrogen molecule
two times that of a hydrogen molecule
half that of a hydrogen molecule

questions.explanation:
According to kinetic theory, average kinetic energy (E) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} k_{B} T{/tex}  where, kB is Boltzmann’s constant. Since, it is independent of
molar mass, it will be same for He and H2 at a given temperature.

questions.question: Equal weights of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is
questions.question_raw: Equal weights of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is
questions.explanation:
If x g of both oxygen and methane are mixed then :
Mole of oxygen = {tex}\frac{x}{32}{/tex}
Mole of methane = {tex}\frac{x}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Mole fraction of oxygen = {tex}\frac{\frac{x}{32}}{\frac{x}{32}+\frac{x}{16}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
According to law of partial pressure
Partial pressure of oxygen (pO2) = Mole fraction {tex}\times{/tex} Total pressure
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{p}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The temperature at which a real gas obeys the ideal gas laws over a wide range of pressure is
questions.question_raw: The temperature at which a real gas obeys the ideal gas laws over a wide range of pressure is
questions.explanation:
It is the Boyle temperature TB. At Boyle temperature, the first virial coefficient (B) vanishes and real gas approaches ideal behaviour.
{tex}T_{B}=\frac{a}{R b}{/tex}
Here, a and b are van der Waals’ constants.

questions.question: The ratio of root mean square velocity to average velocity of a gas molecule at a particular temperature is
questions.question_raw: The ratio of root mean square velocity to average velocity of a gas molecule at a particular temperature is
questions.explanation:
The two types of speeds are defined as;
Root mean square speed (urms) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
Average speed (uav) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex}
For the same gas, at a given temperature, M and T are same, therefore
{tex}\frac{u_{\mathrm{rms}}}{u_{\mathrm{av}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}: \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}}=\sqrt{3}: \sqrt{2.54}{/tex} = 1.085 : 1

questions.question: Which of the following exhibits the weakest intermolecular forces?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following exhibits the weakest intermolecular forces?
questions.explanation: Nobel gases have no intermolecular forces due to inertness.

questions.question: An LPG cylinder, containing 15 kg butane at 27°C and 10 atm pressure, is leaking. After one day, its pressure decreased to 8 atm. The quantity of gas leaked is:
questions.question_raw: An LPG cylinder, containing 15 kg butane at 27°C and 10 atm pressure, is leaking. After one day, its pressure decreased to 8 atm. The quantity of gas leaked is:
questions.explanation:
According to ideal gas equation, PV = nRT
In the question, V, R, and T remain constant. Only P and n are changing
P is directly proportional to n.
n = number of moles = mass/molar mass
Molar mass in both cases is also constant, so. P is directly proportional to the mass of gas.
P1/P2=m1/m2
P1 = 10 bar, P2 = ?
m1 = 15 kg, m2 = 10 kg
After putting the values,
10/P2=15/10/
P2 = 6.67 bar
Pressure loss = 10 - 6.67 = 3.33 bar

questions.question: If the density ratio of O2 and H2 is 16: 1, then ratio of their Vrms will be:
questions.question_raw: If the density ratio of O2 and H2 is 16: 1, then ratio of their Vrms will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\rho\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)}{\rho\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}=\frac{16}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{M\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)}{M\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}=\frac{16}{1}{/tex}
{tex}v_{r m s} \propto\left(\frac{1}{M}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v_{r m s}\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)}{v_{r m s}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}=\left(\frac{M\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)}{M\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following gases will have the highest rate of diffusion?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following gases will have the highest rate of diffusion?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\text { Rate of Diffusion } \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M o l e c u l a r } M a s s}{/tex}
NH3​ has lesser molecular mass than the above gases, so the rate of diffusion of NH3​ is higher. This is according to Graham's law of Diffusion of gases.

questions.question: A mixture of 40 g of oxygen and 40 g of helium has a total pressure of 0.9 atm. The partial pressure of oxygen is:
questions.question_raw: A mixture of 40 g of oxygen and 40 g of helium has a total pressure of 0.9 atm. The partial pressure of oxygen is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}No. \ of \ moles of \ {O}_{2}=\frac{\text { Given mass }}{\text { Mol. mass }}=\frac{40}{32}{/tex}
=1.25mol
{tex}\text { No. of moles of } \mathrm{He}=\frac{40}{4}=10 \mathrm{mol}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Mole fraction of } \mathrm{O}_{2}=\frac{1.25}{1.25+10}=\frac{1.25}{11.25}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
{tex}Partial \ pressure \ of \ oxygen =\frac{1}{9} \times 0.9atm =0.1atm{/tex}

questions.question: Two different gases enclosed in different flasks A and B at same temperature and pressure were found to contain same number of molecules. The ratio of volumes of the flasks A and B must be
questions.question_raw: Two different gases enclosed in different flasks A and B at same temperature and pressure were found to contain same number of molecules. The ratio of volumes of the flasks A and B must be
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law states that the same volume of all gases at the same temperature and pressure, contain an equal number of molecules. Hence, the ratio of volumes must be 1: 1.

questions.question: The temperature, at which the density of O2 at 1 atm, is the same as that of CH4 at S.T.P., is
questions.question_raw: The temperature, at which the density of O2 at 1 atm, is the same as that of CH4 at S.T.P., is
questions.explanation:
The density of O2 at S.T.P.
{tex}\left(d_{1}\right)=\frac{32}{22 \cdot 4} g/litres \ and \ \ that \ of \ {CH}_{4} \ at \ S.T.P.{/tex}
{tex}\left(d_{2}\right)=\frac{16}{22 \cdot 4} \text { g/litres }{/tex}
{tex}\text { We know that } d_{1} T_{1}=d_{2} T_{2} \text { or } T_{2}=\frac{d_{1} T_{1}}{d_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{32}{22.4} \times \frac{273}{1} \times \frac{22 \cdot 4}{16}=546 \mathrm{K}=273^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}

questions.question: A certain gas diffuses four times as quickly as oxygen. The molecular weight of the gas is
questions.question_raw: A certain gas diffuses four times as quickly as oxygen. The molecular weight of the gas is
questions.explanation:
According to Graham's law, the rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the square roots of its molecule weight.
Let m be the mass of the unknown gas
m2​ is the mass of oxygen
r1​ = rate of diffusion of the unknown gas
r2​ = rate of diffusion of oxygen
{tex}\frac{​​​​​​​r_1}{​r_2​​}=\sqrt\frac{m_2}{​m_1}​​​{/tex}  
Given {tex}\frac{r_1}{r_2}=4{/tex} 
thus {tex}4= \sqrt\frac{32}{m_1}{/tex}  ​​
Squaring both sides
16= 32​/m
m1​=2g

questions.question: Which of the following gas molecules have equal total kinetic energy and translational kinetic energy?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following gas molecules have equal total kinetic energy and translational kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: The total kinetic energy of a molecule is the sum of its translational, vibrational, and rotational kinetic energies. The monatomic molecules do not possess vibrational and rotational kinetic energies. Hence, noble gases (like He, Ar, etc.) fulfill the criteria.

questions.question: If a gas occupies a volume of 300 cc at $27^\circ C$ and 620 mm pressure, then the volume of the gas at $47^\circ C$ and 640 mm pressure, is
questions.question_raw: If a gas occupies a volume of 300 cc at {tex}27^\circ C{/tex} and 620 mm pressure, then the volume of the gas at {tex}47^\circ C{/tex} and 640 mm pressure, is
questions.explanation:
According to Charles’ and Boyle’s Law,
{tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{620 \times 300}{300}=\frac{640 \times V_{2}}{320} \Rightarrow V_{2}=310 \mathrm{cc}{/tex}

questions.question: The compressibility factor of an ideal gas is
questions.question_raw: The compressibility factor of an ideal gas is
questions.explanation:
For an ideal gas,
PV=nRT
Z=1
The compressibility factor (Z), also known as the compression factor, is the ratio of the molar volume of a gas to the molar volume of an ideal gas at the same temperature and pressure for an ideal gas the compressibility factor is 1.

questions.question: In the gas equation: $PV = nRT$ . The incorrect statement is
questions.question_raw: In the gas equation: {tex}PV = nRT{/tex} . The incorrect statement is

options.text: n is the no. of moles of the gas have volume V
V is the volume of one mole of the gas
n is the number of molecules of the gas
P is the pressure of one mole of the gas

questions.explanation:
In the general gas equation, 
PV=nRT,

  • P is the pressure of the ideal gas.
  • V is the volume of the ideal gas.
  • n is the amount of ideal gas measured in terms of moles.
  • R is the universal gas constant.
  • T is the temperature.

questions.question: At what pressure will a quantity of gas, which occupies 100 ml at a pressure of 720 mm, occupy a volume of 84 ml?
questions.question_raw: At what pressure will a quantity of gas, which occupies 100 ml at a pressure of 720 mm, occupy a volume of 84 ml?
questions.explanation:
According to Boyle's law,
P1V1 =P2 V2
Putting values here, we get;
{tex}100 \times 720=P_{2} \times 84{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow P_{2}=857.14 \mathrm{mm}{/tex}

questions.question: If 300 ml of a gas at $27^\circ C$ is cooled to $7^\circ C$ at constant pressure, its final volume will be:
questions.question_raw: If 300 ml of a gas at {tex}27^\circ C{/tex} is cooled to {tex}7^\circ C{/tex} at constant pressure, its final volume will be:
questions.explanation:
According to Charle's law,
{tex}\Rightarrow Putting \ the \ given \ values: \frac{300 \mathrm{mL}}{300 \mathrm{K}}=\frac{V_{2}}{280} \Rightarrow V_{2}=280 \mathrm{mL}{/tex}

questions.question: Pressure in a mixture of 4 g of O2 and 2 g of H2 confined in a bulb of 1 litre at 0°C is
questions.question_raw: Pressure in a mixture of 4 g of O2 and 2 g of H2 confined in a bulb of 1 litre at 0°C is
questions.explanation:
Given:
V = 1L
T = 273 K
Let us find the partial pressure of O2
No. of moles = 4/32 =0.125 moles
{tex}P_{1}=\frac{n R T}{v}=0.125 \times 0.082 \times \frac{273}{1}=2.80 \mathrm{atm}{/tex}
Total pressure of the mixture = 2.80 + 22.38 = 25.18atm.
Therefore, 25.18 {tex}\approx{/tex} 25.21atm

questions.question: Helium atom is two times heavier than a hydrogen molecule. At 298 K, the average kinetic energy of the helium atom is
questions.question_raw: Helium atom is two times heavier than a hydrogen molecule. At 298 K, the average kinetic energy of the helium atom is

options.text: Half that of a hydrogen molecule
Two times that of a hydrogen molecule
Four times that of a hydrogen molecule
Same as that of a hydrogen molecule

questions.explanation:
Kinetic energy per mole or per molecule of a gas depends only on the temperature and not on the nature of the gas.
{tex}K E=\frac{3}{2} R T{/tex}
So, the average kinetic energy of a helium atom is the same as that of a hydrogen molecule.

questions.question: If the r.m.s. speed of a gas molecule at $27^\circ C$ is $100\sqrt 2 {\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}$ the r.m.s. speed at $327^\circ C$ would be:
questions.question_raw: If the r.m.s. speed of a gas molecule at {tex}27^\circ C{/tex} is {tex}100\sqrt 2 {\text{ }}m{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} the r.m.s. speed at {tex}327^\circ C{/tex} would be:
questions.explanation:
Root mean square velocity is given by:
{tex}v_{m s}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} \Rightarrow v_{m s} \propto \sqrt{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v_{\text {rms}}\left(\text {at } 27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)}{v_{\text {ms}}\left(\text {at } 327^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)}=\sqrt{\frac{(27+273) \mathrm{K}}{(327+273) \mathrm{K}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{600}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad v_{r m s}\left(\text { at } 327^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)=100 \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}{/tex}
= 200 m/s

questions.question: If P is pressure and p is density of a gas, then P and $\rho $ are related as
questions.question_raw: If P is pressure and p is density of a gas, then P and {tex}\rho {/tex} are related as
questions.explanation:
For a gas,
PV = nRT
so, {tex}P \propto \frac{1}{V} \text { and density } \frac{m}{V}=\rho ; \frac{1}{V}=\frac{\rho}{m}{/tex}
{tex}P \propto \frac{\rho}{m}{/tex}
{tex}i.e. Pressure \propto \rho{/tex}

questions.question: Dominance of strong repulsive forces among the molecules of the gas (Z = compressibility factor)
questions.question_raw: Dominance of strong repulsive forces among the molecules of the gas (Z = compressibility factor)

options.text: Depends on Z and indicated by Z < 1
Depends on Z and indicated by Z > 1
Depends on Z and indicated by Z=1

questions.explanation: Z > 1 implies that the gas is less compressible than the ideal gas, i.e. when we compress the gas, the decrease in volume is less than expected. This is because of the dominance of strong repulsive forces between the molecules preventing them to come closer.

questions.question: Dipole-dipole forces act between the molecules possessing permanent dipole. Ends of dipoles possess partial charges. The partial charge is:
questions.question_raw: Dipole-dipole forces act between the molecules possessing permanent dipole. Ends of dipoles possess partial charges. The partial charge is:
questions.explanation: Partial charges are always lesser than the charge of 1 electron.

questions.question: As the temperature increases, average kinetic energy of molecules increases. What would be the effect of increase of temperature on pressure provided the volume is constant?
questions.question_raw: As the temperature increases, average kinetic energy of molecules increases. What would be the effect of increase of temperature on pressure provided the volume is constant?
questions.explanation: P {tex}\propto{/tex} T at constant V. This is Gay Lussac Law

questions.question: What is SI unit of viscosity coefficient ($\eta$)?
questions.question_raw: What is SI unit of viscosity coefficient ({tex}\eta{/tex})?
questions.explanation:
Force = {tex}\eta \times A \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} (where {tex}\eta{/tex} is coefficient of viscosity)
or {tex}\eta=\frac{F}{A} \times \frac{d x}{d u}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} is velocity gradient and A is area
{tex}\therefore{/tex} unit of {tex}\eta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \times \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}^{2} \times \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}}{/tex} = Nm-2 sec

 p in N/m2

questions.explanation: Boiling occurs at lower temperature where pressure is low.

questions.explanation: The surface tension of liquid decreases with an increase in temperature as an increase in temperature leads to an increase in kinetic energy of molecules.

questions.question: A cylinder was filled with gaseous mixture containing CO and N2 (equal masses). The ratio of their partial pressure in cylinder are:
questions.question_raw: A cylinder was filled with gaseous mixture containing CO and N2 (equal masses). The ratio of their partial pressure in cylinder are:
questions.explanation:
{tex}P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}^{\prime}{/tex} = PT {tex}\times{/tex} mole fraction of N2 
{tex}P_{\mathrm{CO}}^{\prime}{/tex} = PT {tex}\times{/tex} mole fraction of CO
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{P_{\mathrm{CO}}^{\prime}}{P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}^{\prime}}=\frac{\text { mole fraction of } \mathrm{N}_{2}}{\text { mole fraction of } \mathrm{CO}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_{2}}{\text { moles of } \mathrm{CO}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{w / 28}{w / 28}{/tex} = 1 : 1

questions.question: For two gases, A and B with molar masses MA and MB, it is observed that at a certain temperature, T, the mean speed of A is equal to the root mean square speed of B. Thus the mean speed of A can be made equal to the mean speed of B, if:
questions.question_raw: For two gases, A and B with molar masses MA and MB, it is observed that at a certain temperature, T, the mean speed of A is equal to the root mean square speed of B. Thus the mean speed of A can be made equal to the mean speed of B, if:

options.text: both A and B are raised to a higher temperature
A is lowered to a temperature T2 = $\frac{8}{3 \pi} T$ while A is at T
both A and B are placed at lower temperature
A is at temperature, T and B at T', T > T'

questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{AV}-A}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_{A}}}{/tex}; {tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}-B}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M_{B}}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{3 \pi}=\frac{M_{A}}{M_{B}}{/tex} 
For {tex}u_{\mathrm{AV}-A}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_{A}}}{/tex}; {tex}u_{\mathrm{AV}-B}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T_{2}}{\pi M_{B}}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{T_{2}}{T}=\frac{M_{A}}{M_{B}}=\frac{8}{3 \pi}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T2 = {tex}\frac{8}{3 \pi} \cdot T{/tex}

= Nm-2 {tex}\times{/tex} (m3)2 mol-2

questions.question: The volume of ideal gas is lowered by 5% at pressure P and constant temperature T. What is the new pressure?
questions.question_raw: The volume of ideal gas is lowered by 5% at pressure P and constant temperature T. What is the new pressure?
questions.explanation:
At constt. temperature : P1V1 = P2V2 
Let volume be V at pressure P, which increases by 5% to show new pressure P' at constant temperature.
PV = {tex}P^{\prime} \times\left[V-\frac{5 V}{100}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{95}{100} \times P^{\prime}{/tex} 
or P' = {tex}\frac{100 P}{95}{/tex} = 1.0526

questions.question: A 2.24 litre cylinder containing O2 gas at 27°C and 2 atm is found to develop a leakage. When the leakage was repaired, the pressure dropped to 100 cm of Hg at 27°C. The number of moles of gas escaped out during leakage is:
questions.question_raw: A 2.24 litre cylinder containing O2 gas at 27°C and 2 atm is found to develop a leakage. When the leakage was repaired, the pressure dropped to 100 cm of Hg at 27°C. The number of moles of gas escaped out during leakage is:
questions.explanation:
Using PV = nRT
Initially 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.24 = n {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300;
n = 0.182
Finally, {tex}\frac{100}{76} \times 2.24{/tex} = ni {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n1 = 0.120
{tex}\therefore{/tex} moles given out 0.182 - 0.120 = 0.062

questions.question: If pressure of a gas contained in a closed vessel is increased by 0.4% when heated by 1°C its initial temperature must be:
questions.question_raw: If pressure of a gas contained in a closed vessel is increased by 0.4% when heated by 1°C its initial temperature must be:
questions.explanation:
T2 = T1 + 1; P2 = P1 + {tex}\frac{0.4 P_{1}}{100}{/tex};
Now use, {tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex} 
assuming V1 = V2 
{tex}\frac{P_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{100 P_{1}+0.4 P_{1}}{100\left(T_{1}+T\right)}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{P_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{100.4 P_{1}}{100 T_{1}+100 T}{/tex} 
or 100.4T1 - 100T1 = 100T
T1 = 250 K

questions.question: The kinetic energy of two moles of N2 at 27°C is (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1):
questions.question_raw: The kinetic energy of two moles of N2 at 27°C is (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1):
questions.explanation:
KE = {tex}\frac{3}{2} n R T=\frac{3}{2} \times 2 \times 8.314 \times 300{/tex} 
= 7482.6 J

questions.question: Four one litre flasks are separately filled with the gases, O2, F2, CH4 and CO2 under the same conditions. The ratio of number of molecules in these gases:
questions.question_raw: Four one litre flasks are separately filled with the gases, O2, F2, CH4 and CO2 under the same conditions. The ratio of number of molecules in these gases:

questions.question: For a given mass of a gas, if pressure is reduced to half and temperature is increased two times, then the volume would become:
questions.question_raw: For a given mass of a gas, if pressure is reduced to half and temperature is increased two times, then the volume would become:
questions.explanation:
From {tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{P V_{1}}{T}=\frac{P}{2} \times \frac{V_{2}}{2 \times T}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V2 = 4V1

questions.question: 26 mL of CO2 are passed over hot coke. The maximum volume of CO formed is:
questions.question_raw: 26 mL of CO2 are passed over hot coke. The maximum volume of CO formed is:
questions.explanation:
CO2 + C {tex}\to{/tex} 2CO
Ratio of CO : CO2 = 2:1 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Volume of CO formed = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 26 = 52ml

questions.question: Four particles have speed 2, 3, 4 and 5 cm/s respectively. Their RMS speed is:
questions.question_raw: Four particles have speed 2, 3, 4 and 5 cm/s respectively. Their RMS speed is:
questions.explanation: {tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\left[\frac{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}}{1+1+1+1}\right]}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{54}{4}\right]}=\sqrt{\frac{27}{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: For hydrogen gas Cp - Cv = a, and for oxygen gas Cp - Cv = b, so the relation between a and b is:
questions.question_raw: For hydrogen gas Cp - Cv = a, and for oxygen gas Cp - Cv = b, so the relation between a and b is:
questions.explanation: Cp - Cv = R for each gas.

questions.question: A V dm3 flask contains gas A and another flask of 2V dm3 contains gas B at the same temperature. If density of gas A is 3.0 g dm-3 and of gas B is 1.5 g dm-3 and molar mass of A = $\frac{1}{2}$ molar mass of B, then the ratio of pressure exerted by gases is:
questions.question_raw: A V dm3 flask contains gas A and another flask of 2V dm3 contains gas B at the same temperature. If density of gas A is 3.0 g dm-3 and of gas B is 1.5 g dm-3 and molar mass of A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} molar mass of B, then the ratio of pressure exerted by gases is:
questions.explanation:
Using PV = {tex}\frac{w}{m} R T{/tex} or P = {tex}\frac{d}{m} R T{/tex} 
For gas A, PA = {tex}\frac{3}{m_{A}} \times R \times T{/tex} and PB = {tex}\frac{1.5}{m_{B}} \times R \times T{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{P_{A}}{P_{B}}=\frac{2 \times m_{B}}{m_{A}}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times m_{A}}{m_{A}}{/tex} = 4 ({tex}\because{/tex} mA = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{B}{/tex})

questions.question: If the pressure of $\frac{N_2}{H_2}$ mixture in a closed vessel is 100 atmospheres and 20% of the mixture reacts, then the pressure at the same temperature would be:
questions.question_raw: If the pressure of {tex}\frac{N_2}{H_2}{/tex} mixture in a closed vessel is 100 atmospheres and 20% of the mixture reacts, then the pressure at the same temperature would be:
questions.explanation:
20% mixture produces 10% NH3
N2 + 3H2 = 2NH3
Thus mixture left is 90%. Also P {tex}\propto{/tex} n at constant V and T

questions.question: A person living in Shimla observed that cooking food without using a pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude:
questions.question_raw: A person living in Shimla observed that cooking food without using a pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude:
questions.explanation: Boiling of water occurs at lower temperature where atmospheric pressure is low.

questions.question: Which of the following property of water can be used to explain the spherical shape of rain droplets?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following property of water can be used to explain the spherical shape of rain droplets?
questions.explanation: Surface tension

questions.question_raw: Which curve does not represent Boyle’ s law?
questions.explanation: According to Boyle’s law V = {tex}\frac{K}{P}{/tex}.

questions.question: V versus T curves at constant pressure P1 and P2 for an ideal gas are shown in fig. Which is correct?
questions.question_raw: V versus T curves at constant pressure P1 and P2 for an ideal gas are shown in fig. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: PV = constant at constant temperature.

questions.question: Figure shows graphs of pressure versus density for an ideal gas at two temperatures T1 and T2. Which is correct?
questions.question_raw: Figure shows graphs of pressure versus density for an ideal gas at two temperatures T1 and T2. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Use : PM = dRT i.e., P {tex}\propto{/tex} d and P {tex}\propto{/tex} T

questions.question: The ratio of average speed of an oxygen molecule to the RMS speed of a nitrogen molecule at the same temperature is:
questions.question_raw: The ratio of average speed of an oxygen molecule to the RMS speed of a nitrogen molecule at the same temperature is:
questions.explanation:
UAVO2 = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{8 R T}{\pi \times 32}\right]}{/tex}; Urms N2 = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{3 R T}{28}\right]}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{U_{\mathrm{AV}} \mathrm{O}_{2}}{U_{\mathrm{rms}} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{8 \times 28}{\pi \times 32 \times 3}\right]}=\sqrt{\left[\frac{7}{3 \pi}\right]}{/tex}

questions.question: Volume occupied by molecules of one mole of gas at NTP, having radius 10-8 cm is approximately:
questions.question_raw: Volume occupied by molecules of one mole of gas at NTP, having radius 10-8 cm is approximately:
questions.explanation:
Volume of molecules in 1 mole = {tex}4 \times N \times v{/tex} = {tex}4 \times N \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}{/tex} 
= {tex}4 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(10^{-8}\right)^{3}{/tex} = 10.09 mL

questions.question: The ratio $\frac{a}{b}$ (the terms used in van der Waals’ equation) has the unit:
questions.question_raw: The ratio {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} (the terms used in van der Waals’ equation) has the unit:
questions.explanation:
Unit of a = dtm litre2 mol-2 = atm dm6 mol-2 = dyne cm4 mol-2 
Unit of b = litre mol-1 = dm3mol-1 = cm3 mol-1

questions.question: A gas in an open container is heated from 27°C to 127°C. The fraction of the original amount of gas remaining in the container will be:
questions.question_raw: A gas in an open container is heated from 27°C to 127°C. The fraction of the original amount of gas remaining in the container will be:
questions.explanation:
On heating a gas in an open container (P constant. V constant.), moles of gas goes out. Thus n1T1 = n2T2 or n1 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = n2 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}n_{2}=\frac{3 n_{1}}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Fraction of gas left = {tex}\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\frac{3 n_{1}}{4 n_{1}}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: Gases deviate from ideal gas behaviour because their molecules:
questions.question_raw: Gases deviate from ideal gas behaviour because their molecules:

options.text: have forces of attraction between them

questions.explanation: According to van der Waals’ deviations from ideal gas behaviour are due to appreciable forces of attractions among molecules, as well as appreciable volume occupied by molecules at high pressure.

questions.question: The temperature at which H2 has the same RMS speed (at 1 atm) as that of O2 at NTP is:
questions.question_raw: The temperature at which H2 has the same RMS speed (at 1 atm) as that of O2 at NTP is:
questions.explanation:
Urms H2 = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{3 R T_{1}}{2}\right]}{/tex}; Urms O2 = {tex}\sqrt{\left[\frac{3 R T_{2}}{32}\right]}{/tex} (T2 = 273 K)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{T_{1}}{2}=\frac{T_{2}}{32}{/tex} or T1 = {tex}\frac{2 \times 273}{32}{/tex} = 17 K

questions.question: The circulation of blood in human body supplies O2 and releases CO2. The concentration of O2 and CO2 is variable but on the average, 100 mL blood contains 0.02g of O2 and 0.08 g of CO2. Calculate the volume of O2 and CO2 at 1 atm and body temperature 37°C, assuming 10 litre blood in human body:
questions.question_raw: The circulation of blood in human body supplies O2 and releases CO2. The concentration of O2 and CO2 is variable but on the average, 100 mL blood contains 0.02g of O2 and 0.08 g of CO2. Calculate the volume of O2 and CO2 at 1 atm and body temperature 37°C, assuming 10 litre blood in human body:
questions.explanation:
100 mL blood has 0.02 g O2 and 0.08 g CO2 
10,000 mL blood has 2 g O2 and 8 g CO2 
Now, PV = {tex}\frac{w}{m} R T{/tex} 
For O2 : 1 {tex}\times{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{2}{32} \times 0.0821 \times 310{/tex}, {tex}V_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}{/tex} = 1.59 litre
For CO2: 1 {tex}\times{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{8}{44} \times 0.0821 \times 310{/tex}, {tex}V_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}{/tex} = 4.62 litre

questions.question: A balloon filled with ethyne is pricked with a sharp point and quickly dropped in a tank of H2 gas under identical conditions. After a while the balloon will have:
questions.question_raw: A balloon filled with ethyne is pricked with a sharp point and quickly dropped in a tank of H2 gas under identical conditions. After a while the balloon will have:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{26}}{/tex}, i.e., H2 will diffuse more from outside into balloon under identical conditions of P and T.

questions.question: A flask containing 12g of a gas of relative molecular mass 120 at a pressure of 100 atm, was evacuated by means of a pump until the pressure was 0.01 atm at the same T. Which of the following is the best estimate of the number of molecules left in the flask (N0 = 6 $\times$ 1023 mol-1)?
questions.question_raw: A flask containing 12g of a gas of relative molecular mass 120 at a pressure of 100 atm, was evacuated by means of a pump until the pressure was 0.01 atm at the same T. Which of the following is the best estimate of the number of molecules left in the flask (N0 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 mol-1)?
questions.explanation:
At constant V and T: {tex}\frac{P_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{P_{2}}{n_{2}}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{100}{12 / 120}=\frac{0.01}{n_{2}}{/tex} (n1 = {tex}\frac{12}{120}{/tex})
or n2 = {tex}\frac{0.01}{1000}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of molecules left = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 
= 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018

questions.question: For a gas $\frac{R}{C_v}$ = 0.67, the gas may be:
questions.question_raw: For a gas {tex}\frac{R}{C_v}{/tex} = 0.67, the gas may be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{R}{C_{v}}=\frac{C_{p}-C_{v}}{C_{v}}{/tex} = 0.67
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}-1{/tex} = 0.67
or {tex}\gamma=\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}{/tex} = 1.67
Thus gas is monoatomic i.e., Ne.

questions.question: The van der Waals’ equation is $\left(P+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right)$(V - nb) = nRT for n moles where a and b are van der Waals’ constants. Which of the following statements are true about a and b when the temperature of the gas is too low?
questions.question_raw: The van der Waals’ equation is {tex}\left(P+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex}(V - nb) = nRT  for n moles where a and b are van der Waals’ constants. Which of the following statements are true about a and b when the temperature of the gas is too low?
questions.explanation: The van der Waals’ constants a and b proposed to be characteristic constant were found to vary with temperature and pressure. This is one of the limitation of van der Waals’ equation.

questions.question: A vessel has nitrogen gas and water vapour at a total pressure of 1 atm. The partial pressure of water vapour is 0.3 atm. When the contents of this vessel are transformed to another vessel having one third of the capacity of original vessel, completely at the same temperature, the total pressure of the system in the new vessel is:
questions.question_raw: A vessel has nitrogen gas and water vapour at a total pressure of 1 atm. The partial pressure of water vapour is 0.3 atm. When the contents of this vessel are transformed to another vessel having one third of the capacity of original vessel, completely at the same temperature, the total pressure of the system in the new vessel is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 1 atm; 
{tex}\because P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 0.3 atm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.7 atm in I vessel
Now new pressure of N2, in another vessel may be calculated as P1V1 = P2V2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}0.7 \times V_{1}=P_{2} \times \frac{V_{1}}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex} = 2.1 atm and {tex}P_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 0.3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PT = 2.4 atm
Note that {tex}P_{H_2 O}{/tex} (v) remains constant at constant temperature.

questions.question: The compressibility factor for H2 is greater than one:
questions.question_raw: The compressibility factor for H2 is greater than one:

options.text: at low P and high T

questions.explanation: H2 and He shows exceptional behaviour due to lightest gases since attractive forces among molecules are negligible and Z = 1 + {tex}\frac{Pb}{RT}{/tex}.

questions.question: For an ideal gas $\left(\frac{\delta E}{\delta V}\right)_{T}$ is:
questions.question_raw: For an ideal gas {tex}\left(\frac{\delta E}{\delta V}\right)_{T}{/tex} is:

Encergy of gas = {tex}\frac{3}{2} R T{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\delta E}{\delta V}\right)_{T}{/tex} = 0

questions.question: Boyle’s law is valid for:
questions.question_raw: Boyle’s law is valid for:
questions.explanation: {tex}V \propto \frac{1}{P}{/tex} at constant T i. e., isothermal process

questions.question: Charles law is valid for:
questions.question_raw: Charles law is valid for:
questions.explanation: {tex}V \propto T{/tex} at constant P i.e., isobaric process.

questions.question: A plot of volume (V) versus temperature (T) for a gas at constant pressure is a straight line passing through the origin. The plots at different values of pressure are shown in fig. Which of the following order of pressure is correct for this gas?
questions.question_raw: A plot of volume (V) versus temperature (T) for a gas at constant pressure is a straight line passing through the origin. The plots at different values of pressure are shown in fig. Which of the following order of pressure is correct for this gas?
questions.explanation:
At constant temperature, PV is constant. (Higher is V, lesser will be P).
Therefore, the correct order of pressure for gas is P1 < P2 < P3 < P4.

questions.question: Equal weights of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is:
questions.question_raw: Equal weights of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}P_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\prime}{/tex} = PM {tex}\times{/tex} mole fraction of O2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{P^{\prime}}{P_{M}}{/tex} = mole fraction O2 = {tex}\frac{\frac{w}{32}}{\frac{w}{16}+\frac{w}{32}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The temperature at which a real gas obeys the ideal gas laws over a wide range of pressure is:
questions.question_raw: The temperature at which a real gas obeys the ideal gas laws over a wide range of pressure is:
questions.explanation: Boyle’s temperature

questions.question: The ratio of root mean square speed to average speed of a gas molecule at a particular temperature is:
questions.question_raw: The ratio of root mean square speed to average speed of a gas molecule at a particular temperature is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}; {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{u_{\mathrm{rms}}}{u_{\mathrm{av}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \pi}{8}}{/tex} = 1.086

questions.question: The value of van der Waals’ constant a for the gases O2, N2, NH3 and CH4 are 1.360, 1.390, 4.170 and 2.253 L2 atm mol-2 respectively. The gas which can most easily be liquefied is:
questions.question_raw: The value of van der Waals’ constant a for the gases O2, N2, NH3 and CH4 are 1.360, 1.390, 4.170 and 2.253 L2 atm mol-2 respectively. The gas which can most easily be liquefied is:
questions.explanation: Greater the value of ‘a’ i.e., intermolecular forces of attraction for gas, the more easily the gas will be liquefied.

questions.question: A liquid is in equilibrium with its vapours at it’s boiling point. On average, the molecules in the two phases have equal:
questions.question_raw: A liquid is in equilibrium with its vapours at it’s boiling point. On average, the molecules in the two phases have equal:

questions.question: Rate of diffusion of a gas is:

inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass
directly proportional to the square root of its molar mass

questions.question: The average velocity of an ideal gas molecule at 27°C is 0.3 m/s. The average velocity at 927°C will be:
questions.question_raw: The average velocity of an ideal gas molecule at 27°C is 0.3 m/s. The average velocity at 927°C will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{AV}}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{u_{1}}{u_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.3}{u_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{1200}}{/tex} or u2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1200}{300}} \times 0.3{/tex} = 0.6 ms-1

questions.question: In van der Waals’ equation of state for a non-ideal gas, the term that accounts for intermolecular forces is:
questions.question_raw: In van der Waals’ equation of state for a non-ideal gas, the term that accounts for intermolecular forces is:
questions.explanation: Pressure correction in gas equation by van der Waals’ is {tex}\frac{a}{V^2} {/tex} for 1 mole.

questions.question: A bottle of dry ammonia and a bottle of dry hydrogen chloride connected through a long tube are opened simultaneously at both ends. The white ammonium chloride ring first formed will be:
questions.question_raw: A bottle of dry ammonia and a bottle of dry hydrogen chloride connected through a long tube are opened simultaneously at both ends. The white ammonium chloride ring first formed will be:

options.text: at the centre of the tube
throughout the length of the tube

questions.explanation: HCl diffuses at a slower rate because of its higher molar mass.

questions.question: The rate of diffusion of methane at a given temperature is twice that of a gas X. The molecular weight of X is:
questions.question_raw: The rate of diffusion of methane at a given temperature is twice that of a gas X. The molecular weight of X is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}{r_{x}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{x}}{M_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}}{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\frac{2}{1}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{x}}{16}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mx = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 64

questions.question:
According to the kinetic theory of gases for a diatomic molecule :
 the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to the root mean square speed of the molecule
 the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to the mean speed of the molecule
 the root mean square speed of the molecule is inversely proportional of the temperature
 the mean translational kinetic energy of the molecule is proportional to the absolute temperature
questions.question_raw:
According to the kinetic theory of gases for a diatomic molecule :

  1. the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to the root mean square speed of the molecule
  2. the pressure exerted by the gas is proportional to the mean speed of the molecule
  3. the root mean square speed of the molecule is inversely proportional of the temperature
  4. the mean translational kinetic energy of the molecule is proportional to the absolute temperature
questions.explanation: Average K.E. = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{R T}{N}{/tex}, PV = {tex}\frac{1}{3} m n u_{\mathrm{rms}}^{2}{/tex} and {tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{m}}{/tex}

questions.question: Equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is:
questions.question_raw: Equal weights of ethane and hydrogen are mixed in an empty container at 25°C. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by hydrogen is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{P^{\prime}}{P_{M}}{/tex} = mole fraction of H2 = {tex}\frac{\frac{w}{2}}{\frac{w}{2}+\frac{w}{30}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{16}{/tex}

questions.question:

 List A
 List B

questions.question_raw:
The van der Waals’ constant a in L2 mol-2 with gases (not reported in order are given in list A and B).

List A List B
(1) C6H6 (g) (A) 0.217
(2) C6H5CH3(g) (B) 5.464

questions.question: Which of the following gas molecules has the largest mean free path?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following gas molecules has the largest mean free path?
questions.explanation: Due to higher repulsive forces. Smaller is molecule larger is mean free path.

questions.question: The ratio between the root mean square speed of H2 at 50 K and that of O2 at 800 K is:
questions.question_raw: The ratio between the root mean square speed of H2 at 50 K and that of O2 at 800 K is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{3 R T}{M}{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\left(u_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \times R \times 50}{2}}{/tex} at 50 K ...(i)
{tex}\left(u_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \times R \times 800}{32}}{/tex} at 800 K  ...(ii)
So, {tex}\frac{\left(u_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left(u_{\mathrm{rins}}\right)_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{50 \times 32}{2 \times 800}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: X mL of H2 gas effuses through a hole in container in 5 seconds. The time taken for the effusion of the same volume of the gas specified below under identical conditions is:
questions.question_raw: X mL of H2 gas effuses through a hole in container in 5 seconds. The time taken for the effusion of the same volume of the gas specified below under identical conditions is:
questions.explanation:
Use {tex}r_{0}=\frac{v}{t} \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}}{/tex} (P, T)
{tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{M_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{O}},}=\frac{x}{5} \times \frac{t}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = 20 sec

questions.question: The critical temperature of H2O is higher than O2 because the H2O molecule has:
questions.question_raw: The critical temperature of H2O is higher than O2 because the H2O molecule has:
questions.explanation: Dipole moment gives rise to a higher value of van der Waals’ constant of attraction for H2O inspite of low molar mass.

questions.question:
According to Graham’s Law, at a given temperature the ratio of the rates of diffusion, $\frac{r_A}{r_B}$ of gases A and B are given by:
(Where P and M are pressures and molecular weights of gases A and B respectively).
questions.question_raw:
According to Graham’s Law, at a given temperature the ratio of the rates of diffusion, {tex}\frac{r_A}{r_B}{/tex} of gases A and B are given by:
(Where P and M are pressures and molecular weights of gases A and B respectively).
questions.explanation: According to Graham’s law of diffusion {tex}r \propto P \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}}{/tex} at constant temperature.

questions.question: At 400 K, the root mean square (rms) speed of a gas X (molar mass 40) is equal to the most probable speed of a gas Y at 60 K. The molar mass of gas Y is:
questions.question_raw: At 400 K, the root mean square (rms) speed of a gas X (molar mass 40) is equal to the most probable speed of a gas Y at 60 K. The molar mass of gas Y is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{rm}} X=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 400}{40}}{/tex} 
{tex}u_{M P} Y=\sqrt{\frac{2 R \times 60}{M}}{/tex}  
{tex}\therefore{/tex} M = 4 g mol-1

questions.question: 380 mL of a gas at 27°C, 800 mm of Hg weighs 0.455 g. The molar mass of gas is:
questions.question_raw: 380 mL of a gas at 27°C, 800 mm of Hg weighs 0.455 g. The molar mass of gas is:
questions.explanation:
PV = {tex}\frac{w}{m} R T{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{w R T}{P V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.455 \times 0.0821 \times 300 \times 760 \times 1000}{800 \times 380}{/tex} 
= 28 g mol-1

questions.question: The density of neon will be highest at:
questions.question_raw: The density of neon will be highest at:

PV = {tex}\frac{w}{m} R T{/tex} or P = {tex}\frac{w}{V \cdot m} R T{/tex} (if {tex}\frac{w}{V}{/tex} = d)
or P = {tex}\frac{d}{m}{/tex}RT 
Thus, d {tex}\propto{/tex} P and {tex}d \propto \frac{1}{T}{/tex}

questions.question: Consider an ideal gas contained in a vessel. If the intermolecular interactions suddenly begins to act, which of the following will happen?

questions.question: A perfect gas of a given mass is heated first in a small vessel and then in a large vessel, such that their volume remains unchanged. The P-T curves are:
questions.question_raw: A perfect gas of a given mass is heated first in a small vessel and then in a large vessel, such that their volume remains unchanged. The P-T curves are:

questions.question: The rms speed of the molecules of a gas of density 4 kg m-3 and pressure 1.2 $\times$ 105 $\times$ Nm-2 is:
questions.question_raw: The rms speed of the molecules of a gas of density 4 kg m-3 and pressure 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 {tex}\times{/tex} Nm-2 is:
questions.explanation: u = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 P}{d}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{5}}{4}}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 ms-1

questions.question: If one mole of a monoatomic gas ($\gamma= \frac{5}{3}$) is mixed with one mole of a diatomic gas ($\gamma= \frac{7}{5}$), the value of $\gamma$ for the mixture is:
questions.question_raw: If one mole of a monoatomic gas ({tex}\gamma= \frac{5}{3}{/tex}) is mixed with one mole of a diatomic gas ({tex}\gamma= \frac{7}{5}{/tex}), the value of {tex}\gamma{/tex} for the mixture is:
questions.explanation:
For monoatomic gas {tex}C_{v}=\frac{3}{2} R T{/tex}; {tex}C_{p}=\frac{5}{2} R T{/tex} 
For diatomic gas {tex}C_{v}=\frac{5}{2} R T{/tex}; {tex}C_{p}=\frac{7}{2} R T{/tex} 
Thus, for mixture of 1 mole each, 
{tex}C_{v}=\frac{\frac{3}{2} R T+\frac{5}{2} R T}{2}{/tex} and {tex}C_{p}=\frac{\frac{5}{2} R T+\frac{7}{2} R T}{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\frac{C_{p}}{ C_{v}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 R T}{2 R T}{/tex} = 1.5

questions.question: A gas is heated from 0°C to 100°C at 1.0 atm pressure. If the initial volume of the gas is 10 litre, its final volume would be:
questions.question_raw: A gas is heated from 0°C to 100°C at 1.0 atm pressure. If the initial volume of the gas is 10 litre, its final volume would be:
questions.explanation:
At constant pressure
{tex}\frac{V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{10}{273}=\frac{V}{373}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 13.66 litre

questions.question: The compressibility factor of a gas is defined as Z = PV/nRT. The compressibility factor of an ideal gas is:

questions.question: The root mean square speed of the molecules of diatomic gas is u. When the temperature is doubled, the molecules dissociates into two atoms. The new rms speed of the atom is:
questions.question_raw: The root mean square speed of the molecules of diatomic gas is u. When the temperature is doubled, the molecules dissociates into two atoms. The new rms speed of the atom is:

u = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}; if T = 2T and M = {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex}, then u1 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 2 T}{M / 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{u_{1}}{u}=\sqrt{4}{/tex} = 2

questions.question: 4.0 g of argon has pressure P and temperature TK in a vessel. On keeping the vessel at 50° higher temperature, 0.8 g of argon was given out to maintain the pressure P. The original temperature was:

On heating for argon: P {tex}\times{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{3.2}{m} \times R \times(T+50){/tex}

questions.question: The rms speed of hydrogen is $\sqrt{7}$ times the rms speed of nitrogen. If T is the temperature of the gas, then:

questions.question:
Positive deviation from ideal behaviour takes place because of:
 molecular interaction between atoms and $\frac{PV}{nRT}$ > 1
 molecular interaction between atoms and $\frac{PV}{nRT}$ < 1
 finite size of atoms and $\frac{PV}{nRT}$ > 1
 finite size of atoms and $\frac{PV}{nRT}$ < 1
questions.question_raw:
Positive deviation from ideal behaviour takes place because of:

  1. molecular interaction between atoms and {tex}\frac{PV}{nRT}{/tex} > 1
  2. molecular interaction between atoms and {tex}\frac{PV}{nRT}{/tex} < 1
  3. finite size of atoms and {tex}\frac{PV}{nRT}{/tex} > 1
  4. finite size of atoms and {tex}\frac{PV}{nRT}{/tex} < 1
questions.explanation: For positive deviation Z > 1, i.e., the condition when repulsive forces predominates over van der Waals’ forces of attraction a. Also at high pressure, volume is small due to small size and b cannot be neglected. However, the term {tex}\frac{a}{V^2}{/tex} is negligible in comparison to high pressure.

At 40oC; KE2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times R \times 313{/tex}
{tex}\frac{K E_{2}}{K E_{1}}=\frac{313}{293}{/tex}

questions.question: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of P to V at any instant is constant and equal to unity. The molar heat capacity of the gas is:
questions.question_raw: A monoatomic ideal gas undergoes a process in which the ratio of P to V at any instant is constant and equal to unity. The molar heat capacity of the gas is:
questions.explanation:
Let P1 V1 be the pressure and volume of monoatomic gas at temperature T.
P1V1 = RT
P2(V1 + DV) = R(T + 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P_{2}^{2}{/tex} = RT + R  ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{P_{2}}{V_{1}+d V}{/tex} = 1)
or {tex}2\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{v}{/tex} = R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{v}=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\because{/tex} C = {tex}C_{v}+\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{v}{/tex} for a process
= {tex}\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{R}{2}=\frac{4 R}{2}{/tex}

a and Cl2 < a for C2H6 but b for Cl2 > b for C2H6

a - Cl2 6.49 L2 bar mol-2, b - 0.05622 L mol-1

questions.question: The compressibility factor for a real gas at high pressure is:
questions.question_raw: The compressibility factor for a real gas at high pressure is:
questions.explanation:
At high pressure, volume is very low 
{tex}\left[P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right]{/tex}[V - b] = RT
Thus van der Waals’ equation reduces to the (term {tex}\frac{a}{V^2}{/tex} can be neglected in comparison of high pressure)
P[V - b] = RT
PV = RT + Pb
or Z = {tex}\frac{P V}{R T}=1+\frac{P b}{R T}{/tex}

questions.question: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by C*, average speed by $\bar C$ and mean square speed by C, then for a large number of molecules the ratios of these speeds are:
questions.question_raw: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by C*, average speed by {tex}\bar C{/tex} and mean square speed by C, then for a large number of molecules the ratios of these speeds are:
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{\mathrm{m} \mathrm{p}} : u_{\mathrm{Av}}: u_{\mathrm{ms}}:{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}}: \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}: \sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex} ::1 : 1.128 : 1.225
Thus {tex}C^{*}: \bar C: C{/tex} = 1:1.128:1.225

questions.question: If Z is a compressibility factor, van der Waals’ equation at low pressure can be written as:
questions.question_raw: If Z is a compressibility factor, van der Waals’ equation at low pressure can be written as:
questions.explanation:
van der Waals’ equations is
{tex}\left[P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right]{/tex}[V - b] = RT
at low pressure, V is large and b can be neglected
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  PV + {tex}\frac{a}{V}{/tex} = RT
or PV = RT - {tex}\frac{a}{V}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{PV}{RT}{/tex} = Z = 1 - {tex}\frac{a}{V R T}{/tex}

questions.question: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of distance between the molecules is:
questions.question_raw: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of distance between the molecules is:
questions.explanation: Dipole-dipole interaction of two identical molecules is inversely proportional to the cube of the distance between molecule. The hydrogen bond H-bonding which is due to dipole-dipole interaction is correct.

questions.question:
Correct gas equation is:
 $\frac{V_{1} T_{2}}{P_{1}}=\frac{V_{2} T_{1}}{P_{2}}$
 $\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{P_{2} V_{2}}=\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}$
 $\frac{P_{1} T_{1}}{V_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}$
 $\frac{V_{1} V_{2}}{T_{1} T_{2}}=P_{1} P_{2}$
questions.question_raw:
Correct gas equation is:

  1. {tex}\frac{V_{1} T_{2}}{P_{1}}=\frac{V_{2} T_{1}}{P_{2}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{P_{2} V_{2}}=\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{P_{1} T_{1}}{V_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{V_{1} V_{2}}{T_{1} T_{2}}=P_{1} P_{2}{/tex}
questions.explanation: PV = nRT

questions.question: In a closed flask of 5 litres, 1.0 g of H2 is heated from 300 to 600 K. Which statement is incorrect?
questions.question_raw: In a closed flask of 5 litres, 1.0 g of H2 is heated from 300 to 600 K. Which statement is incorrect?

questions.question: A gas is said to behave like an ideal gas when the reaction $\frac{PV}{T}$ = constant. When do you expect a real gas to behave like an ideal gas?
questions.question_raw: A gas is said to behave like an ideal gas when the reaction {tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex} = constant. When do you expect a real gas to behave like an ideal gas?

options.text: When the temperature is high and pressure is low
When both the temperature and pressure are high
When both the temperature and pressure are low

questions.explanation:
As we know that the van der Waals' equation is
(p+aV2)(V−b)=RT
The real gases show ideal behaviour when pressure approaches zero and temperature is high. At this condition there is no force of attraction and repulsion between the molecules of gas. Thus, the effect of aV2 and b is negligible, i.e.
pV=RT
pV/RT=1
We also know pV/RT=ZpV/RT=Z (for ideal gas Z = 1)
(Z is compressibility factor)
Therefore a real has behaves like ideal gas when the temperature is high and pressure is low.

questions.question: For one mole of a van der Waals’ gas when b = 0 and T = 300 K, the PV vs. $\frac{1}{V}$ plot is shown below. The value of the van der Waals’ constant a (atm. litre2 mol-2) is:
questions.question_raw: For one mole of a van der Waals’ gas when b = 0 and T = 300 K, the PV vs. {tex}\frac{1}{V}{/tex} plot is shown below. The value of the van der Waals’ constant a (atm. litre2 mol-2) is:
questions.explanation:
van der Waals’ equation for 1 mol of real gas is
{tex}\left[P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right]{/tex}[V - b] = RT
Given that b = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex}(V) = RT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PV = RT - {tex}\frac{a}{V}{/tex} ...(i)
Following y = mx + c for the curve PV vs {tex}\frac{1}{V}{/tex} 
Slope = -a
Slope = {tex}\frac{21.6-20.1}{2-3}{/tex} = -1.5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 1.5

questions.question: One mole of a monoatomic real gas satisfied the equation P(V - b) = RT where b is a constant. The relationship of interatomic potential V(r) and interatomic distance r for the gas is given by:
questions.question_raw: One mole of a monoatomic real gas satisfied the equation P(V - b) = RT where b is a constant. The relationship of interatomic potential V(r) and interatomic distance r for the gas is given by:
questions.explanation:
P(V - b) = RT or PV = RT + Pb i.e. positive deviations or z > 1 (since a = 0)

Only repulsive forces operates among molecules and the repulsive forces contribute only at very close distance. So potential energy increases abruptly. Note that interatomic potential is never negative.

questions.question: Two closed bulbs of equal volume (V) containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulb is then raised to T2. The final pressure pf, is:
questions.question_raw: Two closed bulbs of equal volume (V) containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulb is then raised to T2. The final pressure pf, is:
questions.explanation:
It is clear that moles of gas and volume remains constant during operation.
Initial moles = final moles
{tex}\frac{P_{i} \times V}{R T_{1}}+\frac{P_{i} \times V}{R T_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P_{f} \times V}{R T_{2}}+\frac{P_{f} \times V}{R T_{1}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{P_{i}}{T_{1}}+\frac{P_{i}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{f}}{T_{2}}+\frac{P_{f}}{T_{1}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2 P_{i}}{T_{1}}=P_{f}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}}+\frac{1}{T_{1}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2 P_{i}}{T_{1}}=P_{f}\left(\frac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex} 
Pf = {tex}2 P_{i} \times\left(\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}+T_{2}}\right){/tex}

questions.question: The density of a gas is equal to (P = pressure, V = volume, T = temperature, R = gas constant, n = number of mole and M = molecular weight):

{tex}\frac{w}{V}{/tex} = density = {tex}\frac{PM}{RT}{/tex}

questions.question: Internal energy and pressure of a gas per unit volume are related as:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} If V = 1 litre then P = {tex}\frac{2}{3} E{/tex}

questions.question_raw: Under what conditions will a pure sample of an ideal gas not only exhibit a pressure of 1 atm but also a concentration of 1 mole litre-1? (R = 0.082 litre atm mol-1 deg-1)
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT
or P = {tex}\frac{n}{V} R T{/tex} = CRT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} T
T = {tex}\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{0.0821}{/tex} = 12.18 K

questions.question: 50 mL of hydrogen diffuses out through a small hole of a vessel, in 20 minutes. The time taken by 40 mL of oxygen to diffuse out is:
questions.question_raw: 50 mL of hydrogen diffuses out through a small hole of a vessel, in 20 minutes. The time taken by 40 mL of oxygen to diffuse out is:

{tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\frac{V_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{t_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}} \times \frac{t_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{V_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{M_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{50}{20} \times \frac{t_{O_{2}}}{40}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\frac{4 \times 40 \times 2}{5}{/tex} = 64 minutes

 Gases do not have definite shape and volume

Which is not correct for gases?

  2. Actual volume of gas is equal to volume of container confining the gas
  3. Confined gas exerts uniform pressure on the walls of its container in all directions

questions.question: At what temperature, the rate of effusion of N2 would be 1.625 times than the rate of SO2 at 500°C?
questions.question_raw: At what temperature, the rate of effusion of N2 would be 1.625 times than the rate of SO2 at 500°C?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}}=1.625=\sqrt{\frac{M_{2}}{M_{1}}}{/tex} 
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}u \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}{/tex} and {tex}r \propto u{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}}{/tex} = 1.625 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{T_{\mathrm{N}_{2}} \times M_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}}{T_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}} \times M_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{T \times 64}{773 \times 28}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = 893oC or 620oC

questions.question: At 25°C and 730 mm pressure, 380 mL of dry oxygen was collected. If the temperature is constant, what volume will the oxygen occupy at 760 mm pressure?
questions.question_raw: At 25°C and 730 mm pressure, 380 mL of dry oxygen was collected. If the temperature is constant, what volume will the oxygen occupy at 760 mm pressure?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{730}{760} \times \frac{380}{1000}{/tex} = n {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} 298
{tex}\frac{760}{760} \times V{/tex} = n {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} 298
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 0.365 litre

questions.question: The energy absorbed by each molecule (A2) of a substance is 4.4 $\times$ 10-19J and bond energy per molecule is 4.0 $\times$ 10-19 J. The kinetic energy of the molecule per atom will be:
questions.question_raw: The energy absorbed by each molecule (A2) of a substance is 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J and bond energy per molecule is 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J. The kinetic energy of the molecule per atom will be:
questions.explanation:
A2 {tex}\to {/tex} 2A
{tex}\Delta E{/tex} used for K.E.= Energy absorbed - energy used in dissociation 
= 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 - 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
= 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K.E. atom = {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-20}}{2}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 J

questions.question: The pressure exerted by 6.0 g of methane gas in a 0.03 m3 vessel at 129°C is: (Atomic masses of C = 12.01, H = 1.01 and R = 8.314J K-1 mol-1)
questions.question_raw: The pressure exerted by 6.0 g of methane gas in a 0.03 m3 vessel at 129°C is: (Atomic masses of C = 12.01, H = 1.01 and R = 8.314J K-1 mol-1)
questions.explanation:
PV = {tex}\frac{w}{M} R T{/tex} 
P {tex}\times{/tex} 0.03 = {tex}\frac{6}{16.05} \times 8.314 \times 402{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = 41647.7 Pa = 41648 Pa

questions.question: Two gases A and B having the same volume diffuse through a porous partition in 20 and 10 seconds respectively. The molar mass of A is 49 u. Molar mass of B will be:
questions.question_raw: Two gases A and B having the same volume diffuse through a porous partition in 20 and 10 seconds respectively. The molar mass of A is 49 u. Molar mass of B will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{V_{A}}{t_{A}} \times \frac{t_{B}}{V_{B}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{B}}{M_{A}}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{10}{20}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{B}}{49}}{/tex} 
MB = {tex}\frac{49u}{4}{/tex} = 12.254 u

questions.question: A gaseous mixture was prepared by taking equal mole of CO and N2. If the total pressure of the mixture was found 1 atmosphere, the partial pressure of the nitrogen (N2) in the mixture is:

= {tex}1 \times \frac{1}{1+1}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5 atm

questions.question: By what factor does the average velocity of a gaseous molecule increase when the temperature (in kelvin) is doubled?
questions.question_raw: By what factor does the average velocity of a gaseous molecule increase when the temperature (in kelvin) is doubled?

{tex}u_{\alpha v} \propto \sqrt{T}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{u_{1}}{u_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}u_{2}=\sqrt{2} u_{1}=1.4 u_{1}{/tex}

questions.question: A bubble of air is underwater at temperature 15°C and the pressure 1.5 bar. If the bubble rises to the surface where the temperature is 25°C and the pressure is 1.0 bar, what will happened to the volume of the bubble?
questions.question_raw: A bubble of air is underwater at temperature 15°C and the pressure 1.5 bar. If the bubble rises to the surface where the temperature is 25°C and the pressure is 1.0 bar, what will happened to the volume of the bubble?

Volume will become greater by a factor of 1.6
Volume will become greater by a factor of 1.1

questions.explanation:
PV = RT
1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} V1 = R {tex}\times{/tex} 288
1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} V1 = R {tex}\times{/tex} 298
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{288}{298 \times 1.5}{/tex} 
or V2 = 1.55 V1

questions.question:
For real gases van der Waals’ equation is written as 
$\left(p+\frac{a n^{2}}{V^{2}}\right)$ (V - nb) = nRT
where a and b are van der Waals’ constants. Two sets of gases are:
 O2, CO2, H2 and He
 CH4, O2 and H2
The gases given in set-I in increasing order of and gases given in set-II in decreasing order of a, are arranged below. Select the correct order from the following:
questions.question_raw:
For real gases van der Waals’ equation is written as 
{tex}\left(p+\frac{a n^{2}}{V^{2}}\right){/tex} (V - nb) = nRT
where a and b are van der Waals’ constants. Two sets of gases are:

  1. O2, CO2, H2 and He
  2. CH4, O2 and H2

The gases given in set-I in increasing order of and gases given in set-II in decreasing order of a, are arranged below. Select the correct order from the following:

options.text:
(I) He < H2 < CO2 < O2
(II) CH4 > H2 > O2

(I) H2 < O2 < He < CO2
(II) O2 > CH4 > H2

(I) O2 < He < H2 < CO2
(II) H2 > O2 > CH4

(I) H2 < He < O2 < CO2
(II) CH4 > O2 > H2

questions.explanation:
CO2 is more easily liquefied than O2 gas. Hence (a) of CO2 is more than that of O2. Also CH4 is easily liquefied than H2 and He. Hence a of CH4 is more than H2 and He.

  He H2  O2  CO2  CH4   
a 0.434 0.244 1.36 3.59 2.25 atom l2 mole-2 
b 0.0237 0.0266 0.0318 0.427 0.428 l mole-1  

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of a H2 < He < O2 < CO2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of b CH4 > O2 > H2

questions.question: A mixture of gases contains H2 and O2 gases in the ratio of 1 : 4 (w/w). What is the molar ratio of the two gases in the mixture?
questions.question_raw: A mixture of gases contains H2 and O2 gases in the ratio of 1 : 4 (w/w). What is the molar ratio of the two gases in the mixture?
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\frac{w_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{m_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}} \times \frac{m_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{w_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 \times 32}{2 \times 4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{1}\left[\frac{w_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{w_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\frac{1}{4}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: A gas such as carbon monoxide would be most likely to obey the ideal gas law at:
questions.question_raw: A gas such as carbon monoxide would be most likely to obey the ideal gas law at:
questions.explanation: Real gases show minimum deviation from ideal gas nature at high T and low P.

questions.question: Equal moles of hydrogen and oxygen gases are placed in a container with a pin-hole through which both can escape. What fraction of the oxygen escapes in the time required for one-half of the hydrogen to escape?
questions.question_raw: Equal moles of hydrogen and oxygen gases are placed in a container with a pin-hole through which both can escape. What fraction of the oxygen escapes in the time required for one-half of the hydrogen to escape?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}} \times \frac{P_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{P_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}{/tex} 
(Since equal moles are given so partial pressure is equal)
or {tex}\frac{r_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{r_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} / t}{\frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{2} / t}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{32}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} (t is same)
or {tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} / t}{\frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{2} / t}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{n_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{n_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} 
Fraction of oxygen escaped = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: The root mean square speed of gaseous molecules having density 4 kg m-3 and pressure 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 Nm-2 is:

questions.question: The root mean square speed of diatomic gaseous molecules is u at temperature T. On heating the gas to 2T, the gaseous molecules are completely dissociated into atoms. What is the rms speed of atoms?
questions.question_raw: The root mean square speed of diatomic gaseous molecules is u at temperature T. On heating the gas to 2T, the gaseous molecules are completely dissociated into atoms. What is the rms speed of atoms?
questions.explanation:
{tex}u_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex} 
Case I at T: u = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex} 
Case II at 2T: u1 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 2 T}{M / 2}}{/tex} 
u1 = 2u

questions.question: 128 g N2 is confined in a valve fitted vessel of 25.0 litre at 10°C. How much N2 should be released through valve in order to reduce the pressure inside the vessel to 1.75 atm?
questions.question_raw: 128 g N2 is confined in a valve fitted vessel of 25.0 litre at 10°C. How much N2 should be released through valve in order to reduce the pressure inside the vessel to 1.75 atm?
questions.explanation:
{tex}P_{\text {initial }} \times V=\frac{128}{28} R T{/tex} 
Pi = {tex}\frac{128 \times 0.0821 \times 283}{28 \times 25}{/tex} = 4.25 atm
{tex}P_{\text {final }} \times V=\frac{w}{28} R T{/tex} 
w = {tex}\frac{28 \times P \times V}{R T}{/tex} 
w = {tex}\frac{1.75 \times 25 \times 28}{0.0821 \times 283}{/tex} = 52.72
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of N2 given out = 128 - 52.72 = 75.28 g

questions.question: If molecular diameter of CO2 is 4.60 $\mathop A\limits^ \circ $. The mean free path for CO2 at 27°C on 10”6 torr pressure is:
questions.question_raw: If molecular diameter of CO2 is 4.60 {tex}\mathop A\limits^ \circ {/tex}. The mean free path for CO2 at 27°C on 10”6 torr pressure is:
questions.explanation:
P = {tex}\frac{10^{-6}}{760}{/tex} atm, {tex}\sigma=4.60 \times 10^{-8}{/tex} 
{tex}\because{/tex} PV = nRT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{P V}{R T}{/tex} 
or no. of molecules/cc/ (N*) = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times P}{1000 \times R T}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 10^{-6}}{760 \times 0.0821 \times 300 \times 1000}{/tex} 
= 3.218 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 
Now mean free path l = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} \times N^{*}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \times 3.14 \times\left(4.60 \times 10^{-8}\right)^{2} \times 3.218 \times 10^{10}}{/tex} 
= 3.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 cm

questions.question: 1 mol of H2, O2, N2 and O3 are kept in a vessel volume V at temperature T. A hole was then made in the container. After 2 hour, the order of partial pressure in the container will be:
questions.question_raw: 1 mol of H2, O2, N2 and O3 are kept in a vessel volume V at temperature T. A hole was then made in the container. After 2 hour, the order of partial pressure in the container will be:
questions.explanation:
The diffusion of gas will be in the order
H2 > N2 > O2 > O3 
{tex}r \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}}{/tex} at constant P, T
Thus, {tex}P_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex} will be minimum and {tex}P_{\mathrm{O}_{3}}{/tex} will be maximum. Also {tex}P_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}>P_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex}.

questions.question: The total energy of a monoatomic ideal gas is made up of:
questions.question_raw: The total energy of a monoatomic ideal gas is made up of:
questions.explanation: The energy of an ideal gas is comprised of only kinetic energy.

questions.question: 8 g He is mixed with 4 mole H2. The molar heat capacity of mixture at constant pressure in cal is:
questions.question_raw: 8 g He is mixed with 4 mole H2. The molar heat capacity of mixture at constant pressure in cal is:
questions.explanation:
mole of He (n1) = {tex}\frac{8}{4}{/tex} = 2, Cp of He = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} R
mole of H2(n2) = 4, Cp of H2 = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cp of mixture = {tex}\frac{n_{1} \times C_{p \mathrm{He}}+n_{2} \times C_{p \mathrm{H}_{2}}}{n_{1}+n_{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{2 \times 5 R}{2}+\frac{4 \times 7 R}{2}}{2+4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{19 R}{6}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{19 \times 2}{6}{/tex} cal = 6.33 cal K-1 mol-1

questions.question:
Dimethyl ether placed in a container decomposes to 20% as:
CH3OCH3 (g) $\to$ CH4 (g) + CO (g) + H2 (g)
A pin hole is then developed in container. The rate of diffusion through the pin hole for CH3OCH3 (g) and CH4 (g) are in the ratio:
questions.question_raw:
Dimethyl ether placed in a container decomposes to 20% as:
CH3OCH3 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CH4 (g) + CO (g) + H2 (g)
A pin hole is then developed in container. The rate of diffusion through the pin hole for CH3OCH3 (g) and CH4 (g) are in the ratio:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}OC{H_3}(g)}\limits_{\mathop 1\limits_{0.8} } \to \mathop {C{H_4}(g)}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_{0.2} } + \mathop {CO(g)}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_{0.2} } + \mathop {{H_2}(g)}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_{0.2} } {/tex} 
{tex}r \propto \frac{P}{\sqrt{M}}{/tex} 
P {tex}\propto{/tex} mole
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}r_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \propto \frac{0.8}{\sqrt{46}}{/tex}, {tex}r_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}=\frac{0.2}{\sqrt{16}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{r_{ \mathrm{CH}_{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{OCH}_{3}}}{r_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}{/tex} = {tex}4 \times \sqrt{\frac{16}{46}}{/tex} = 2.36

questions.question: The average momentum in g-cm/sec/molecule of a Maxwell gas of molar mass 44 g mol-1 at 27°C is:
questions.question_raw: The average momentum in g-cm/sec/molecule of a Maxwell gas of molar mass 44 g mol-1 at 27°C is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of one molecule of CO2 = {tex}\frac{44 \times 10^{-3}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{/tex} kg
{tex}u_{A V}=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 300}{3.14 \times 44 \times 10^{-3}}}{/tex} 
= 380.03 m/sec
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Momentum = mu = {tex}\frac{44 \times 10^{-3}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \times 380.03{/tex} 
= 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 kg-m/sec/molecule
= 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 kg-m/sec/molecule
= 2.78 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18  g-cm/sec/molecule

questions.question: At what temperature oxygen molecules have the same average momentum as helium molecule at 27°C?
questions.question_raw: At what temperature oxygen molecules have the same average momentum as helium molecule at 27°C?
questions.explanation:
Average momentum for a molecule = muAV = {tex}m \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{M}{N} \times \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{N} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T \cdot M}{\pi}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{u_{\mathrm{He}}}{u_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=1=\sqrt{\frac{T_{\mathrm{He}} \times M_{\mathrm{He}}}{T_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \times M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}}{/tex} 
or {tex}T_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\frac{T_{\mathrm{He}} \times M_{\mathrm{He}}}{M_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{300 \times 4}{32}{/tex} = 37.5 K
