questions.question: The density of a gas is 1.964 g dm-3 at 273 K and 76 cm Hg. The gas is :
questions.question_raw: The density of a gas is 1.964 g dm-3 at 273 K and 76 cm Hg. The gas is :
questions.explanation:
d = 1.964 g dm-3, P = 76 cm = 1 atm
T = 273 K, M = ?
P = {tex}\frac{d}{M} R T{/tex} 
M = {tex}\frac{d R T}{P}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1.964 \times 0.082 \times 273}{1}{/tex} = 44
The molecular mass of gas is 44 g mol-1 which means the gas is CO2.

questions.question: The normal density of HCl gas at 0°C is 1.6391 g /litre and PV = 5481. If extrapolated values of P0V0 for HCl gas is 5521 and limiting density of hydrogen gas is 0.089922 g/litre, the true atomic mass of chlorine is:
questions.question_raw: The normal density of HCl gas at 0°C is 1.6391 g /litre and PV = 5481. If extrapolated values of P0V0 for HCl gas is 5521 and limiting density of hydrogen gas is 0.089922 g/litre, the true atomic mass of chlorine is:
questions.explanation:
The density of a gas when the ratio of density per unit pressure is extra polated to zero pressure, the point at which a gas exhibit ideal gas behaviour. 
{tex}\rho=\frac{M P}{R T}{/tex} 
Limiting density of HCl = {tex}\frac{\text { Normal density } \times P_{N} V_{N}}{P_{0} V_{0}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1.6391 \times 5481}{5521}{/tex} = 1.627
Molar mass = limiting density {tex}\times{/tex} PV = limiting density {tex}\times{/tex} RT
Molar mass of HCl = 1.627 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 273 = 36.466
Molar mass of H2 = 0.089922 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 273 = 2.015
Atomic mass of H = 1.0075
Atomic mass of Cl = 36.466 - 1.0075 = 35.4585

questions.question: 5.6 litre of an unknown gas at NTP requires 12.5 calories to raise its temperature by 10°C at constant volume. The gas may be:
questions.question_raw: 5.6 litre of an unknown gas at NTP requires 12.5 calories to raise its temperature by 10°C at constant volume. The gas may be:
questions.explanation:
n = {tex}\frac{P V}{R T}=\frac{1 \times 5.6}{0.0821 \times 273}{/tex} = 0.25
qu = 12.5 = mS.{tex}\Delta T{/tex} = m.Cv.{tex}\Delta T{/tex} = 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} Cv {tex}\times{/tex} 10
Cv = 5 calorie
Cp = Cv + R = 5 + 2 = 7 calorie
r = {tex}\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}=\frac{7}{5}{/tex} = 1.4 i.e., gas is diatomic

questions.question:
A mixture of N2 and Ar gases in a cylinder contains 7 g of N2 and 8 g of Ar. If the total pressure of the mixture of the gases in the cylinder is 27 bar, the partial pressure of N2 is:
[Use atomic masses (in g mol-1): N = 14, Ar = 40]
questions.question_raw:
A mixture of N2 and Ar gases in a cylinder contains 7 g of N2 and 8 g of Ar. If the total pressure of the mixture of the gases in the cylinder is 27 bar, the partial pressure of N2 is:
[Use atomic masses (in g mol-1): N = 14, Ar = 40]
questions.explanation:
The partial pressure of N2 = mole fraction {tex}\times{/tex} PTotal of N2
{tex}X_{N_{2}}=\frac{\text { moles of } N_{2}}{\text { total moles }}{/tex}
moles of N2 = {tex}\frac{7}{28}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
moles of Ar = {tex}\frac{8}{40}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
{tex}X_{N_{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)}{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
{tex}P_{N_{2}}=\frac{5}{9} \times{/tex} 27 = 15 bar

If two soap bubbles of different radii are connected by a tube:
 air flows from the bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till the sizes become equal

 there is no flow of air

If two soap bubbles of different radii are connected by a tube:

  1. air flows from the bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till the sizes become equal
  2. air flows from bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till the sizes are interchanged
  3. air flows from the smaller bubble to the bigger
  4. there is no flow of air
questions.explanation:
Pressure inside a bubble = {tex}{P}_{{o}}+\frac{{4 S}}{{R}}{/tex}
When they are connected using a tube then, as smaller bubbles {due to small radius} has higher pressure, the air flows from smaller bubble to bigger bubble until the smaller bubble vanishes completely.

questions.question: A gaseous mixture consists of 16 g of helium and 16 g of oxygen. Then ratio $\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}$ of the mixture is:
questions.question_raw: A gaseous mixture consists of 16 g of helium and 16 g of oxygen. Then ratio {tex}\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}{/tex} of the mixture is:
questions.explanation:
Given,
MHe​ = 4, mhe = 16g
Mox ​= 32, mox = 16g
Specific heat of mixture at constant volume is given by,
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}_{1}}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V} 2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}{/tex}....(1)
For Helium gas,
No. of moles,
{tex}\mathrm{n}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{1 6}}{\mathrm{4}}{/tex} = 4
{tex}\gamma_{1}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
For oxygen gas,
no. of moles, n2 = {tex}\frac{{m}_{\mathrm{ox}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{ox}}}{/tex}
{tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{16}{{3 2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{n}_{2}=\frac{16}{\mathrm{3 2}}=\frac{\mathrm{1}}{\mathrm{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma_{2}=\frac{7}{5}{/tex}
And specific heat constant volume of helium gas,
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V} 1}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\gamma_{1}-\mathrm{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\frac{5}{3}-\mathrm{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{3}}{\mathrm{2}} \mathrm{R}{/tex}
The specific heat of oxygen at constant volume,
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V} 1}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\gamma_{2}-\mathrm{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\frac{7}{5}-\mathrm{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{5}}{\mathrm{2}} \mathrm{R}{/tex}
From equation (1),
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{4 \times \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}}{\frac{4}{1}+\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{2 9}}{\mathrm{1 8}} \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\gamma-\mathrm{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\frac{18}{29}+1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\gamma{/tex} = 1.62

Let us consider, 1.0 L of liquid water is converted into steam. Volume of H2O (l) = 1L, mass = 1000 g

{tex}\because{/tex} Volume of molecules in {tex}\frac{1000}{0.0006}{/tex} cm3 steam = 1000 cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of molecules in 1000 cm3 steam = {tex}\frac{1000}{1000} \times 0.0006 \times 1000{/tex} = 0.60 cm3

questions.question: The average translational kinetic energy of O2(32 g/mol-1) molecules at a particular temperature is 0.048 eV. The translational kinetic energy of N2 (28 g mol-1) at the same temperature in eV is:
questions.question_raw: The average translational kinetic energy of O2(32 g/mol-1) molecules at a particular temperature is 0.048 eV. The translational kinetic energy of N2 (28 g mol-1) at the same temperature in eV is:
questions.explanation:
Given,
{tex}{E}_{{O}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.048eV
We know that,
Translational kinetic energy,
E = {tex}\frac{{3}}{{2}} {K} {T}{/tex}
where, k = Boltzmann constant
T = Temperature
{tex}\therefore{E} \propto {T}{/tex}
Since, the temperature is same for both oxygen and nitrogen therefore, {tex}{E}_{{O}_{2}}={E}_{{N}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}{E}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex} = 0.048eV

or {tex}\frac{{P}_{2}}{{P}}=\frac{{2} {T}}{{T}}{/tex} = P2 = 2P

questions.question: For a real gas when b is negligible and RT= 2$\sqrt {aP}$ and then volume occupied by 1 mole gas is:
questions.question_raw: For a real gas when b is negligible and RT= 2{tex}\sqrt {aP}{/tex} and then volume occupied by 1 mole gas is:
questions.explanation:
The van der Waal equation is
(P + {tex}\frac {{a} }{ {V}_{{m}}^{2}}{/tex})(Vm − b) = RT
At low pressures, V is large and b is negligible in comparison with V
Therefore, the equation reduces to
{tex}\left(\mathrm{P}+ \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}}^{2}\right){/tex}(Vm) = RT
or, {tex}\mathrm{P} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}}{/tex} = RT
{tex}\mathrm{P} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}^{2}{/tex} + a = RTVm
{tex}\mathrm{P} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{2}}{/tex} - RTVm = 0
Vm = {tex}\frac{-(-\mathrm{R} \mathrm{T}) \pm \sqrt{(\mathrm{R T})^{2}-4 \mathrm{a} \mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{2 P}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{R T} \pm \sqrt{4 \mathrm{aP}-4 \mathrm{a} \mathrm{P}}}{2 \mathrm{P}}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: real gas obeys, P(V - nb) = nRT. The value of V is equal to 10 b at certain pressure and temperature. The compressibility factor for the gas under these conditions is :
questions.explanation:
Given equation of gas
P(V - nb) = nRT
The relation compressibility 'z' & vm​ is given by
{tex}{z}=\frac{{v}_{{m}}}{{v}_{{m}}-{b}}-\frac{{a}}{{v}_{{m}} {R} {T}}{/tex}
in the above relation of gas a=0, so second term can be neglected
{tex}{z}=\frac{{v}_{{m}}}{{v}_{{m}}-{b}}{/tex}
given that vm ​= 10b
{tex}z=\frac{10 b}{10 b-b}=\frac{10}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: 4.0 g of argon has pressure P and temperature TK in a vessel. On keeping the vessel at 50°C higher temperature, 0.8 g of argon was given out to maintain the pressure P. The original temperature was:
questions.question_raw: 4.0 g of argon has pressure P and temperature TK in a vessel. On keeping the vessel at 50°C higher temperature, 0.8 g of argon was given out to maintain the pressure P. The original temperature was:
questions.explanation:
According to ideal gas equation, PV = nRT
Here, Initially for argon: {tex}{P} \times {V}=\frac{{4}}{{M}} \times {R} \times {T}{/tex}
On heating for argon: {tex}{P} \times {V}=\frac{{3 . 2}}{{M}} \times {R} \times({T}+{5 0}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4T = 3.2T + 160
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.8T = 160
T = {tex}\frac{160}{0.8}{/tex} = 200K

questions.question: When an ideal diatomic gas is heated at constant pressure the fraction of the heat energy supplied, which increases the internal energy of the gas is:
questions.question_raw: When an ideal diatomic gas is heated at constant pressure the fraction of the heat energy supplied, which increases the internal energy of the gas is:
questions.explanation:
Total heat energy supplied to raise temperature of a diatomic gas at constant pressure = {tex}{n} {C}_{{p}} \Delta {T}{/tex} Total rise in internal energy = {tex}{n} {C}_{{v}} \Delta {T}{/tex}
Thus fraction of heat energy utilized in increasing internal energy = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{T}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{C}_{{v}}}{{C}_{{p}}}=\frac{\frac{{f}}{{2}}}{\frac{{f}}{{2}}+{1}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{f}}{{f}+{2}}{/tex}
where f is the number of degrees of freedom of the gas.
For diatomic gas, f = 5
Thus fraction = {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: The average speed of gas molecules in a gas present in equilibrium mixture is:
questions.explanation:
As we know, the average speed of a gas molecule is given by,
{tex}{u}_{\mathrm{av}}=\sqrt{\frac{{8 R T}}{{\pi M}}}{/tex}
So, for mixture {tex}{u}_{\mathrm{av}} \propto \sqrt{{T}}{/tex}
Hence option {tex}\propto \sqrt{T}{/tex} is correct

questions.question: Which of the following is greater for identical conditions and the same gas?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is greater for identical conditions and the same gas?

options.text: most probable and average speed have the same value

questions.explanation:
The root mean square (rms) speed is always the greatest, followed by the average speed, and the most probable speed is the smallest for the same gas at identical conditions. This is because the rms speed accounts for the squares of the speeds, giving it a higher value than the other two speeds, which are based on different statistical averages.
So, for the same gas under identical conditions:
{tex}v_{\mathrm{rms}}>v_{\mathrm{avg}}>v_{\mathrm{mp}}{/tex}

questions.explanation:
From van der Waal,s equation
{tex}\left[{P}+\frac{\mathbf{a}}{{V}^{2}}\right][{V}-{b}]{/tex} = nRT .....(1)
{tex}\because{/tex} Z = {tex}\frac{{p V}}{{nRT}}{/tex} ; if Z > 1 then PV > nRT
Which is possible from Eq. (1) when {tex}\frac a{V^2}{/tex} is negligible, i.e., P(V − b) = RT
or PV = RT + Pb
or PV > RT
So constant a is negligible.

questions.question: One mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with 3 mole of a diatomic gas. The molar heat capacity at constant volume of mixture (in cal) is:
questions.question_raw: One mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with 3 mole of a diatomic gas. The molar heat capacity at constant volume of mixture (in cal) is:
questions.explanation:
As we know, Cv ​ for monoatomic gas is {tex}\frac {3R}2{/tex} and for diatomic gas is {tex}\frac {5R}2{/tex}.
So for mixture, Cv ​= {tex}\frac{1 \times \frac{3}{2} {R}+\frac{3 \times 5}{2} {R}}{4} {/tex} ={tex} \frac{9}{4} {R}=\frac{9}{4} \times 2{/tex} = 4.5 cal.

questions.explanation:
As we know, RMS speed is
{tex}{U}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex}
given, 
{tex}\frac{\left(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{2}}{\left(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_{\mathrm{t}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}}{/tex}
{tex}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{{T}_{2}}{{T}_{1}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}{T}_{2}={4 T}_{1}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 1200 K
Also, {tex}{Q}_{{v}}={n} \times {C}_{{v}} \times {d} {T}{/tex}

questions.question: Two gases A and B (having molar masses ratio $\frac{M_{A}}{M_{B}}$ = 2) n two separate vessels at same P and T are allowed to diffuse through different orifices, circular orifice for A and square orifice for B. If radius of circular orifice is equal to length of square orifice, the ratio of rates of diffusion $\frac{r_{A}}{r_{B}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: Two gases A and B (having molar masses ratio {tex}\frac{M_{A}}{M_{B}}{/tex} = 2) n two separate vessels at same P and T are allowed to diffuse through different orifices, circular orifice for A and square orifice for B. If radius of circular orifice is equal to length of square orifice, the ratio of rates of diffusion {tex}\frac{r_{A}}{r_{B}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{y}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{y}}}{/tex} where Ax​ and Ay​ are the areas of a cross-section of the orifice.
{tex}\frac{\mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{y}}}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}{\mathrm{l}^{2}}=\pi \sqrt{2}{/tex}
r = radius of a radius orifice
l = length of the square orifice.

questions.question: A vessel of volume 0.2 m3 contains H2 gas (Cv = 5 cal mol-1 K-1) at 300 K and 1 atm P. The heat required to raise the temperature to 400 K is:
questions.question_raw: A vessel of volume 0.2 m3 contains H2 gas (Cv = 5 cal mol-1 K-1) at 300 K and 1 atm P. The heat required to raise the temperature to 400 K is:
questions.explanation:
We know that PV = nRT
{tex}{n}=\frac{{P} {V}}{{R T}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{n}=\frac{100000 \times 0.2}{8.31 \times 300}=\frac{20000}{2493}=8.022{/tex}
Now temperature rise is 100K and number of moles of gas is 8.022
Therefore
Total heat required = {tex}{n} \times {c} \times \Delta {T}{/tex}
= 8.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 4011 = 4 kcal

 KE of the gas becomes zero, but molecular motion does not become zero.
 KE of the molecules becomes zero and the molecular motion also becomes zero.
 KE of the gas decreases but does not become zero.
 None of these
questions.question_raw:
Which is valid at absolute zero?

  1. KE of the gas becomes zero, but molecular motion does not become zero.
  2. KE of the molecules becomes zero and the molecular motion also becomes zero.
  3. KE of the gas decreases but does not become zero.
  4. None of these
questions.explanation:
The translational kinetic energy for one mole of an ideal gas is {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}RT.
So at absolute zero kelvin, it becomes zero.
KE of the molecules becomes zero and the molecular motion also becomes zero.

{tex}{P} {V}=\frac{{w}}{{M}} {R} {T}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}_{1}}{\mathrm{~d}_{2}}=\frac{70 \times \mathrm{M} \times {R} \times {3 0 0}}{{R} \times {2 8 0} \times {7 6} \times \mathrm{M}}{/tex} = 0.987

questions.question: An ideal gas at pressure P0 in a vessel. If the masses of all the molecules are halved and their rms speeds doubled, the resulting pressure P will be:

As we know,
{tex}{U}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{{3 R T}}{{M}}}=\sqrt{\frac{{3 P} {V}}{{M}}}{/tex}
{tex}{U}_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{{3 P}_{0} {V}}{{M}}}{/tex}
{tex}2 {U}_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{3 {P} {V}}{{M} / {2}}}{/tex}
by dividing these equations,
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{{P}_{0}}{2 {P}}}{/tex}
2P = 4P0​
P = 2P0​

questions.question: At sea level air is dense. This is practical application evidence of:
questions.question_raw: At sea level air is dense. This is practical application evidence of:
questions.explanation:
Dalton's law:
The relation between the total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting gases and the pressures of the individual gases was expressed by Dalton in the form of the law of partial pressures. The partial pressure of a gas in a mixture is defined as the pressure at which the gas would exist if it is allowed to occupy the whole volume of the mixture at the same temperature. According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, the total pressure of a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the constituent gases.
Pmoist gas = Pdry gas ​+ Aqueous tension; and P {tex}\propto{/tex} density
At sea level P is high, so d is high
Boyle's law:
Relation between pressure and volume of a gas
PV = constant (n & T constant)
Charles law:
Relation between volume and temperature of a gas. {tex}\frac VT{/tex} = constant (P & n constant).
Avogadro's law:
Relationship between volume and amount of a gas {tex}\frac vn{/tex}= constant (P & T constant).

T = {tex}\frac{{P}}{{R T}}=\frac{1}{1 \times 0.0821}{/tex} = 12K

questions.question: If Xm, Xp and Xv are mole fraction, pressure fraction and volume fraction respectively for a gaseous component in mixture than select the correct relationship between them:
questions.question_raw: If Xm, Xp and Xv are mole fraction, pressure fraction and volume fraction respectively for a gaseous component in mixture than select the correct relationship between them:
questions.explanation:
As we know,
{tex}{P}_{{A}}^{\prime}={P}_{{T}} \cdot {X}_{{A}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}{X}_{{A}}=\frac{{P}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{P}_{{T}}}{/tex}
i.e., Pressure fraction = mole fraction
Also, from Amagat's law of partial volume total volume of a mixture of gas is equal to the sum of partial volume of the constituents gases, i.e.,
{tex}{V}_{{A}}^{\prime}={V}_{{T}} \cdot {X}_{{A}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{V}_{{A}}^{\prime}}{{V}_{{T}}}={X}_{{A}}{/tex}
i.e., the volume fraction is equal to mole fraction.
So, pressure fraction = mole fraction = volume fraction

Vrms​ = Vmp
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{X}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{R} \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Y}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Y}}=\frac{2 \times 60}{3 \times 400} \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{X}}{/tex}
{tex}M_{Y}=\frac{2 \times 60 \times 40}{3 \times 400}{/tex}
MY = 4
Hence the correct answer is 4

questions.question:
Which of the following is true?
 Momentum is conserved in all collisions but kinetic energy is conserved in elastic collision
 Momentum and kinetic energy are conserved in all collision
 Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved in elastic collisions
 None of these
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following is true?

  1. Momentum is conserved in all collisions but kinetic energy is conserved in elastic collision
  2. Momentum and kinetic energy are conserved in all collision
  3. Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved in elastic collisions
  4. None of these
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is not conserved in an inelastic collision as some energy is stored as deformation at the point of collision in the form of potential energy. Since no deformation occurs in the case of the elastic collision so, kinetic energy is conserved. But momentum is conserved in both elastic and inelastic collisions as in both the cases, no external force is applied on them so, no change in momentum.

questions.question_raw: The ratio of pressure of two gases at the same temperature if their densities are in the ratio 2 : 1 and their molecular mass ratio is 1 : 2 is :
questions.explanation:
According to ideal gas equation:
{tex}{P} {V}=\frac{{w}}{{M}} {R} {T}{/tex}
PM = dRT
Given, 
{tex}\frac{{d}_{1}}{{d}_{2}}=\frac{{2}}{{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{M}_{1}}{{M}_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{P}_{1} {M}_{1}}{{P}_{2} {M}_{2}}=\frac{{d}_{1}}{{d}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{P}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}=\frac{2}{{1}} \times \frac{{2}}{{1}} {/tex} = 4:1

questions.explanation: Among given intermolecular forces, ionic interactions are stronger as compared to van der Waal interaction. Thus, the correct order is ion-ion > ion-dipole > dipole-dipole.

questions.question: Assuming ideal gas behaviour, the ratio of density of ammonia to that of hydrogen chloride at same temperature and pressure is: (Atomic wt. of Cl = 35.5u)
questions.question_raw: Assuming ideal gas behaviour, the ratio of density of ammonia to that of hydrogen chloride at same temperature and pressure is: (Atomic wt. of Cl = 35.5u)
questions.explanation:
From ideal gas equation PV = nRT
where n = {tex}\frac mM{/tex}
So, PV = {tex}\frac {mRT}M{/tex}, P = {tex}\frac {mRT}{MV}{/tex}
P = {tex}\frac {dRT}M{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} At constant temperature and pressure, d {tex}\propto{/tex} M
{tex}\therefore \frac {d_1}{d_2} = \frac {M_1}{M_2}{/tex}
(Here d1 and M1 are density and molecular mass of ammonia whereas d2 and M2 are density and molecular mass of hydrogen chloride)
{tex}\frac {d_1}{d_2} =\frac {17}{36.5} : \frac {d_1}{d_2}{/tex} = 0.46

questions.question: At 300 K, the density of a certain gaseous molecule at 2 bar is double to that of dinitrogen (N2) at 4 bar. The molar mass of gaseous molecule is:
questions.question_raw: At 300 K, the density of a certain gaseous molecule at 2 bar is double to that of dinitrogen (N2) at 4 bar. The molar mass of gaseous molecule is:
questions.explanation:
Density {tex}(\rho)=\frac{P M}{R T}{/tex} (1 bar = 0.987 atm)
{tex}\rho_{{N}_{2}}=\frac{4 \times 0.987 \ atm \times 28 \ g / {mol}}{{R} \times 300K}{/tex}
Let the molar mass of gas be x
{tex}\rho_{\text {gas }}=\frac{2 \times 0.987 \text { atm } \times x}{R \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex}
Given {tex}\rho_{\text {gas }}=\rho_{N_{2}} \times 2{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2 \times 0.987 \ atm \times x}{R \times 300 K}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \times 0.987 \ atm \times 28 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}{R \times 300} \times 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 112 g/mol

questions.question: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behaviour?
questions.question_raw: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behaviour?

options.text: At high pressure and high temperature
At low pressure and high temperature
At low pressure and low temperature
At high pressure and low temperature

questions.explanation: At high pressure and low temperature, gaseous atoms or molecules get closer to each other and van der Waal forces operate. So molecules or atoms start attracting each other. Hence a gas deviates the most from its ideal behavior. While in ideal behavior we consider that gases do not attract each other, i.e., there are no intermolecular forces of attraction.

questions.question: Sulphur dioxide and oxygen were allowed to diffuse through a porous partition. 20 dm3 of SO2 diffuses through the porous partition in 60 seconds. The volume of O2 in dm3 which diffuses under a similar condition in 30 seconds will be (atomic mass of sulphur = 32 u):
questions.question_raw: Sulphur dioxide and oxygen were allowed to diffuse through a porous partition. 20 dm3 of SO2 diffuses through the porous partition in 60 seconds. The volume of O2 in dm3 which diffuses under a similar condition in 30 seconds will be (atomic mass of sulphur = 32 u):
questions.explanation:
According to Graham’s Law Diffusion:
{tex}\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \mathrm{d}=\frac{\text { Mol.wt }}{2}\right]{/tex}
Since rate of diffusion = {tex}\frac{\text { Vol. of gas diffused }(V)}{\text { Time taken for diffusion }(t)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{\frac {V_{1}}{ t_{1}}}{\frac {V_{2}}{ t_{2}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\eta}{r_{2}}=\frac{\frac {V_{1}}{ t_{1}}}{\frac {V_{2}}{ t_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}}=\frac{\frac {20}{ 60}}{\frac{V_{2}} {30}}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{64}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{10}{V_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} ; V_{2}=10 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
V2 = 14.1

questions.question: X mL of H2 gas effuses through a hole in a container in 5 seconds. The time taken for the effusion of the same volume of the gas specified below under identical conditions is:
questions.question_raw: X mL of H2 gas effuses through a hole in a container in 5 seconds. The time taken for the effusion of the same volume of the gas specified below under identical conditions is:
questions.explanation:
Under identical conditions, {tex}\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{2}}{M_{1}}}{/tex}
As rate of diffusion is also inversely proportional to time,
we will have, {tex}\frac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{M_{2}}{M_{1}}}{/tex} [M1 = 2 and t1 = 5]

  1. Thus, For He, t2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{4}{2}}{/tex}(5s) = 5{tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} s {tex}\ne{/tex} 10s;
  2. For O2, t2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}{/tex}(5s) = 20s
  3. For CO, t2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{28}{2}}{/tex}(5s) {tex}\ne{/tex} 25s;
  4. For CO2, t2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{44}{2}}{/tex}(5s) {tex}\ne{/tex} 55s

questions.question: Initially, the root mean square (rms) velocity of N2 molecules at a certain temperature is u. If this temperature is doubled and all the nitrogen molecules dissociate into nitrogen atoms, then the rms velocity will be:
questions.question_raw: Initially, the root mean square (rms) velocity of N2 molecules at a certain temperature is u. If this temperature is doubled and all the nitrogen molecules dissociate into nitrogen atoms, then the rms velocity will be:
questions.explanation:
rms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
rms for N2 = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{28}}{/tex}
After dissocitation, {tex}u^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 2 T}{14}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{u}{u^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{u}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}u^\prime{/tex} = 2u

questions.question: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?

options.text: A gas consists of many identical particles which are in continual motion
Collisions of gas particles are perfectly elastic
At high pressure, gas particles are difficult to compress

questions.explanation: At high-pressure real gas particles are easily compressed.

questions.question: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
questions.question_raw: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of O2 = {tex}\frac m{32}{/tex}
Number of moles of N2 = {tex}\frac{4 m}{28}=\frac{m}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio = {tex}\frac{m}{32}: \frac{m}{7}{/tex} = 7 : 32

questions.question: The temperature at which oxygen molecules have the same root mean square speed as helium atoms have at 300 K is: (Atomic masses: He = 4 u, O = 16 u)
questions.question_raw: The temperature at which oxygen molecules have the same root mean square speed as helium atoms have at 300 K is: (Atomic masses: He = 4 u, O = 16 u)
questions.explanation:
Vrms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
Vrms (O2) = Vrms (He)
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T_{{O}_{2}}}{M_{{O}_{2}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T_{{He}}}{M_{{He}}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{T_{{O}_{2}}}{M_{{O}_{2}}}=\frac{T_{{He}}}{M_{{He}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore T_{{O}_{2}}=\frac{300 \times 32}{4}{/tex} = 2400 K

questions.question: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from 5 $\times$ 104 cm/ s to 10 $\times$ 104 cm/s?
questions.question_raw: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cm/ s to 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 cm/s?
questions.explanation:
r.m.s. velocity Vrms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{10 \times 10^{4}}=\frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T2 = 4T1

questions.question: Calculate the root mean square speed of hydrogen in m/s at 27oC?
questions.question_raw: Calculate the root mean square speed of hydrogen in m/s at 27oC?
questions.explanation:
The root mean square (rms) speed of a gas is calculated using the formula:
{tex}v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
Where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin, and M is the molar mass of the gas in kg/mol.
For hydrogen at 27oC (300.15 K), using its molar mass of 2.016 g/mol, the rms speed comes out to be approximately 1927 m/s. This shows how fast hydrogen molecules move at this temperature.

questions.question: Longest mean free path stands for:
questions.question_raw: Longest mean free path stands for:
questions.explanation:
The mean free path, {tex}\lambda=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi a^{2} N}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda \alpha \frac{1}{a^{2}}{/tex}, where {tex}\alpha{/tex} = molecular diameter
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Smaller the molecular diameter, the longer the mean free path. Hence, H2 is the answer.

questions.question: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
questions.question_raw: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
questions.explanation:
According to van der Waals equation for one mole of gas
{tex}\left(P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex}(V - b) = RT
At very high-pressure P >> {tex}\frac{a}{V^{2}}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{a}{V^{2}}{/tex} is negligible.
P(V - b) = RT; PV - Pb = RT
on dividing RT on both sides.
{tex}\therefore Z=1+\frac{P b}{R T}{/tex} compressibility factor.

questions.question: van der Waals equation for a gas is stated as, P = $\frac{n R T}{V-n b}-a\left(\frac{n}{V}\right)^{2}$. This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P = $\frac{n R T}{V}$ when,
questions.question_raw: van der Waals equation for a gas is stated as, P = {tex}\frac{n R T}{V-n b}-a\left(\frac{n}{V}\right)^{2}{/tex}. This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P = {tex}\frac{n R T}{V}{/tex} when,

options.text: both temperature and pressure are very high
temperature is sufficient high and pressure is low
temperature is sufficient low and pressure is high
both temperature and pressure are very low

questions.explanation:
Given P = {tex}\frac{n R T}{V-n b}-a\left(\frac{n}{V}\right)^{2}{/tex}
Which can also be written as
{tex}\left[P+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right]{/tex}(V - nb) = nRT
At low pressure and high temperature, the effect of {tex}\frac{a}{V^{2}}{/tex} and b is negligible hence PV = nRT.

questions.question:
Arrange the van der Waals constant for the gases:
 I. C6H6(g)
 A. 0.217
 II. C6H5$\cdot$CH3(g)
 B. 5.464
 III. Ne(g)
 C. 18.000
 IV. H2O(g)
 D. 24.060
questions.question_raw:
Arrange the van der Waals constant for the gases:

I. C6H6(g) A. 0.217
II. C6H5{tex}\cdot{/tex}CH3(g) B. 5.464
III. Ne(g) C. 18.000
IV. H2O(g) D. 24.060

options.text: I - A, II - D, III - C, IV - B
I - B, II - C, III - A, IV - D
I - C, II - D, III - A, IV - B
I - D, II - A, III - B, IV - C

questions.explanation:
The value of ‘a’ indicates the magnitude of attractive forces between gas molecules.
Value of ‘a’ {tex}\propto{/tex} size of the molecule.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} inert gas will have a minimum value of ‘a’ followed by H2O, C6H6 and C6H5CH3

questions.question:
The qualitative sketches I, II and III given below show the variation of surface tension with molar concentration of three different aqueous solutions of KCl, CH3OH and CH3(CH2)11O$SO^-_{3}$ Na+ at room temperature.
The correct assignment of the sketches is
questions.question_raw:
The qualitative sketches I, II and III given below show the variation of surface tension with molar concentration of three different aqueous solutions of KCl, CH3OH and CH3(CH2)11O{tex}SO^-_{3}{/tex} Na+ at room temperature.

The correct assignment of the sketches is

options.text: I - CH3OH, II - KCl, III - CH3(CH2)11OSO3- Na+
I - KCl, II - CH3OH, III - CH3(CH2)11O $SO_3^-$ Na+
I - KCl, II - CH3(CH2)11O $SO_3^-$ Na+, III - CH3OH
I - CH3(CH2)11O $SO_3^-$ Na+, II - CH3OH, III - KCl

questions.explanation:
I (CH3OH): Surface tension decreases as concentration increases.
II (KCl): Surface tension increases with concentration for ionic salt.
III [CH3(CH2)11 OSO3 Na+]: It is an anionic detergent.
There is a decrease in surface tension before micelle formation, and after CMC (Critical Micelle Concentration) is attained, no change in surface tension.

questions.question:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?
d = Density, P = Pressure, T = Temperature
questions.question_raw:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d = Density, P = Pressure, T = Temperature
questions.explanation:
For ideal gas
PV = nRT
PV = {tex}\frac{m}{M}{/tex}RT {tex}\left(\because n=\frac{m}{M}\right){/tex}
PM = {tex}\frac{m}{M}{/tex}RT; PM = dRT; d = {tex}\left[\frac{P M}{R}\right] \frac{1}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d \propto \frac{1}{T}{/tex}; d {tex}\propto{/tex} P
So, graph between d Vs T is not straight line.

questions.question: One mole of N2O4(g) at 300 K is kept in a closed container under one atmosphere. It is heated to 600 K when 20% by mass of N2O4 (g) decomposes to NO2(g). The resultant pressure is:
questions.question_raw: One mole of N2O4(g) at 300 K is kept in a closed container under one atmosphere. It is heated to 600 K when 20% by mass of N2O4 (g) decomposes to NO2(g). The resultant pressure is:
questions.explanation:
According to ideal gas equation, at two conditions
At 300 K; P0V = n0RT0
1 {tex}\times{/tex} V = 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} 300 ...(i)
At 600 K; P1V = n1RT1
{tex}P_{1} \times V{/tex} = (0.86 + 0.43) {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} 600 ...(ii)
Divide (ii) by (i),
{tex}\frac{P_{1}}{1}=\frac{1.29 \times 600}{1.08 \times 300} ; \quad P_{1}=\frac{1.29 \times 2}{1.08}{/tex} = 2.38 atm. = 2.4 atm

questions.question:
Identify the correct labels of A, B and C in the following graph from the options given below:
Root mean square speed (Vrms); most probable speed (Vmp); average speed (Vav).
questions.question_raw:

Root mean square speed (Vrms); most probable speed (Vmp); average speed (Vav).

A - Vav; B - Vrms; C - Vmp
A - Vmp; B - Vrms; C - Vav
A - Vrms; B - Vmp; C - Vav

questions.explanation:
Vrms > Vaverage > Vmps
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}>\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}>\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}}{/tex}

questions.question: If the distribution of molecular speeds of a gas is as per the figure shown below, then the ratio of the most probable, the average, and the root mean square speeds, respectively, is:
questions.question_raw: If the distribution of molecular speeds of a gas is as per the figure shown below, then the ratio of the most probable, the average, and the root mean square speeds, respectively, is:
questions.explanation: Graph shows the symmetrical distribution of speed therefore, the most probable and the average speed should be the same in the square of speeds. Higher speed will give more contribution, therefore the root means square speed must be greater than the average speed.

questions.question: The critical temperature of the water is higher than that of O2 because the H2O molecule has
questions.question_raw: The critical temperature of the water is higher than that of O2 because the H2O molecule has
questions.explanation: The critical temperature is directly proportional to the intermolecular force of attraction. H2O is a polar molecule, has a greater intermolecular force of attraction than O2, hence higher critical temperature.

questions.question: 320 mL of oxygen gas and 120 mL of hydrogen gas each measured at 25 oC and 1 atm pressure are mixed together in a 720 mL flask. Find the final pressure of the mixture at 25 oC.
questions.question_raw: 320 mL of oxygen gas and 120 mL of hydrogen gas each measured at 25 oC and 1 atm pressure are mixed together in a 720 mL flask. Find the final pressure of the mixture at 25 oC.
questions.explanation:
Partial pressure of oxygen
P1V1 = P2V2
760 {tex}\times{/tex} 320 = P2 {tex}\times{/tex} 720
P2 = {tex}\frac {243200}{720}{/tex} = 3337.77 mm
Partial pressure of hydrogen,
120 {tex}\times{/tex} 760 = P2 {tex}\times{/tex} 720
P2 = 126.66 mm
Final pressure = 337.77 + 126.66
Final pressure = 464.43 mm Hg

questions.question: Which of the following equations does NOT represent Charles’s law for a given mass of gas at constant pressure?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following equations does NOT represent Charles’s law for a given mass of gas at constant pressure?

options.text: log V = log K + log T
log K = log V + log T

questions.explanation:
Charle’s law: V {tex}\propto{/tex} T at constant pressure and mass.
V = KT (K is constant = t(oC) + 273.15]
InV = InK + InT ...(i)
2.303 log V = 2.303 log V + 2.303 log T
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log V = log K + log T
Taking derivative of equation (i)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}(\ln \mathrm{V})}{\mathrm{dT}}=0+\frac{\mathrm{d}(\ln \mathrm{T})}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}(\ln \mathrm{V})}{\mathrm{dT}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a flask contains 20 litre of He at STP, which of the following changes will keep the volume constant?
questions.question_raw: If a flask contains 20 litre of He at STP, which of the following changes will keep the volume constant?
questions.explanation:
P1 = 1 atm, T1 = 273 K, P2 = ?, T2 = ?
According to Gay Lussac’s law,
{tex}\frac{P_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex} = constant
{tex}\frac{1}{273}=\frac{P_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex} = constant
When P2 = 2 atm and T2 = 273 oC (i.e. 546 K), the ratio of {tex}\frac{2}{546}=\frac{1}{273}{/tex} is maintained.

questions.question: Calculate the kinetic energy of 1 g of N2 at 27 °C.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the kinetic energy of 1 g of N2 at 27 °C.
questions.explanation:
K.E. = {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex}nRT = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{1}{28} \times 8.314 \times 300{/tex}
= 1.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 102J

questions.question: A LPG cylinder can hold 14.6 L of propane at 320 Psi and 20 oC. If per day 8.2 L of propane at 1.0 atm used, for how many days will the cylinder suffice if the temperature is considered 20 oC at all time. (1 atm = 14.695 Psi)
questions.question_raw: A LPG cylinder can hold 14.6 L of propane at 320 Psi and 20 oC. If per day 8.2 L of propane at 1.0 atm used, for how many days will the cylinder suffice if the temperature is considered 20 oC at all time. (1 atm = 14.695 Psi)
questions.explanation:
320 Psi = 21.77 atm ({tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 atm = 14.695 Psi)
P1V1 = P2V2
21.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 14.6 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} V2
V2 = 317.84 L
Number of days = {tex}\frac {317.84}{8.2}{/tex} = 38 days

questions.question: Which among the following gases has the lowest root mean square velocity at 25 °C?
questions.question_raw: Which among the following gases has the lowest root mean square velocity at 25 °C?
questions.explanation: Root mean square velocity {tex}\propto{/tex}{tex}\sqrt {\frac {1}{M}}{/tex}

questions.question: To what temperature, should a sample of neon gas be heated in order to double its pressure, if initial volume of the gas is reduced to 25 % at 50 oC?
questions.question_raw: To what temperature, should a sample of neon gas be heated in order to double its pressure, if initial volume of the gas is reduced to 25 % at 50 oC?
questions.explanation:
P1 = P, V1 = V,T1 = 273 + 50 = 323 K
P2 = 2P,V2 = {tex}\frac {25}{100}{/tex}, T2 = ?
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{V}}{323}=\frac{2 \mathrm{P} \times 25 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2} \times 100}{/tex}
T2 = {tex}\frac{323 \times 2 \times 25}{100}{/tex} = 161.5 K

questions.question: Calculate the difference in temperature when root means square velocities of nitrogen and oxygen gases become equal, given the temperature of N2 gas is 100 K.
questions.question_raw: Calculate the difference in temperature when root means square velocities of nitrogen and oxygen gases become equal, given the temperature of N2 gas is 100 K.
questions.explanation:
ur.m.s. = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{u}_{r \cdot \mathrm{ms}\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{r.m.s}\left(\mathrm{o}_{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{R} \times 100}{28}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T}}{32}}{/tex}
T = 114.28
Temperature difference = 114.10 - 100
= 14.10 K {tex}\cong{/tex} 14 K

questions.question: Which of the following forces is predominant between CO molecules?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following forces is predominant between CO molecules?
questions.explanation: Carbon is electropositive, while oxygen is electronegative. Thus, in CO carbon atom acquires a slight positive charge and the oxygen atom acquires a slight negative charge. Hence, in CO dipole-dipole force is predominant.

questions.question: At 100 oC and 1 atm, if the density of liquid water is 1.0 g cm-3 and that of water vapour is 0.0006 g cm-3, then the volume occupied by water molecules in 4 litre of steam at 100 oC is
questions.question_raw: At 100 oC and 1 atm, if the density of liquid water is 1.0 g cm-3 and that of water vapour is 0.0006 g cm-3, then the volume occupied by water molecules in 4 litre of steam at 100 oC is
questions.explanation:
Volume of steam = 4 L = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 cm3
{tex}\because{/tex} m = dV
{tex}\therefore{/tex} mass of steam = density {tex}\times{/tex} volume
= {tex}\frac {0.0006 \ gm}{cm^3}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 cm3 = 2.4 g
Actual volume occupied by H2O molecules is equal to the volume of water of same mass.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Actual volume of H2O molecules in 2.4 g steam
= mass of steam/density of water
= {tex}\frac{2.4 \mathrm{~g}}{1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.4 cm3

questions.question: Dipole-induced dipole interactions are present in which of the following pairs?
questions.question_raw: Dipole-induced dipole interactions are present in which of the following pairs?
questions.explanation:
H2O and alcohol are polar molecules and so dipole-dipole interactions (hydrogen bonding) are present between them.
SiF4, He, Cl2 and CCl4 are nonpolar while HCl is polar. Hence, dipole-induced dipole interactions are present between HCl and He atoms.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding dispersion forces?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding dispersion forces?

options.text: Atoms and non-polar molecules are electrically symmetrical.
Their magnitude depends on the polarizability of the molecules.
It is the force of attraction between two permanent dipoles.
They are weaker than hydrogen bonds.

questions.explanation: Dispersion forces or London forces are forces of attraction between two temporary dipoles.

questions.question: 0.8 mole of each gas H2, SO2, CH4 and He are kept in a closed container. If a hole is made in the container then, after two hours maximum partial pressure in the flask would be exerted by ________.
questions.question_raw: 0.8 mole of each gas H2, SO2, CH4 and He are kept in a closed container. If a hole is made in the container then, after two hours maximum partial pressure in the flask would be exerted by ________.
questions.explanation:
According to Graham’s law, rate of effusion {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1}{M}}{/tex}
Since equal moles of all gases were taken in the container, the rate of effusion will be in the order: H2 > He > CH4 > SO2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The order of partial pressure of the gases remaining in the container will be:
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}>\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}>\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{He}}>\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}

Boiling point increases in the order CH4 < SiH4 < GeH4.

questions.question: The rms velocity of CO molecules at 27 oC is approximately 1000 m/s. For N2 molecules at 400 K, the rms velocity is approximately ________.
questions.question_raw: The rms velocity of CO molecules at 27 oC is approximately 1000 m/s. For N2 molecules at 400 K, the rms velocity is approximately ________.
questions.explanation:
urms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} uCO = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{CO}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Co}}}}{/tex} and {tex}{u}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{u_{\mathrm{CO}}}{u_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{CO}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Co}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1000}{\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{28} \times \frac{28}{400}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{1.73}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\frac{2000}{1.73}{/tex} = 1156

questions.question: A person living in Mahabaleshwar observed that cooking food without using pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude,
questions.question_raw: A person living in Mahabaleshwar observed that cooking food without using pressure cooker takes more time. The reason for this observation is that at high altitude,
questions.explanation: At high altitudes, the pressure is low and hence boiling takes place at a lower temperature due to which food takes more time to cook.

questions.question: The P-V plot for a fixed amount of an ideal gas at a given temperature is a/an ________.
questions.question_raw: The P-V plot for a fixed amount of an ideal gas at a given temperature is a/an ________.
questions.explanation: rectangular hyperbola

questions.question: Volume occupied by a gas at one atmospheric pressure and 0 °C is V mL. Its volume at 546 K will be ________.
questions.question_raw: Volume occupied by a gas at one atmospheric pressure and 0 °C is V mL. Its volume at 546 K will be ________.
questions.explanation: 0 oC is equivalent to 273 K. Since pressure conditions are the same, volume will be 2V mL.

questions.question: The root mean square velocity of CO gas molecules at 27 °C is approximately 516 m/s. The average speed and the most probable speed of CO gas (in m/s) are ________, respectively.
questions.question_raw: The root mean square velocity of CO gas molecules at 27 °C is approximately 516 m/s. The average speed and the most probable speed of CO gas (in m/s) are ________, respectively.
questions.explanation:
If ur.m.s. of molcules of a gas is given, then umpand uavcan be calculated using following formulae:
Most probable speed (ump) = 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} Root mean square speed (ur.m.s.)
Average speed (uav) = 0.9213 {tex}\times{/tex} Root mean square speed (ur.m.s.)
Average speed (uav) = 0.9213 {tex}\times{/tex} ur.m.s
= 0.9213 {tex}\times{/tex} 516
= 475 m/s
Most probable speed (ump)
= 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} ur.m.s.
= 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} 516
= 421 m/s

questions.question: At what temperature in the Celsius scale, volume of a certain mass of an ideal gas at 27 °C will be doubled keeping the pressure constant?
questions.question_raw: At what temperature in the Celsius scale, volume of a certain mass of an ideal gas at 27 °C will be doubled keeping the pressure constant?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac {V_1}{V_2} = \frac {V_1}{V_2}{/tex}
V1 = V, V2 = 2V, T1 = 27 + 273 = 300 K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T2 = {tex}\frac{T_{1} V_{2}}{V_{1}}=\frac{300 \mathrm{~K} \times 2 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{~V}}{/tex} = 600 K
= 600 - 273
= 327 oC

questions.question: A gas at 350 K and 15 bar has molar volume 20 percent smaller than that for an ideal gas under the same conditions. The CORRECT option about the gas and its compressibility factor (Z) is ________.
questions.question_raw: A gas at 350 K and 15 bar has molar volume 20 percent smaller than that for an ideal gas under the same conditions. The CORRECT option about the gas and its compressibility factor (Z) is ________.

options.text: Z > 1 and repulsive forces are dominant
Z > 1 and attractive forces are dominant
Z < 1 and attractive forces are dominant
Z < 1 and repulsive forces are dominant

questions.explanation:
Z = {tex}\frac {(V)_{real}}{(V)_{ideal}}{/tex}
Since volume is lesser than an ideal gas volume, Z < 1. For Z < 1, attractive forces dominant.

questions.question: What is the approximate most probable velocity of oxygen if the kinetic energy of one mole of oxygen is 3741.3 J?
questions.question_raw: What is the approximate most probable velocity of oxygen if the kinetic energy of one mole of oxygen is 3741.3 J?
questions.explanation:
For one mole of the gas, K.E. = {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex}RT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RT = {tex}\frac {2 K.E.}{3}{/tex} ...(i)
Most probable velocity (ump) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Substituting (i) in (ii), we get
ump = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2 K . E .}{3 M}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2 \times 3741.3}{3 \times 32 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{14965.2 \times 10^{4}}{959.95}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{155895 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}} \approx \sqrt{155887 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}}{/tex}

questions.question: Use of hot air balloons in sports and meteorological observations is an application of ________.
questions.question_raw: Use of hot air balloons in sports and meteorological observations is an application of ________.
questions.explanation: Air expands on heating and hence, its density decreases. Thus, hot air is lighter than atmospheric air.

questions.question: A gas such as carbon monoxide would be most likely to obey the ideal gas law at ________.
questions.question_raw: A gas such as carbon monoxide would be most likely to obey the ideal gas law at ________.
questions.explanation:
A gas such as carbon monoxide would be most likely to obey the ideal gas law at high temperature and low pressure.
Under these conditions, the volume V is very large and the terms {tex}\frac {a}{V^2}{/tex} and b can be neglected.
The van der Waal's equation reduces to ideal gas equation PV = nRT

questions.question: Boyle’s law relates the way two gas properties change while the others remain constant. What are the two properties that remain constant in Boyle’s law?
questions.question_raw: Boyle’s law relates the way two gas properties change while the others remain constant. What are the two properties that remain constant in Boyle’s law?
questions.explanation:
According to Boyle’s law
{tex}P \propto \frac{1}{V}{/tex} (at constant n and T)

questions.question: A container contains 18 molecules of oxygen gas moving at a speed of 4 ms-1 and 28 molecules of oxygen gas moving at a speed of 6 ms-1. Find most probable speed of oxygen gas molecule in ms-1.
questions.question_raw: A container contains 18 molecules of oxygen gas moving at a speed of 4 ms-1 and 28 molecules of oxygen gas moving at a speed of 6 ms-1. Find most probable speed of oxygen gas molecule in ms-1.
questions.explanation:
Root mean square speed = ur.m.s. = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\sum \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}}{\sum \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{i}}}}{/tex}
ur.m.s. = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{18 \times 4^{2}+28 \times 6^{2}}{18+28}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{288+1008}{46}}=\sqrt{\frac{1296}{46}}{/tex}
If ur.m.s. of molcules of a gas is given, then ump and uav can be calculated using following formulae:
Most probable speed (ump) = 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} Root mean square speed (ur.m.s.)
Average speed (uav) = 0.9213 {tex}\times{/tex} Root mean square speed (ur.m.s.)
Most probable speed (ump)
= 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} ur.m.s.
= 0.816 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1296}{46}}{/tex} ms-1

questions.question: At fairly high pressures the van der Waals equation becomes
questions.question_raw: At fairly high pressures the van der Waals equation becomes
questions.explanation:
At high pressures, V is so small that ‘b’ cannot be neglected in comparison to V. The factor {tex}\frac {a}{V^2}{/tex} is no doubt large but as P is very high, {tex}\frac {a}{V^2}{/tex} can be neglected in comparison to P. Thus, van der Waals equation reduces to the form:
P(V - b) = RT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PV = RT + Pb

questions.question: For n moles of a real gas, the van der Waals equation of state is given by ________.
questions.question_raw: For n moles of a real gas, the van der Waals equation of state is given by ________.
questions.explanation: {tex}\left(P+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex}(V - nb) = nRT

questions.question: A scuba diver inhales dry air (78% N2, 20.9 % O2 with Ar and CO2) before his dive and the pressure of the lungs is equal to the atmospheric pressue (1 atm) on the surface. At the depth of 99 feet (where atomspheric pressure is considered to be 4 atm), the volume of his lungs is 2.3 L. As he returns to the surface (1 atm), if he does not exhale, what is the new volume of his lungs when he reaches the surface? [Assume that temperature is constant and the role of partial pressures of different gases are not considered.]
questions.question_raw: A scuba diver inhales dry air (78% N2, 20.9 % O2 with Ar and CO2) before his dive and the pressure of the lungs is equal to the atmospheric pressue (1 atm) on the surface. At the depth of 99 feet (where atomspheric pressure is considered to be 4 atm), the volume of his lungs is 2.3 L. As he returns to the surface (1 atm), if he does not exhale, what is the new volume of his lungs when he reaches the surface? [Assume that temperature is constant and the role of partial pressures of different gases are not considered.]
questions.explanation:
At constant temperature, Boyle’s law is applicable.
P1V1 (Under water) = P2V2 (Surface)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4atm {tex}\times{/tex} 2.3 L = 1 atm {tex}\times{/tex} V2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V2 = {tex}\frac{4 \mathrm{~atm} \times 2.3 \mathrm{~L}}{1 \mathrm{~atm}}{/tex} = 9.2 L

questions.question:
While filling air in a balloon volume as well as pressure increases. This does not follow Boyle’s law even at same temperature, because
 mass is increasing
 number of molecules of gas is increasing
 number of moles of gas is decreasing
questions.question_raw:
While filling air in a balloon volume as well as pressure increases. This does not follow Boyle’s law even at same temperature, because

  1. mass is increasing
  2. number of molecules of gas is increasing
  3. number of moles of gas is decreasing
questions.explanation: Boyle’s law is applicable only for a definite amount of gas. As air is filled into the balloon, more gas is introduced. Thus, the number of gas molecules as well as the mass of air inside the balloon increase. Hence, the law is not applicable.

Pressure exerted by 1 mole of methane in a 0.5 litre container at 400 K using van der Waals equation is ________.

questions.question_raw:
Pressure exerted by 1 mole of methane in a 0.5 litre container at 400 K using van der Waals equation is ________.
(given a = 2.253 atm lit2 mol-2, b = 0.0428 lit mol-1)
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(P+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right){/tex} (V - nb) = nRT
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (P + 9.012) (0.4572) = 32.84
P + 9.012 = 71.82
P = 62.80 atm

questions.question: Using van der Waals equation, find the value of the constant which measures the magnitude of attractive forces between molecules in two moles of gas confined in a 3 litre flask exerting a pressure of 13.40 atm at 30 °C. Value of excluded volume is 0.042 L mol-1.
questions.question_raw: Using van der Waals equation, find the value of the constant which measures the magnitude of attractive forces between molecules in two moles of gas confined in a 3 litre flask exerting a pressure of 13.40 atm at 30 °C. Value of excluded volume is 0.042 L mol-1.
questions.explanation:
van der Waals equation
{tex}\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{an}^{2}}{\mathrm{~V}^{2}}\right){/tex}(V - nb) = nRT
a = measure of magnitude of attractive forces
b = Excluded volume = 0.042 L mol-1
a = {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb}}-\mathrm{P}\right) \frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{/tex}
a = {tex}\left(\frac{2 \times 0.0821 \times 303}{3-(2 \times 0.042)}-13.40\right) \frac{3^{2}}{2^{2}}{/tex}
a = 8.24 atm L2 mol-2

questions.question: The number of moles of H2 in 0.896 L of hydrogen gas at STP assuming ideal gas behaviour is ________.

questions.explanation:
At STP, 22.4 L of H2 = 1 mol of H2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.896 L of H2 = {tex}\frac {0.896}{22.4}{/tex} = 0.04 mol of H2

questions.question:
Gases possess characteristic critical temperatures, which depends on the magnitude of intermolecular forces between gas molecules.
 Gases
 CO
 He
 O2
 CO2
 Critical temperature (K)
 132.7
 5.3
 154.3
 304
From the above data what would be the order of liquefaction of these gases? Arrange them in the CORRECT order from the gas liquefying first.
questions.question_raw:
Gases possess characteristic critical temperatures, which depends on the magnitude of intermolecular forces between gas molecules.

Gases CO He O2 CO2
Critical temperature (K) 132.7 5.3 154.3 304

From the above data what would be the order of liquefaction of these gases? Arrange them in the CORRECT order from the gas liquefying first.
questions.explanation:
Critical temperature (Tc) of a gas is the temperature above which the gas cannot be liquified even when high pressure is applied. So, the gas having the highest critical temperature will liquefy first.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The correct order of liquefaction of gases is: CO2, O2, CO, He.

questions.question:
If the pressure exerted by one mole of SO2 occupying a volume of 450 mL is 45 atm at 27 oC, which of the following statements are true?
 Gas shows negative deviation.
 Gas shows positive deviation.
 Gas is more compressible than expected from ideal behaviour.
 Gas is less compressible than expected from ideal behaviour.
questions.question_raw:
If the pressure exerted by one mole of SO2 occupying a volume of 450 mL is 45 atm at 27 oC, which of the following statements are true?

  1. Gas shows negative deviation.
  2. Gas shows positive deviation.
  3. Gas is more compressible than expected from ideal behaviour.
  4. Gas is less compressible than expected from ideal behaviour.
questions.explanation:
Compressibility factor, Z = {tex}\frac {PV}{nRT}{/tex}
Z = {tex}\frac{45 \times 0.45 \mathrm{~L}}{1 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{/tex}
Z = {tex}\frac {20.25}{24.63}{/tex} = 0.822
For ideal gas, Z = 1, as for the given gas Z < 1 it shows negative deviation, i.e., it is more compressible than expected from ideal behaviour.

questions.question:
Find van der Waals constants for NH3, given Pc = 111.32 atm
Tc = 405.4 K
questions.question_raw:
Find van der Waals constants for NH3, given Pc = 111.32 atm
Tc = 405.4 K

options.text:
a = 0.015 lit2 atm mol-2
b = 2.08 lit/mol

a = 0.037 lit2 atm mol-2
b = 4.16 lit/mol

a = 2.08 lit2 atm mol-2
b = 0.015 lit/mol

a = 4.15 lit2 atm mol-2
b = 0.037 lit/mol

questions.explanation:
Pc = {tex}\frac {a}{27 b^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = Pc27b2
Tc = {tex}\frac {8a}{27Rb}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Tc = {tex}\frac{8 . \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{c}} \cdot 27 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}{27 \mathrm{Rb}}{/tex}
b = {tex}\frac {1}{8}{/tex}R{tex}\frac {T_c}{P_c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 \times 0.082 \times 405.4}{8 \times 111.32}{/tex}
b = 0.037 litre/mole
Now, Tc = {tex}\frac {8a}{27Rb}{/tex}
a = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}} 27 \mathrm{Rb}}{8}{/tex} = 4.156 lit2 atm mol-2

questions.question: Which pair represents gases that occupy minimum and maximum volume at STP? Assume 1 g of each gas.
questions.question_raw: Which pair represents gases that occupy minimum and maximum volume at STP? Assume 1 g of each gas.
questions.explanation:
Molar volume of a gas, i.e., volume of 1 mole of gas = 22.4 L at STP
1 mole of O2 = 32 g
32 g of oxygen occupies a volume of 22.4 L at STP
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1g of O2 occupies a volume = {tex}\frac {22.4}{32}{/tex}L = 0.7 L
Similarly,
1g NO2 = {tex}\frac {22.4}{46}{/tex} = 0.48L
1g SO2 = {tex}\frac {22.4}{64}{/tex} = 0.35L
1g C2H6 = {tex}\frac {22.4}{30}{/tex} = 0.74L

questions.question: What would happen if a liquid is heated to its critical temperature?
questions.question_raw: What would happen if a liquid is heated to its critical temperature?
