questions.question: In an experiment during the analysis of a carbon compound, 290 L of H2 was collected at 760 mm H2 pressure and 27 °C temperature. The mass of H2 is nearly ________.
questions.question_raw: In an experiment during the analysis of a carbon compound, 290 L of H2 was collected at 760 mm H2 pressure and 27 °C temperature. The mass of H2 is nearly ________.
questions.explanation:
PV = nRT, n = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{RT}}=\frac{1 \times 290}{0.082 \times 300}{/tex} = 11.8 {tex}\approx{/tex} 12 mol
1 mol of H2 = 2g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 12 mol of H2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 24g

questions.question: 8 g of oxygen and 8 g of hydrogen are mixed and kept at 1 atm pressure and 10 °C. The total volume occupied by the mixture will be nearly ________.
questions.question_raw: 8 g of oxygen and 8 g of hydrogen are mixed and kept at 1 atm pressure and 10 °C. The total volume occupied by the mixture will be nearly ________.
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of O2 = {tex}\frac {8}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{4}{/tex}
Number of moles of H2 = {tex}\frac {8}{2}{/tex} = 4
Total no. of moles = {tex}\frac {1}{4}{/tex} + 4 = 4.25
V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{4.25 \times 0.082 \times 283}{\mathrm{I}}{/tex} = 98.6 L

questions.question: Praveen went to a hill station where he experienced some breathing problems due to low density of oxygen. If at STP density of oxygen is 1.520 g L-1, what is the difference in densities, if the temperature at the hill station is 3 oC and pressure is 705 mm Hg? The hill is at a height of 2173 m
questions.question_raw: Praveen went to a hill station where he experienced some breathing problems due to low density of oxygen. If at STP density of oxygen is 1.520 g L-1, what is the difference in densities, if the temperature at the hill station is 3 oC and pressure is 705 mm Hg? The hill is at a height of 2173 m
questions.explanation:
d = {tex}\frac {PM}{RT}{/tex} OR {tex}\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}=\frac{P_{1}}{T_{1}} \times \frac{T_{2}}{P_{2}}{/tex}
P1 = 760 mm Hg, T1 = 298 K,
P2 = 705 mm Hg, T2 = 276 K
d1 = 1.52 g L-1, d2 = ? d2 - d1 = ?
{tex}\frac{1.52}{\mathrm{~d}_{2}}=\frac{760}{298} \times \frac{276}{705}{/tex}
d2 = {tex}\frac{319336.8}{209760}{/tex} = 1.522
d2 - d1 = 1.522 - 1.52
d2 = d1 = 0.002

questions.question: At same temperature and pressure, the density of gas A is twice that of gas B. If the molecular mass of A is M, the molecular mass of B is ________.
questions.question_raw: At same temperature and pressure, the density of gas A is twice that of gas B. If the molecular mass of A is M, the molecular mass of B is ________.
questions.explanation:
d {tex}\propto{/tex} M {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}_{1}}{\mathrm{~d}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}} ; \frac{2 \mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac {M}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The density of a gas at 27 oC and 1 atm is d. If pressure is kept constant, at what temperature will its density become 1.5 d?
questions.question_raw: The density of a gas at 27 oC and 1 atm is d. If pressure is kept constant, at what temperature will its density become 1.5 d?
questions.explanation:
A constant pressure
V {tex}\propto{/tex} nT {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac {m}{M}{/tex}T
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}} \times \frac{\mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}_{2}}{\mathrm{~d}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{300 \mathrm{~K}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}=\frac{1.5 \mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{~d}}{/tex}
T2 = {tex}\frac {300}{1.5}{/tex} = 200 K

questions.question: Two beakers of the equal volume containing a major component of nail polish remover (A) and ethylene glycol (B), respectively, slip off the table. Which of the following is TRUE?
questions.question_raw: Two beakers of the equal volume containing a major component of nail polish remover (A) and ethylene glycol (B), respectively, slip off the table. Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Rate of flow of A and B are equal.
B will flow faster than A.
A will flow faster than B.
Flow cannot be determined from the given data.

questions.explanation:
Acetone is a major component of nail polish remover.
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{Acetone\;(A)} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {CHO - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_{Ethylene\;glycol\;(B)} {/tex}
Acetone contains a carbonyl group. Hence, it experiences both dipole-dipole as well as London forces of attraction. Ethylene glycol on the other hand contains two OH groups and hence, it experiences strong hydrogen bonding in addition to the dipole-dipole interactions and dispersion forces. Thus ethylene glycol is more viscous than acetone.

questions.question: A chemistry teacher accidentally opens a bottle of laughing gas on the first bench of a classroom containing 40 students placed in 8 equidistant rows. At the same time a mischievous student opens a bottle of tear gas (CCI3NO2) from the last bench. Find the row at which a student will start laughing and crying simultaneously.
questions.question_raw: A chemistry teacher accidentally opens a bottle of laughing gas on the first bench of a classroom containing 40 students placed in 8 equidistant rows. At the same time a mischievous student opens a bottle of tear gas (CCI3NO2) from the last bench. Find the row at which a student will start laughing and crying simultaneously.
questions.explanation:
Nitrous oxide (N2O) is also known as a laughing gas.
Molecular weight of N2O = 44 g/mol
Molecular weight of tear gas (CCl3NO2) = 164.5 g/mol
From Graham’s law, we know that
rate of diffusion {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{ }^{\mathrm{T}}_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{CCl}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{164.5}{44}}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\approx{/tex} 2
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}}=2 \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{CCl}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Laughing gas will spread twice as fast as tear gas. When tear gas diffuses in the last row, laughing gas would have diffused into the first and second row at the same time.
Thus, students of 6th row will start laughing and crying simultaneously.

questions.question: The vapour density of a gas is 16.0. The volume occupied by 16.0 g of this gas at S.T.P. is ________.
questions.question_raw: The vapour density of a gas is 16.0. The volume occupied by 16.0 g of this gas at S.T.P. is ________.
questions.explanation:
Molecular mass = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} vapour density
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.0 = 32.0
Volume of 32.0 g of substance at STP= 22.4 L
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 16.0 g substance at STP = 11.2 L

questions.question: Dipole-dipole interaction among halogen halides are in the order HCl > HBr > HI, but boiling points are found to be in the order HCl < HBr < HI. This order of boiling point can be explained on the basis of ________.
questions.question_raw: Dipole-dipole interaction among halogen halides are in the order HCl > HBr > HI, but boiling points are found to be in the order HCl < HBr < HI. This order of boiling point can be explained on the basis of ________.
questions.explanation: All molecules HCl, HBr, and HI have permanent dipoles. Hence, they have dipole-dipole and London forces. Dipole-dipole interactions are in the order HCl > HBr > HI, but boiling points are found to be in the order HCl < HBr < HI. This shows that London forces dominate. As the number of electrons in the molecules increases, the London force increases.

questions.question: A certain gas behaves ideally at STP. If the density of the gas is 1.4 g/L. What is the most likely identity of this gas? (The gas is diatomic)
questions.question_raw: A certain gas behaves ideally at STP. If the density of the gas is 1.4 g/L. What is the most likely identity of this gas? (The gas is diatomic)
questions.explanation:
For a gas at STP, P = 1 atm, T = 273 K and R = 0.0821 L atm K1 mol-1
Density ({tex}\frac {g}{L}{/tex}) of a gas at STP = {tex}\frac {PM}{RT}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {Molar \ mass}{22.4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molar mass of the gas = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 22.4
= 31.36 g/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The gas is most likely to be O2.

questions.question: Which of the following mixture of gases does NOT obey Dalton’s law of partial pressure?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following mixture of gases does NOT obey Dalton’s law of partial pressure?
questions.explanation: NH3 and HCl can react to form NH4Cl. Hence Dalton’s law of partial pressure cannot be used for NH3 and HCl.

questions.question: If a balloon filled with ideal gas is taken from the surface of the sea to a depth of 224 m, find the volume of the balloon in terms of its original volume.
questions.question_raw: If a balloon filled with ideal gas is taken from the surface of the sea to a depth of 224 m, find the volume of the balloon in terms of its original volume.
questions.explanation:
Pressure exerted by 1 cm of Hg is equal to 13.6 cm pressure exerted by water.
Pressure at the surface = 760 mm of Hg
= 76 cm of Hg = 76 {tex}\times{/tex} 13.6 cm of H2O
= 1033.6 cm = 10.3 m of H2O
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Pressure at 224 m depth = 224 + 10.3 = 234.3
Applying P1V1 = P2V2
10.3 {tex}\times{/tex} V1 = 234.3 {tex}\times{/tex} V2
V2 = 0.044 V1 or 4.4% of V

questions.question: N2 is placed in a 0.5 litre flask under 200 kPa pressure and O2 is placed in a different 2 litre flask under 420 kPa pressure. When the two flasks are connected, the resulting pressure is ________.
questions.question_raw: N2 is placed in a 0.5 litre flask under 200 kPa pressure and O2 is placed in a different 2 litre flask under 420 kPa pressure. When the two flasks are connected, the resulting pressure is ________.
questions.explanation:
Total volume of two flasks = 0.5 + 2 = 2.5 L
If P1 is the pressure of N2 gas in the mixture of N2 and O2
P1 = 200 kPa, P2 = ?, V1 = 0.5 litre, V2 = 2 litre
Applying Boyle’s law, P1V1 = P2V2
200 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = P2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.5; P2 = 40
If P3 is the pressure of O2 gas in the mixture of O2 and N2 then, 420 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = P3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.5; P3 = 336 Hence, Total pressure P = P2 + P3 = 40 + 336 = 376 kPa

questions.question: If relative humidity of air is 42 %, find the weight of water vapour per litre of air at 310 K and 3.4 $\times$ 103 Pa i.e. saturation vapour pressure.
questions.question_raw: If relative humidity of air is 42 %, find the weight of water vapour per litre of air at 310 K and 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 Pa i.e. saturation vapour pressure.
questions.explanation:
PV = {tex}\frac {w}{MT}{/tex}RT
3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m3 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}{18}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 310
{tex}W_{H_2O}{/tex} = 23.74g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Relative humidity is 0.42
Mass of water vapour = 23.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.42 = 9.97 g

questions.question: Which of the following diagrams could represent a gas sample in a balloon at constant temperature before and after an increase in external pressure?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following diagrams could represent a gas sample in a balloon at constant temperature before and after an increase in external pressure?
questions.explanation: According to Boyle’s law, pressure is inversely proportional to volume. As the external pressure increases, the volume occupied by the gas sample in the balloon decreases, and the balloon shrinks.

questions.question: Diagram X represents a gaseous sample with n moles at temperature T, pressure P and volume V, in a cylinder fitted with a movable piston. Which of the following accurately represents the gas sample after the pressure on the piston is doubled (at constant temperature) and the temperature is increased by two-fold (at constant pressure)?
questions.question_raw: Diagram X represents a gaseous sample with n moles at temperature T, pressure P and volume V, in a cylinder fitted with a movable piston. Which of the following accurately represents the gas sample after the pressure on the piston is doubled (at constant temperature) and the temperature is increased by two-fold (at constant pressure)?
questions.explanation:
Doubling the pressure decreases the volume by one-half.
Increasing the temperature by two folds increases the volume by two times, i.e., the system goes back to its initial state.

questions.question: Diagram X represents an equimolar mixture of two gases (neon-shaded spheres and helium-white spheres) at time t. Which of the following best represents the composition in the two chambers after a certain period of time has passed?
questions.question_raw: Diagram X represents an equimolar mixture of two gases (neon-shaded spheres and helium-white spheres) at time t. Which of the following best represents the composition in the two chambers after a certain period of time has passed?
questions.explanation: Since molar mass of He < molar mass of Ne, Helium will effuse faster through the pinhole into the adjacent chamber, and hence due to faster effusion more Helium will effuse into the second chamber.

questions.question:
The diagrams represent four different solvents of identical concentrations in equilibrium with their vapour phases in a closed system. Based on the observation made from these diagrams, arrange the solvents in decreasing order of strength of their intermolecular forces,
top layer = vapour phase
bottom layer = liquid phase
questions.question_raw:
The diagrams represent four different solvents of identical concentrations in equilibrium with their vapour phases in a closed system. Based on the observation made from these diagrams, arrange the solvents in decreasing order of strength of their intermolecular forces,
top layer = vapour phase
bottom layer = liquid phase
questions.explanation: Fewer molecules in the vapour phase indicate a lower vapour pressure of the solvent, i.e., stronger intermolecular forces.

questions.question: The graph below represents the distribution of molecular speed by three different oxides of nitrogen at the same temperature. (Nitrogen = shaded sphere, Oxygen = white sphere) Identity the oxides.
questions.question_raw: The graph below represents the distribution of molecular speed by three different oxides of nitrogen at the same temperature. (Nitrogen = shaded sphere, Oxygen = white sphere) Identity the oxides.
questions.explanation:
= No (molar mass = 30 g/mol)
 = N2O (molar mass = 44 g/mol)
 = NO2 (molar mass = 46 g/mol)
Lighter gas molecules have higher diffusion velocities, lower diffusion times and higher rates of diffusion.

questions.question: Determine the total pressure inside the flasks when three stop cocks are opened. Ignore volume occupied by the side tubes.
questions.question_raw: Determine the total pressure inside the flasks when three stop cocks are opened. Ignore volume occupied by the side tubes.
questions.explanation:
From Boyle’s law
P1V1 = P2V2
Total volume of all the flask = 1 + 0.5 + 2 + 1 = 4.5 L
For flask 1 containing He, the pressure exerted by the gas is given by
P1V1 = P2V2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = {tex}P_{T _{1}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} VT
1.5 = {tex}P_{T _{1}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4.5
{tex}P_{T_{1}}=\frac{1.5}{4.5}{/tex} = 0.33 atm
where {tex}P_{T _{1}}{/tex} = Pressure exerted by He in the total volume.
For flask 2 containing CO2 the pressure exerted by the gas is given by
P1V1 = P2V2
2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}_{2}} \cdot \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}_{2}}=\frac{1.25}{4.5}{/tex} = 0.27 atm
{tex}P_{T _{2}}{/tex} = 0.27 atm
where {tex}P_{T _{2}}{/tex} = Pressure exerted by CO2 in the total volume.
For flask 3 containing Ar the pressure exerted by the gas is given by
P1V1 = P2V2
1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = {tex}P_{T _{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4.5
PAr = {tex}\frac {2.1}{4.5}{/tex} = 0.466 atm
{tex}P_{T _{3}}{/tex} = 0.46 atm
where {tex}P_{T _{3}}{/tex} is the pressure exerted by Ar on the total volume.
For flask 4 containing H2 the pressure exerted by the gas by
P1V1 = P2V2
0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = {tex}P_{T _{4}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4.5
{tex}P_{T _{4}}{/tex} = 0.17 atm
where {tex}P_{T _{4}}{/tex} is the pressure exerted by H2 on the total volume.
By Dalton’s law of partial pressure
PTotal = {tex}P_{T_{1}}+P_{T_{2}}+P_{T_{3}}+P_{T_{4}}{/tex}
= 0.33 + 0.27 + 0.46 + 0.17
= 1.23 atm

questions.question: In the above setup after opening the stop cock, the pressure of the system has a constant value of 620 mm Hg. If the temperature is kept constant at 298 K, find the volume of container A (ignore volume occupied by connecting tube).
questions.question_raw: In the above setup after opening the stop cock, the pressure of the system has a constant value of 620 mm Hg. If the temperature is kept constant at 298 K, find the volume of container A (ignore volume occupied by connecting tube).
questions.explanation:
If volume of A = V
So V1 = V and P1 = 2 atm
After opening stopcock total volume become,
V + 3.5 L
So V2 = (V + 3.5)
P2 = 620 mm Hg = {tex}\frac {620}{760}{/tex}atm
By Boyle's law,
P1V1 = P2V2
V {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = P2 {tex}\times{/tex} (V + 3.5)
2V = {tex}\frac {620}{760}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (V + 3.5)
2V = 0.8158 (V + 3.5)
2V = 0.8158 V + 2.8553
1.1842 V = 2.8553
V = 2.41

questions.question: Based on the following sequence find the molar mass of unknown gas.
questions.question_raw: Based on the following sequence find the molar mass of unknown gas.
questions.explanation:
Mass of water filling the flask = 1234.4 - 155.4= 1079 g
({tex}\therefore{/tex} density of water = 1 g cm-3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of flask = Volume of water filling the flask = 1079 cm3
P = 730 mm, T = 27 + 273 = 300 K,
V = 1079 cm3
Formula,
PV = nRT
{tex}\left(\frac{730}{760} \mathrm{~atm}\right)\left(\frac{1079}{1000} \mathrm{~L}\right){/tex}
= n {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 K
1.036 = 24.63 n
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac {1.036}{24.63}{/tex} = 0.042 mol
Mass of 0.042 mol of gas = (155.4 - 150.4) = 5g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of 1 mol of gas = {tex}\frac {5}{0.042}{/tex} = 119 g

questions.question:
A balloon was filled with one mole of H2 gas according to given diagram. Find the CORRECT statements.
Balloon will burst between 75 to 80 oC
Balloon will burst between 70 - 75 oC
Pressure exerted by H2 gas at 9 oC is 42 atm
Pressure exerted by H2 gas at 9 oC is 23 atm
questions.question_raw:
A balloon was filled with one mole of H2 gas according to given diagram. Find the CORRECT statements.

  1. Balloon will burst between 75 to 80 oC
  2. Balloon will burst between 70 - 75 oC
  3. Pressure exerted by H2 gas at 9 oC is 42 atm
  4. Pressure exerted by H2 gas at 9 oC is 23 atm
questions.explanation:
If maximum capacity is X, then
{tex}\frac {4}{5}{/tex} of X is 547 mL
X = {tex}\frac {5}{4}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 547 = 683.75
Maximum capacity = 683.75 mL
V1 = 547 mL
n = 1 mole
T1 = 282 K
V2 = 68.75 mL
T2 = ? (Temperature at which balloon bursts)
{tex}\frac{V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{V_{2}}{T_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{547}{282}=\frac{683.75}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}{/tex}
T2 = {tex}\frac{282 \times 683.75}{547}{/tex} = 352.5
T2 = 79.5 oC
Pressure created by gas at 9 oC is PV = nRT
P {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {547}{1000}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 282
P = 42.33 atm

questions.question: Radius of bubble X1 is 0.32 cm and X2 is 0.48 cm. What is the temperature of the pond at the position of bubble X2, if pressure at the bottom of the pond is 2.8 atm and at the top is 1 atm?
questions.question_raw: Radius of bubble X1 is 0.32 cm and X2 is 0.48 cm. What is the temperature of the pond at the position of bubble X2, if pressure at the bottom of the pond is 2.8 atm and at the top is 1 atm?
questions.explanation:
Moles of air contained in the given bubble is the same at the bottom of the pond as well as at the surface.
n = {tex}\begin{aligned} &\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{R T_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{R T_{2}}\\ &\text { bottom surface } \end{aligned}{/tex} of the pond
{tex}\frac{2.8 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi(0.32)^{3}}{\mathrm{R} \times 276.5}=\frac{1 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \times(0.48)^{3}}{\mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{0.0917504}{276.5}=\frac{0.110592}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}{/tex}
T2 = 333.28
T2 = 60.28 oC

questions.question: In the above diagram if SO3 and water vapour are introduced simultaneously, at what distance from SO3 end and H2O(Vapour) end respectively would H2SO4 first appear?
questions.question_raw: In the above diagram if SO3 and water vapour are introduced simultaneously, at what distance from SO3 end and H2O(Vapour) end respectively would H2SO4 first appear?
questions.explanation:
Let distance of P from x end be a cm for simultaneous diffusion of SO3 and H2O vapour at same pressure where formation of H2SO4 occurs.
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{so}_3}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{so}_{3}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{2}} \mathrm{O}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{so}_{3}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{300-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{so}_{3}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}{\mathrm{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{18}{80}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac {300-a}{a}{/tex} = 0.47 ({tex}t _{H_2O} = t_{SO_3}{/tex})
a = 204.1

questions.question: Find the partial pressure of methane in the flask shown here.
questions.question_raw: Find the partial pressure of methane in the flask shown here.
questions.explanation:
Number of moles of CH4 = {tex}\frac {2.4}{16}{/tex} = 0.15
Number of moles of O2 = {tex}\frac {0.8}{32}{/tex} = 0.025
Mole fraction of CH4 = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.15}{0.15+0.025}=\frac{0.15}{0.175}{/tex}
Mole fraction of CH4 = 0.857
Partial pressure of CH4
= Mole fraction of CH4 {tex}\times{/tex} Total pressure
= 0.857 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.96
Partial pressure of CH4 = 0.82 atm

questions.question: The graph depicts one of the fundamental laws of chemistry related to the gaseous state of a substance. On differentiating the above law which of the following equation is obtained?
questions.question_raw: The graph depicts one of the fundamental laws of chemistry related to the gaseous state of a substance. On differentiating the above law which of the following equation is obtained?
questions.explanation:
The above graph depicts Boyle’s Law which is given by PV = constant
On differentiating the equation we get
d(PV) = d(C)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VdP + PdV = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} VdP = -PdV
{tex}\frac {dP}{P} = -\frac {dV}{V}{/tex}

questions.question: A one litre flask filled with NH3 is connected through a long tube to another one litre flask filled with dry HCl. If the stop cocks connecting the tubes are opened simultaneously a white ring of NH4CI will be formed ________.
questions.question_raw: A one litre flask filled with NH3 is connected through a long tube to another one litre flask filled with dry HCl. If the stop cocks connecting the tubes are opened simultaneously a white ring of NH4CI will be formed ________.

options.text: at the centre of the tube
throughout the length of the tube

questions.question: Four flasks A, B, C and D contain inert gases in the increasing order of their atomic radii, respectively. The molecules present in which of the following flask has the highest root mean square velocity at 298 K?
questions.question_raw: Four flasks A, B, C and D contain inert gases in the increasing order of their atomic radii, respectively. The molecules present in which of the following flask has the highest root mean square velocity at 298 K?
questions.explanation:
Assume the four gases A, B, C and D to be He, Ne, Ar and Kr, respectively.
Root mean square velocity {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Out of the four gases, he has the lowest molar mass and hence it has the highest root mean square velocity.

questions.question: According to the above set up if 42 mL of nitrogen gas is collected at 20 oC, what is the volume of the gas at STP?
questions.question_raw: According to the above set up if 42 mL of nitrogen gas is collected at 20 oC, what is the volume of the gas at STP?
questions.explanation:
Given Condition
V1 = 42 mL
T1 = 20 + 273 = 293 K
P1 = 725.5 - 16.5
= 709 mm Hg
Final Condition
V2 = ?
T2 = 0 + 273 = 273 K
P2 = 760 mm Hg
(Pi = Pressure of dry gas)
(For applying gas equation, pressure of dry gas is considered instead of moist gas)

V2 = {tex}\frac{709 \times 42 \times 273}{293 \times 760}{/tex} = 36.51 mL

questions.question: Which of the following statement is CORRECT regarding van der Waals constant a and b?

questions.question: Which of the following statements is wrong?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements is wrong?

options.text: Gas C is a real gas and we can find V and if intersection data is given
For gas A, a = 0 and Z will linearly depend on pressure
For gas B, b = 0 and Z will linearly depend on pressure
All van der Waal gases will behave like gas C and give positive slope at high pressure

questions.explanation:
For gas A, a = 0, Z = {tex}1+\frac{\text {Pb}}{\text {RT}}{/tex} implies Z varies linearly with pressure.
For gas B, b = 0, Z = {tex}1-\frac{a}{\text {VRT}}{/tex}. Hence, Z does not vary linearly with pressure.
Given the intersection data for gas C, it is possible to find the values of 'a' and 'b'. All van der Waal gases, like gas C, give positive slope at high pressures.

According to ideal gas equation

PV = nRT {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PV = {tex}\frac{w}{M} R T {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = {tex}\frac{R T}{M} \times d{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P {tex}\propto{/tex} d and P {tex}\propto{/tex} T

questions.question: An evacuated glass vessel weighs 40.0 g when empty, 135.0 g when filled with a liquid of density 0.95 g mL-1 and 40.5 g when filled with an ideal gas at 0.82 atm at 250 K. The molar mass of the gas in g mol-1 is:
questions.question_raw: An evacuated glass vessel weighs 40.0 g when empty, 135.0 g when filled with a liquid of density 0.95 g mL-1 and 40.5 g when filled with an ideal gas at 0.82 atm at 250 K. The molar mass of the gas in g mol-1 is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of liquid = 135 - 40 = 95 g
Volume of liquid = {tex}\frac {\text {mass}}{\text {density}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {95}{0.95}{/tex}mL = 100 mL = 0.1 L
Mass of ideal gas = 40.5 - 40g = 0.5g
PV = nRT
0.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = {tex}\left(\frac{0.5}{M}\right) \times{/tex}0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = 125

questions.question:

 At low pressure, real gases show ideal behaviour.
 At very low temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
 At very large volume, real gases show ideal behaviour.
 At Boyle's temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
questions.question_raw:
Among the following, the incorrect statement is:

  1. At low pressure, real gases show ideal behaviour.
  2. At very low temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
  3. At very large volume, real gases show ideal behaviour.
  4. At Boyle's temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
questions.explanation: The real gases show deviation from ideality at low temperature, high pressure and low volume.

questions.question: The initial volume of a gas cylinder is 750.0 mL. If the pressure of gas inside the cylinder changes from 840.0 mm Hg to 360.0 mm Hg, the final volume the gas will be:
questions.question_raw: The initial volume of a gas cylinder is 750.0 mL. If the pressure of gas inside the cylinder changes from 840.0 mm Hg to 360.0 mm Hg, the final volume the gas will be:
questions.explanation:
According to Boyle’s law
{tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P_2}{P_1} {/tex}; {tex}\frac{750}{V_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{360}{840}{/tex}
V2 = 1750 mL = 1.750 L

questions.question:
Choose the correct option for the total pressure (in atm.) in a mixture of 4 g O2and 2 g H2 confined in a total
volume of one litre at 0o C is [Given R = 0.082 L atm mol-1 K-1, T = 273 K]
questions.question_raw:
Choose the correct option for the total pressure (in atm.) in a mixture of 4 g O2and 2 g H2 confined in a total
volume of one litre at 0o C is [Given R = 0.082 L atm mol-1 K-1, T = 273 K]
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left.\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{O}_2}{\text { Molar mass }}=\frac{4}{32}=\frac{1}{8} \mathrm{~mol} \\ \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2}=\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{H}_2}{\text { Molar mass }}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
ntotal = {tex}n_{O_2}{/tex} + {tex}n_{H_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac18{/tex} + 1 = {tex}\frac 98{/tex} mol
PtV = ntRT - ideal gas equation
T = 273 K
V = 1.0 L
Pt = {tex}\frac{\frac{9}{8} \mathrm{~mol} \times 0.082 \mathrm{~L}-\mathrm{atm} \times 273 \mathrm{~K}}{1 \mathrm{~L} \times \mathrm{K}-\mathrm{mol}}{/tex}
= 25.18 atm.

questions.question: Equal masses of H2, O2 and methane have been taken in a container of volume V at temperature 27o C in identical conditions. The rational of the volume of gases H2 : O2 : CH4 would be:
questions.question_raw: Equal masses of H2, O2 and methane have been taken in a container of volume V at temperature 27o C in identical conditions. The rational of the volume of gases H2 : O2 : CH4 would be:
questions.explanation:
According to Avogadro's hypothesis, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to the number of moles. Thus, volume of each gas in the container is directly proportional to its number of moles in the container now, number of moles are,
H2 = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{H}_2}{\text { Molecular weight }}=\frac{m_{\mathrm{H}_2}}{2}{/tex} 
O2 = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{O}_2}{\text { Molecular weight }}=\frac{m_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{32}{/tex}
CH4 = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{CH}_4}{\text { Molecular weight }}=\frac{m_{\mathrm{CH}_4}}{16}{/tex}
Now, ratio of volume of these gases is directly proportional to the ratio of number of moles of these gases. Hence,
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}_2}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}_2}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CH}_4}=\frac{m_{\mathrm{H}_2}}{2}: \frac{m_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{32}: \frac{m_{\mathrm{CH}_4}}{16}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}: \frac{1}{32}: \frac{1}{16}{/tex} (As, MH2 = MO2 = MCH4)
= 16 : 1 : 2.

questions.question: In Duma's method of estimation of nitrogen 0.35 gm of an organic compound gave 55 ml of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound would be (Aqueous tension at 300 K = 15 mm)
questions.question_raw: In Duma's method of estimation of nitrogen 0.35 gm of an organic compound gave 55 ml of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound would be (Aqueous tension at 300 K = 15 mm)
questions.explanation:
Pressure of nitrogen = 715 mm - 15 mm = 700 mm
{{tex}\because{/tex} Measured pressure - aqueous tension}
Room Temperature = 300 K
Volume of nitrogen = 55 mL
Now, at standard temperature pressure (STP) the volume of nitrogen can be calculated by combined gas equation as follows
{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{\text {given }}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{\text {at STP }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{700 \mathrm{~mm} \times 55 \mathrm{~mL}}{300 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{760 \mathrm{~mm} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{at} S \mathrm{TP}}}{273 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex}
VatSTP = 46.098 mL
Now, 22400 mL of nitrogen = 1 mole
46.098 mL of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{1 \times 46.098}{22400}{/tex} mol
Weight of nitrogen = {tex}\frac{1 \times 46.098}{22400}{/tex} mol {tex}\times{/tex} 28 g/mol
Percent composition of nitrogen in 0.35 g of compound
= {tex}\frac{0.0576}{0.35} \times 100 \%{/tex} = 16.45%.

questions.question: Cyclopropane and oxygen at partial pressure 170 torr and 570 torr are mixed in a gas cylinder. What is the ratio of the number of moles of cyclopropane to the number of moles of oxygen?
questions.question_raw: Cyclopropane and oxygen at partial pressure 170 torr and 570 torr are mixed in a gas cylinder. What is the ratio of the number of moles of cyclopropane to the number of moles of oxygen?
questions.explanation:
Partial pressure of cyclopropane = 170 torr
Partial pressure of oxygen = 570 torr
According to ideal gas equation
PV = nRT
For cyclopropane, 170 torr {tex}\times{/tex} V = nc {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} T
{tex}\frac{170}{n_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}}{/tex} ...(1)
For oxygen,
570 torr {tex}\times{/tex} V = nO {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\times{/tex} T
{tex}\frac{570}{n_{\mathrm{O}}}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}}{/tex} ...(2)
As volume of container, temperature and gas constant are same for both cases. Thus, from eqn (1) and eqn (2)
{tex}\frac{170}{n_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{570}{n_{\mathrm{O}}} \Rightarrow \frac{170}{570}=\frac{n_{\mathrm{C}}}{n_{\mathrm{O}}} \Rightarrow \frac{n_{\mathrm{C}}}{n_{\mathrm{O}}}{/tex} = 0.30

questions.question: 600 cc of a gas at a pressure of 750 mm is compressed to 500 cc. Taking the temperature to remain constant, the increase in pressure is:
questions.question_raw: 600 cc of a gas at a pressure of 750 mm is compressed to 500 cc. Taking the temperature to remain constant, the increase in pressure is:
questions.explanation:
According to Boyle's law,
P1V1 = P2V2
750 mm {tex}\times{/tex} 600 cc = P2 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 cc
P2 = 150 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 900 mm
Thus, increase in pressure = 900 mm - 750 mm = 150 mm.

questions.question: 500 mL of nitrogen at 27o C is cooled to -5o C at the same pressure. The new volume becomes:
questions.question_raw: 500 mL of nitrogen at 27o C is cooled to -5o C at the same pressure. The new volume becomes:
questions.explanation:
V1 = 500 mL, T1 = 27 + 273 = 300 K
V2 = ?, T2 = - 5 + 273 = 268 K
According to Charle's law
{tex}\frac{V_2}{T_1}=\frac{V_2}{T_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{500 \mathrm{~mL}}{300 \mathrm{~K}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_2}{268 \mathrm{~K}}{/tex}
V2 = 446.66 mL

questions.question: 50 mL of each gas A and of gas B takes 150 and 200 s respectively for effusing through a pin hole under the similar conditions. If molecular mass of gas B is 36, the molecular mass of gas A will be:
questions.question_raw: 50 mL of each gas A and of gas B takes 150 and 200 s respectively for effusing through a pin hole under the similar conditions. If molecular mass of gas B is 36, the molecular mass of gas A will be:
questions.explanation:
According to Graham's law of diffusion,
{tex}\frac{r_B}{r_A}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_A}{\mathrm{M}_B}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac {\frac {\text V_\text B}{\text t_\text B}}{\frac {\text V_\text A}{\text t_\text A}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_A}{\mathrm{M}_B}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac {\frac {50 \text {ml}}{200 \text s}}{\frac {50 \text {ml}}{150 \text s}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}{36}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{3}{4}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_A}{36}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} MA = {tex}\frac{9}{16}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 36 20.25

questions.question: At which one of the following temperature pressure conditions, the deviation of a gas from ideal behaviour is expected to be minimum?
questions.question_raw: At which one of the following temperature pressure conditions, the deviation of a gas from ideal behaviour is expected to be minimum?
questions.explanation: Deviation of a gas from ideal behaviour is less at high temperature and low pressure. Thus, the highest temperature and lowest pressure will give minimum deviation from ideal behaviour which is 550 K and 1 atm among the given temperature, pressure conditions.

questions.question: The root mean square speeds at STP for the gases H2, N2, O2 and HBr are in the order:
questions.question_raw: The root mean square speeds at STP for the gases H2, N2, O2 and HBr are in the order:
questions.explanation:
Root mean square speed,
urms = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}{/tex}
MH2 = 2 g mol-1, MN2 = 14 g mol-1,
{tex}\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_2}{/tex} = 32 g mol-1, MHBr = 36.5 g mol-1
As, urms {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}{/tex} thus, lower the M value, higher is the urms.
So, urms of of these gases are in the following order HBr < O2 < N2 < H2

questions.question: A 10.0L flask contains 64 g of oxygen at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Assume $\mathrm{O}_2$ gas is behaving ideally). The pressure inside the flask in bar is (Given $\mathbf{R}=0.0831 \mathrm{~L}$ bar $\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
questions.question_raw: A 10.0L flask contains 64 g of oxygen at {tex}27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}. (Assume {tex}\mathrm{O}_2{/tex} gas is behaving ideally). The pressure inside the flask in bar is (Given {tex}\mathbf{R}=0.0831 \mathrm{~L}{/tex} bar {tex}\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
We know for ideal gas
{tex} P V=n R T {/tex}
{tex} P=n \frac{R T}{V} {/tex}
{tex} P=\frac{64}{32} \times \frac{0.0831 \times 300}{10} {/tex}
{tex} P=4.9 \text { bar } {/tex}
Pressure of {tex}O_2{/tex} gas inside the flask {tex}=4.9{/tex} bar

questions.question: Which one is not correct mathematical equation for Dalton's Law of partial pressure? Here p = total pressure of gaseous mixture
questions.question_raw: Which one is not correct mathematical equation for Dalton's Law of partial pressure? Here p = total pressure of gaseous mixture

options.text:
$p_i=\chi_i p_i{ }^0, \chi_i=$ mole fraction of $i^{\text {th }}$ gas in gaseous mixture
$p_i^0=$ pressure of $i^{\text {th }}$ gas in pure state

$p_i=\chi_i p$, where $p_i=$ partial pressure of ${ }^{t h}$ gas
$\chi_{\mathrm{i}}=$ mole fraction of ${ }^{\text {th }}$ gas in gaseous mixture

questions.explanation:
Dalton's law of partial pressure states that the total pressure by the mixture of non-reactive gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of individual gases.
{tex}p_{\text {Total }}=p_1+p_2+p_3{/tex}
Also, {tex}p_i=\gamma p{/tex}; where {tex}p_i{/tex} and {tex}\chi_i{/tex} are the partial pressure and mole fraction of {tex}i{ }^{\text {wi }}{/tex} gas respectively and {tex}p{/tex} is the total pressure.
{tex}p_{\text {Total }}=p_1+p_2+p_3 {/tex}
{tex} =n_1 \frac{R T}{V}+n_2 \frac{R T}{V}+n_3 \frac{R T}{V} {/tex}
{tex} =\left(n_1+n_2+n_3\right) \frac{R T}{V} {/tex}
